Google 


This  is  a  digital  copy  of  a  book  that  was  preserved  for  generations  on  library  shelves  before  it  was  carefully  scanned  by  Google  as  part  of  a  project 

to  make  the  world's  books  discoverable  online. 

It  has  survived  long  enough  for  the  copyright  to  expire  and  the  book  to  enter  the  public  domain.  A  public  domain  book  is  one  that  was  never  subject 

to  copyright  or  whose  legal  copyright  term  has  expired.  Whether  a  book  is  in  the  public  domain  may  vary  country  to  country.  Public  domain  books 

are  our  gateways  to  the  past,  representing  a  wealth  of  history,  culture  and  knowledge  that's  often  difficult  to  discover. 

Marks,  notations  and  other  maiginalia  present  in  the  original  volume  will  appear  in  this  file  -  a  reminder  of  this  book's  long  journey  from  the 

publisher  to  a  library  and  finally  to  you. 

Usage  guidelines 

Google  is  proud  to  partner  with  libraries  to  digitize  public  domain  materials  and  make  them  widely  accessible.  Public  domain  books  belong  to  the 
public  and  we  are  merely  their  custodians.  Nevertheless,  this  work  is  expensive,  so  in  order  to  keep  providing  tliis  resource,  we  liave  taken  steps  to 
prevent  abuse  by  commercial  parties,  including  placing  technical  restrictions  on  automated  querying. 
We  also  ask  that  you: 

+  Make  non-commercial  use  of  the  files  We  designed  Google  Book  Search  for  use  by  individuals,  and  we  request  that  you  use  these  files  for 
personal,  non-commercial  purposes. 

+  Refrain  fivm  automated  querying  Do  not  send  automated  queries  of  any  sort  to  Google's  system:  If  you  are  conducting  research  on  machine 
translation,  optical  character  recognition  or  other  areas  where  access  to  a  large  amount  of  text  is  helpful,  please  contact  us.  We  encourage  the 
use  of  public  domain  materials  for  these  purposes  and  may  be  able  to  help. 

+  Maintain  attributionTht  GoogXt  "watermark"  you  see  on  each  file  is  essential  for  in  forming  people  about  this  project  and  helping  them  find 
additional  materials  through  Google  Book  Search.  Please  do  not  remove  it. 

+  Keep  it  legal  Whatever  your  use,  remember  that  you  are  responsible  for  ensuring  that  what  you  are  doing  is  legal.  Do  not  assume  that  just 
because  we  believe  a  book  is  in  the  public  domain  for  users  in  the  United  States,  that  the  work  is  also  in  the  public  domain  for  users  in  other 
countries.  Whether  a  book  is  still  in  copyright  varies  from  country  to  country,  and  we  can't  offer  guidance  on  whether  any  specific  use  of 
any  specific  book  is  allowed.  Please  do  not  assume  that  a  book's  appearance  in  Google  Book  Search  means  it  can  be  used  in  any  manner 
anywhere  in  the  world.  Copyright  infringement  liabili^  can  be  quite  severe. 

About  Google  Book  Search 

Google's  mission  is  to  organize  the  world's  information  and  to  make  it  universally  accessible  and  useful.   Google  Book  Search  helps  readers 
discover  the  world's  books  while  helping  authors  and  publishers  reach  new  audiences.  You  can  search  through  the  full  text  of  this  book  on  the  web

PREFACE 


Very  few  words  are  needed  by  way  of  preface  to  the 
present  volume  of  The  Literary  Year-Book  before  passing 
in  rapid  review  the  chief  events  of  the  year  now  drawing 
to  a  close.  Thanks  are  again  due  to  those  who  have  helped 
the  editor  in  checking  and  correcting  his  information. 
The  difficulty  of  compiling  the  Annual  would  be  consider- 
ably lightened  if  authors  and  others  who  are  circularized 
during  the  autumn  would  make  a  point  of  returning  their 
replies.  It  is  hoped  that  many  inaccuracies  have  been 
removed,  and  that  the  new  information  included  is  accurate 
and  up-to-date.  Except  in  rare  instances  it  has  not  been 
corrected  later  than  the  middle  of  November,  the  date  of 
publication  having  been  fixed  for  December  ii,  1906. 

The  Literary  Year-Book  having  been  adopted  in  October 
as  the  organ  for  the  publication  of  the  Returns  of 
Public  Libraries,  with  the  assistance  of  the  Council  of 
the  Library  Association,  it  became  necessary  during  that 
month  to  send  out  over  one  thousand  revised  circulars  to 
Librarians,  involving  a  complete  recast  and  reissue  of  that 
section  of  the  book.  In  its  present  form  it  will  be  of  con- 
siderably more  value  than  in  the  past,  and  the  Introduction 
— ^rewritten  by  a  Public  Librarian — ^will  be  found  to  deal 
in  an  effective  manner  with  all  topics  of  interest  to  this 
important  branch  of  education  and  culture  in  the  country. 
Further  particulars  as  to  details  of  arrangement,  etc.,  will 
be  found  in  the  Prefatory  Note  to  the  section. 

The  rapid  development  in  the  establishment  of  retail 
shops  by  Messrs.  W.  H.  Smith  and  Son  and  Messrs.  Boots 
has  very  much  enlarged  the  lists  of  Town  and  Country 
Booksellers,  and    the    usual    changes  necessitated  by 

I — 2 


157419 


4  PREFACE 

twelve  montlis  of  change,  and  growth  have  been  made 
in  the  Directories  of  Authors,  Publishers,  Periodicals, 
and  Societies. 

The  Index  to  Current  Literature,  which  was  a  feature  of 
the  issues  of  1905  and  1906,  has  now  been  omitted,  and 
its  place  this  year  is  taken  by  a  special  Bibliography, 
devoted,  as  is  appropriate,  to  the  works  of  the  greatest 
living  master  who  has  survived  the  Victorian  Age.  The 
Bibliography  of  the  Works  in  Prose  and  Verse  of 
George  Meredith  has  been  compiled  by  Mr.  A.  J.  K. 
Esdaiile,  and  will,  it  is  hoped,  be  welcome  to  the  large  and 
growing  number  of  the  admirers  of  Mr.  Meredith^s  writings, 
which  have  been  so  effectively  recommended  this  year  by 
Mr.  G.  M.  Trevelyan's  book  on  The  Poetry  and  Philosophy 
of  George  Meredith. 

The  editor  takes  this  opportunity  of  renewing  his  thanks 
to  Mr.  C.  T.  Jacobi  and  Mr.  G.  Herbert  Thring  for  their 
assistance  in  connection  with  the  typographical  section 
and  with  the  information  as  to  the  Nobel  Prizes  ;  and  he 
trusts,  in  conclusion,  that  this  Annual  will  be  found  to 
maintain  its  reputation  as  a  serviceable  Year-Book  for  the 
use  of  members  of  all  branches  of  the  literary  world. 


1906 

The  advent  of  a  Liberal  Administration  to  power  at  tlie 
close  of  last  year  placed  several  distinguished  men  of  letters 
in  the  Cabinet  and  the  Government.  These  include 
Mr.  John  Morley,  Mr.  James  Bryce,  Lord  Fitzmaurice, 
Mr.  Augustine  Birrell,  Mr.  Haldane,  and,  among  authors 
of  books,  Mr.  Winston  Churchill,  Mr.  Sydney  Buxton, 
and  Mr.  Herbert  Samuel.  The  General  Election  of  the 
following  January,  and  subsequent  by-elections,  restored 
or  introduced  to  Parliament  the  following  members, 
among  others,  who  are  honourably  associated  with  one  or 
another  form  of  letters  :  Sir  William  Anson,  Lord  Balcarres, 
Mr.  Balfour,  Mr.  Hilaire  Belloc,  Dr.  S.  H.  Butcher,  Sir 
Henry  Craik,  Sir  Charles  Dilke,  Mr.  Stephen  Gwynn,  Mr. 


PREFACE  5 

R.  C.  Lehmann,  Dr.  Macnamara,  Sir  Philip  Magnus,  Mr. 
A.  E.  W.  Mason,  Mr.  T.  P.  O'Connor,  Sir  Gilbert  Parker, 
Mr.  Herbert  Paul,  Mr.  J.  M.  Robertson,  Mr.  Arthur  Sherwell, 
Mr.  J.  A.  Simon,  Mr.  Yoxall,  and  Mr.  George  Wyndham. 
Among  the  honours  conferred  by  Mr.  Balfour  before  his 
resignation  were  a  peerage  to  Lord  Northcliffe  (Sir  Alfred 
Harmsworth),  and  a  knighthood  to  Sir  Clement  Kinloch- 
Cooke.  The  Prime  Minister  has  since  conferred  the  honour 
of  knighthood  upon  Sir  Francis  Carruthers  Gould  and  Sir 
Henry  Norman. 

The  Obituary  of  the  year  includes  several  notable 
names,  four  of  which  go  back  to  the  last  month  of  1905. 
Sir  Richard  Jebb,  Regius  Professor  of  Greek  in  the 
University  of  Cambridge,  and  Member  of  Parliament  for 
his  University,  editor  of  Sophocles,  and  author  of,  among 
other  works.  Characters  of  Theophrastus,  Introduction  to 
Homer,  Attic  Orators,  and  Bentley  (English  Men  of  Letters), 
died  on  December  9.  Henry  Harland,  sometime  editor 
of  the  Yellow  Book,  and  better  known  as  the  author  of 
Grey  Roses,  The  Cardinal's  Snuff-Box,  and  My  Friend 
Prospero,  died  on  December  20.  William  Henry  Wilkins, 
author  of  The  Love  of  an  Uncrowned  Queen,  A  Queen  of 
Tears,  Mrs,  Fitzherbert  and  George  IV.,  etc.,  died  on 
December  22,  and  William  Sharp's  death  on  December  12 
robbed  English  literature  in  one  day  of  two  notable 
personalities — his  own  and  that  of  Fiona  Macleod,  whose 
posthumous  book.  Where  the  Forest  Murmurs,  was  published 
in  November,  1906.  In  January,  1906,  died  Herman 
Charles  Merivale,  H.  Sutherland  Edwards,  Sir  Mountstuart 
Grant  Duff,  and  two  men  of  more  enduring  fame,  George 
Jacob  Holyoake,  in  his  ninetieth  year,  and  Charles  John 
Cornish,  in  his  forty-eighth.  It  is  satisfactory  to  learn 
that  Mr.  Cornish's  posthumous  book.  Carpenter  Birds, 
which  Messrs.  Longmans  are  shortly  to  publish,  will  be 
accompanied  by  a  short  memoir  of  this  capable  and 
lovable  writer,  whose  widow,  herself  a  regular  contributor 
to  some  of  the  papers  in  which  he  was  interested,  is  now 
engaged  in  editing  his  literary  remains.  Mrs.  Wilfrid 
Chesson  (Nora  Hopper),  a  Celtic  poetess  of  rare  talent, 
died  in  April,  and  the  mourning  at  her  early  death  took 


6  PREFACE 

a  practical  shape  which  did  credit  to  the  friends  of  good 
literature.  In  the  following  month  Scandinavian  drama 
lost  the  massive  personality  of  Henrik  Ibsen,  and  English 
art  and  letters  in  the  spring  had  likewise  to  mourn  Sir 
Wyke  Bayliss,  Mrs.  Ballin,  Mrs.  Brightwen,  Dr.  Richard 
Gamett,  and  Dr.  Perowne.  Michael  Davitt,  whose  Leaves 
from  a  Prison  Diary  is  perhaps  the  best  remembered, 
although  not  the  best  of  his  books,  died  in  July,  and  the 
summer  holidays  were  saddened  by  the  sudden  death  in 
August  of  Mrs.  Craigie  (John  Oliver  Hobbes),  just  before 
the  appearance  of  her  last  book.  The  Dream  and  the 
Business,  Mr.  H.  D.  Lowry  died  in  October,  and  in  the 
same  month  there  died  Mrs.  L.  Studdiford  McChesney, 
whose  poem.  The  Nun  before  the  Christ-Child,  appeared  in 
the  November  (1906)  number  of  The  Monthly  Review, 

Trade  changes  this  year,  apart  from  the  activity  above- 
mentioned  of  Messrs.  Smiths  and  Messrs.  Boots  as  retail 
booksellers,  have  not  been  extensive.  Publishers  have 
been  recruited  by  Messrs.  Sisley,  Messrs.  E.  Grant  Richards, 
and  the  Globe  Press.  The  Reader  (weekly)  has  been 
started  by  the  proprietors  of  The  Daily  Chronicle,  The 
Tribune  (daily)  appeared  almost  simultaneously  with  the 
new  Parliament.  Mr.  Hilaire  Belloc  has  joined  the  literary 
staff  of  The  Morning  Post,  and  Science  Progress  has  been 
started  by  Mr.  Murray  as  a  new  quarterly  review. 

It  had  been  hoped  to  include  in  this  year's  Annual  a 
complete  classified  list  of  the  various  series  of  reprints, 
bound  in  cloth,  and  sold  at  retail  prices  from  tenpence 
upwards,  which  have  been  so  marked  a  feature  of  publishers' 
activities  during  the  past  few  years.  It  has  proved  im- 
possible, however,  to  prepare  this  catalogue  effectively  in 
time  for  the  present  issue,  and  it  must  again  be  postponed. 
To  the  tentative  list  suggested  last  year  there  is  now  to  be 
added  the  remarkable  series  started  by  Messrs.  Dent  under 
the  title  of  '  Everyman's  Library.'  Vols.  IX  and  IV  of 
The  Cambridge  Modern  History  have  punctually  appeared, 
and  announcement  has  been  made  of  a  similar  publication 
in  fourteen  volumes  by  the  Cambridge  University  Press 
dealing   with    the   History   of   English    Literature.    Mr. 


PREFACE  7 

Herbert  Paul  has  completed  his  History  of  Modern  England 
(Macmillan),  and  among  the  more  important  books  of  the 
year  may  be  mentioned  Personal  and  Literary  Letters  of 
Robert,  First  Earl  of  Lytton,  edited  by  his  daughter,  Lady 
Betty  Balfour;  the  new  edition  (2  vols.,  ids.  net.)  of 
Mr,  Morley's  Life  of  Gladstone,  The  Memoirs  of  Prince 
Hohenlohe  in  2  vols.,  translated  from  the  German;  The 
Life  and  Letters  of  Leslie  Stephen,  by  Frederic  W.  Maitland ; 
British  Malaya,  by  Sir  Frank  Swettenham,  K.C.M.G. ; 
Mr.  Rudyard  Kipling's  Puck  of  Pook's  Hill;  and,  among; 
novels,  though  selection  is  invidious,  Joseph  Vance,  by 
William  De  Morgan.  It  is  satisfactory  to  be  able  to  add 
that  the  tendency  in  fiction  seems  to  be  growing  more 
wholesome  ;  its  output  is,  perhaps,  not  quite  so  big. 

At  the  close  of  our  preface  last  year  we  noted  the 
foundation  of  The  Times  Book  Club  in  New  Bond  Street, 
and  stated  that  '*  its  success  depends  on  the  ability  of 
The  Times  (already,  apparently,  overestimated)  to  cope 
with  the  immense  demand  which  its  offer  has  invited,"  and 
we  added  that  '*  its  influence,  prejudicial  or  otherwise,  on 
the  retail  bookseller  is,  at  present,  entirely  a  matter  of 
conjecture.*'  The  events  of  the  present  autumn  have  fully 
justified  both  expressions  of  opinion.  The  Times  has 
proved  unable  to  cope  with  the  demands  which  the  tone 
and  the  promises  of  its  earlier  advertisements  invited  the 
public  to  make.  Its  first  catalogue  was  speedily  withdrawn, 
and  smaller  lists  of  books  were  substituted,  which  differed 
very  little,  if  at  all,  from  the  lists  of  other  circulating 
libraries.  More  recently — the  cause  is  immaterial — The 
Times  Book  Club,  which  removed  its  premises  from  New 
Bond  Street  to  a  huge  house  on  the  north  side  of  Oxford 
Street,  has  asked  its  subscribers  to  refrain  from  requisition- 
ing certain  definite  books,  and  the  publications  of  certain 
definite  houses,  which  it  was  unwilling  to  supply.  And, 
in  the  second  particular,  our  conjecture  that  the  influence 
of  this  Book  Club  on  the  retail  booksellers  might  be 
prejudicial  has  been  confirmed  by  the  stand  made  against 
It  by  the  Booksellers'  and  the  Publishers'  Associations, 
wd  by  the  Council  of  the  Authors'  Society — a  triple  solid 
wall,  against  which  The  Times  newspaper,  sometimes  over 


8  PREFACE 

the  signature  of  Mr.  A.  F.  Walter  himself,  has  directed 
pamphlet  after  pamphlet  and  advertisement  after  advertise- 
ment, full  of  statements,  many  of  which  have  not  borne  even 
the  most  superficial  criticism  of  experts.     It  is  not  proposed 
in  this  place  to  review  this  somewhat  sordid  trade  aispute — 
for  it  amounts  in  its  essence  to  nothing  more — at  any 
length.     Before  these  lines  are  in  print — certainly  before 
this  Annual  is  replaced  by  its  successor — the  whole  matter 
will,  without  doubt,  be  settled  and  on  the  way  to  be  for- 
gotten.    Therefore  it  is  undesirable,  in  the  longer  interests 
of  peace,  and  in  the  seemlier  cause  of  literature,  to  preserve 
even  for  a  year  the  ephemeral  aspects  of  what  is  now 
known  as  the  *  Book  War.*     But  this,  at  least,  must  be 
said :  that  the  alleged  intention  of  The  Times  to  benefit 
authors  has  been  repudiated  by  the  Council  of  the  Authors* 
Society,  as  well  as  by  the  most  authoritative  and  one  of 
the   most   honourable   literary   organs    in    England,    The 
Athenceum^  which,  in  its  issue  of  November  3,  published, 
in  the  rare  form  of  a  leading  article,  a  severe  and  final 
indictment  of   the   Club  ;    that   its   alleged   intention   to 
benefit  bookselling  as  a  trade  is  repudiated  by  the  fact 
that  this  trade,  which  had  begim  slowly  to  revive  under 
the  *'  net  book  "  system,  is  united  to  a  man  against  the 
attempt  of  The  Times  to  undermine  it ;  and,  lastly,  that 
its  practical  example  recommended  to  the  imitation  of 
publishers,  and  announced  in  many  columns  of  gratuitous 
advertisement  of  a  book-of-the-piay  dramatized   from   a 
well-known  novel,  was  very  wisely  ignored  by  itself  when 
this  volume  was  stillborn,  and  when  the  fallacies  under- 
lying the  experiment  had  provoked  a  chorus  of  ridicule 
from   authors,    publishers,   and   booksellers.      Facts   and 
figures  relating  to  the  profits  of  publishers,  misrepresented 
by  The   Times  by   the   disingenuous   omission   of  plant 
expenses,  advertisement  expenses,  travelling  and  establish- 
ment expenses,  by  an  underestimate  of  the  cost  of  pro- 
duction, of  various  trade  discounts,  and,  most  of  all,  of 
the  advances  on  royalties   paid   to   authors,   and  by   a 
corresponding  overestimate  of   the  selling   capacities   of 
new  books  by  untried  writers,  will  be  found  in  the  section 
dealing  with  Law  and  Letters.     It  may,  perhaps,  be  added 
her^  that  Dr.  Arthur  Shadwell's  Industrial  Efficiency  should 


PREFACE  9 

have  been  included  among  the  most  notable  books  of 
1906,  and  would  have  found  a  place  there  if  it  had  not 
been  published  at  a  price  so  entirely  at  variance  with 
the  economic  views  on  this  subject  expressed  in  his  articles 
contributed  to  The  Times  newspaper.  At  the  time  of 
writii^  there  seems  to  be  every  probability  that  subscribers 
to  a  circulating  library,  whatever  its  pretensions,  will  not 
tolerate  dictation  as  to  the  books  which  they  are  not  to 
read,  and  that  the  views  of  the  trade  combination  against 
the  methods  adopted  by  The  Times,  resting,  as  they  do,  not 
wholly  on  selfish  or  interested  motives,  but  on  a  reasonable 
apprehension  of  the  true  interests  of  literature,  will  prevail 
against  the  united  forces  of  America  and  Printing-House 
Square.  From  the  author's  point  of  view  especially  it 
is  to  be  hoped  that  this  regrettable  squabble  will  be 
forgotten  as  soon  as  possible. 


THE  EDITOR, 


C/o  George  Routledge  &  Sons,  Ltd., 
Broadway  House, 

LuDGATE  Hill,  E.C., 

November  30,  1906.

A  UTERARY  MOENING  FAFER. 

A  conspicuous  feature  of  THE  BTAMD  ARD  is  the  interest  it  possesses  for  all  who  read  hooks. 

Being  conscious  of  the  fact  that  the  paper  is  appreciated  by  an  educateil  class,  the  present 
Manafi[ement  has  made  provision  for  maintaining,  and  even  increasing,  the  attention  which  TKS 
BTANDA1U>  has  consistently  paid  to  literary  matters. 

A  very  liberal  amount  of  space  is  allotted  to  reviews  of  new  books,  and  erery  Tneidfty  Mid  Friday 
the  colimmi  of  TKS  BTAMnDARDy  both  litonuy  and  advertiaommit,  may  bo  couoltod  wltn 
the  fullest  confidence  for  Information  regarding  everything  of  importance  that  ia  taking  place  in 
the  book  world.  While  these  two  days  are  to  be  regarded  as  "  s|)ccial  Publishers'  days,  a  strong 
literary  interest  is  always  to  be  found  in  the  paper,  which  devotes  space  to  Reviews  of  Books  every  day, 
and  frequently  deals  at  length  with  some  particular  publication  or  event  of  an  unusual  character. 
TKS  STANDARD  is  singularljr  well  placed  for  giving  early  publication  to  important  literary 
news  from  abroad,  of  which  fact  sufficient  inaication  h  is  been  recently  given  by  the  articles  on,  e.jz.t 
Count  Keventlow's  book  on  the  Kaiser  and  Herr  Bleibtreu's  work  on  uie  authorship  of  the  Shake- 
S|)carean  plays.

cannot  do  better  than  advertiee  in  TKS  BTAMDAKD.

A  LITERARY  EVENING  FAFER. 

One  has  but  to  glance  at  TKS  SVENINO  BTANDARD  AND  BT.  JAMOBS'B 
OAJSETTS  to  perceive  that  it  appeals  to  educated  people.  There  is  no  other  evening  ]iaiier  that  so 
happily  supplies  the  needs  of  what  may  be  termed  the  evening  mood.  While  bright  and  entertaining, 
it  so  presents  the  topics  it  deals  with  that  it  enlists  the  sympathies  of  a  thoughtful  and  refined  Inxly  of 
readers.  Among  penny  evening  papers  it  is  by  far  the  most  successful,  its  circulation  exceeding  that  of 
any  two  other  penny  London  evening  papers  put  together.  The  daily  illustratecl  page  for  and  aliout 
women  makes  it  specially  attractive  to  ladies.

days and  Thursdays  this  characteristic  is  more  tlian  usually  marked.

ENQU5H    LITERATURE. 

HOW  TO  READ  ENGLISH  LITERATURE :  Chancer  to  Milton. 

By  LAURIE  MAGNUS,  M.A., 

Author  of  •'  Introduction  to  Poetry,"  "  Words  and  their  U.se,"  etc. 

[George  Routledge  and  Sons.    as.  6d. 

Contents. — Chap.  I.  England  among  the  Naticois ;  II.  Chaucer's  Eneland  and 
England's  Chaucer ;  III.  A  Barren  Age  and  its  Termination  ;  IV.  England's  Meridian  ; 
V.  Cynthia  and  her  Shepherds;  VI.  Shakespeare;  VII.  The  Progress  of  Pro.sc; 
VIII.  The  Puritan  Reaction  ;  Index. 

The  Second  Part,  Dryden  to  Meredith,  will  be  issued  in  the 

Spring  of  1907. 

From  the  Author* s  Preface. — "  These  chapters  are  intended  as  an  introductory  course 
to  the  study  of  English  literature.  They  attempt,  first,  to  interest  the  reader,  as  the 
primary  condition  of  his  future  studies;  and,  secondly,  to  direct  his  attention  to  the 
general  uni^ing^  principles  which  govern  the  subject,  and  to  provide  the  itudent  with 
the  means  of '  orientation' — the  power  to  find  the  points  of  the  compass,  whatever  part 
of  the  field  he  may  be  surveying.  Thus,  they  deal  with  impressions  and  with  states  of 
being  rather  than  with  methods  and  minutiae  of  criticism.  ..."

AUTHORS 

NOTE. — The  entries  in  this  Directory  have  been  revised,  in  nearly  all 
eases,  by  the  authors  themselves,  and  the  Publishers  desire  to 
thank  the  very  large  number  of  correspondents  who  have  assisted 
in  this  manner.  Owing  to  considerations  of  space,  it  has  not 
always  been  possible  to  give  an  exhaustive  list  of  each  writer's 
work ;  but  the  titles  included  having  been  selected  by  the  authors, 

V  ")  the  information  in  such  eases  will  be  found  representative.  Where 
no  returns  have  been  made,  information  has  been  gathered  to  a 
large  extent  from  other  sources ;  but  in  doubtful  cases — i.e.,  where 
no  definite  information  has  been  forthcoming — names  have  been 
.  necessarily  though  reluctantly  omitted.  Returns  received  later 
than  December  1st  have  not  been  included,  nor  have  songs, 
pamphlets,  serials,  or  sub-titles,  as  a  rule,  been  inserted. 

A  (t)  prefixed  means  that  the  name  is  a  pseudonym;  a 
H)  implies  Membership  of  the  French  Academy. 

The  abbreviations  used  in  the  Directory  will,  it  is  hoped, 
explain  themselves.  C= Contributor.  E=:  Elected.  Where 
the  titles  of  two  or  more  consecutive  books  have  been  given. 
Issued  by  the  same  firm,  the  publisher's  name  has  not  been 
repeated.  Foreign  books  are  inserted  with  the  date  of  publica- 
tion without  brackets ;  but  if  also  published  in  England,  these 
particulars  are  bracketed.

HAHILTON,  Cosmo.  Second  son  of  the  late  Henry  Gibbs,  Board 
of  Education,  and  Helen  Hamilton.  Assumed  mother's  name 
in  1898.  Dramatist  and  novelist.  Author  of  Duke's  Son  ; 
The  Danger  of  Innocence  ;  The  Passing  of  Arthur ;  The  Glamour 
of  the  Impossible  ;  Which  is  Absurd  ;  Nature's  Vagabond,  &c.  ; 
and  of  plays  :  The  Wisdom  of  Folly  ;  The  Mountain  Climber  ; 
Grandfather  Coquesne  ;  Castles  in  Spain  ;  and  part  au.  of  The

AUTHORS'  ASSISTANTS 
I.  AGENTS 

(i.)   BRITISH 

This  list  consists  chiefly  of  the  names  of  Literary  Agents,  whose  work 
includes  the  arrangement  of  books  for  publication  and  all  com- 
mercial negotiations  concerning  them,  together  with  the  general 
management  of  literary  property.  Other  names  are  here  given, 
with  explanatory  particulars. 

ASSOCIATED  PRESS,  24  Old  Jewry,  e.c.  An  agency  representing 
the  prominent  newspapers  throughout  the  United  States  of 
America  and  the  British  Provinces,  also  acting  for  the  Reuter, 
Havas,  and  Wolff  Agencies  of  Europe.  Telegraphic  address  : 
*'  Associated,  London."     Telephone  :  5933  Bank,  8294  Central. 

AUTHORS'  SYNDICATE,  5-7  Southampton  Street,  Strand,  w.c. 
Founded  1890.  This  organization  acts  as  advisers  and  agents 
for  the  valuation,  sale,  purchase,  registration,  and  management 
of  literary  and  dramatic  copyright  property  in  England,  the 
Continent  of  Europe,  and  the  United  States  of  America. 
Mr.  W.  Morris  Colles,  the  founder  and  manager,  part  author  of 
Playright  and  Copyright  in  all  Countries  (Macmillan),  can  produce 
testimonials  from,  amongst  many  others,  A.  W.  A'Beckett, 
E.  Lester  Arnold,  E.  F.  Benson,  Hall  Caine,  Sir  W.  Martin 
Conway,  the  Right  Hon.  Lord  Curzon  of  Kedleston,  F.  Marion 
Crawford,  Thomas  Hardy,  Dr.  Sven  Hedin,  Sir  Thomas  Hunger- 
ford  Holdich,  Dr.  J.  Scott  Keltie,  George  Meredith,  Sir  Lewis 
Morris,  Eden  PhiUpotts,  F.  C.  Selous,  Flora  Annie  Steel, 
I.  Zangwill. 

BENNETT,  Mr.  HANNAFORD,  6  Clement's  Inn,  w.c.  Literary 
agent.  Mr.  Bennett  was  a  partner  in  the  publishing  house 
of  Messrs.  Henry  &  Co.  for  many  years  previously  to  1898, 
in  which  year  he  estabUshed  an  agency  for  supplying  newspapers 
and  periodicals  with  the  best  fiction  and  special  articles. 

BROWN,  CURTIS,  International  Publishing  Bureau,  5  Henrietta

nental book  and  serial  rights  placed  for  authors.  Repre- 
sentatives in  New  York,  Toronto  (Canada),  Paris,  Berlin, 
Rome,  Vienna,  Madrid,  and  Stockholm.  American  copyrights 
secured. 

BURGHES,  Mr.  A.  M.,  34  Paternoster  Row,  E.c.     Literary  agent. 

CASSELL'S  GENERAL  PRESS.  Established  to  supply  the  require- 
ments of  London,  Provincial,  and  Colonial  newspapers.  Serial 
and  short  stories  by  all  the  best  authors,  and  articles  of  general 
and  topical  interest  by  leading  writers  of  the  day.  Weekly 
features  a  speciality.  Address  :  Cassell's  General  Press,  La 
Belle  Sauvage,  e.c.

HARRIS-BICKFORD.  E.  L.  T.,  Thornley  House,  Redruth.  Uterary 
tutor,  adviser,  and  authors'  agent.  Mr.  Harris-Bickford 
corrects,  criticises,  and  comments  on  articles,  stories,  and 
verse  submitted  to  him,  and  suggests  suitable  channels  for  the

INTERNATIONAL  ART  SUPPLY  AND  GENERAL  INFORMA- 
TION AGENCY,  The  Studios,  Linden  Gardens,  London,  w. 
EstabUshed  1890.  Under  Royal  Patronage.  This  Agency, 
unique  of  its  kind,  was  founded  by  Mr.  Augustin  Rischgitz, 
and  is  under  his  own  personal  direction.  It  appeals  to  editors, 
pubUshers,  authors,  and  cc^ectors,  and  by  means  of  a  special 
IntelUgence  Department  in  connection  with  artists,  photo- 
graphers, and  correspondents,  at  home  and  abroad,  supplies 
pictures,  engravings,  and  photographs  of  objects  and  places 
of  all  kinds  for  purposes  of  special  illustration  ;  also  arranging 
for  rights  of  reproduction  when  required.  The  Agency  in 
addition  has  a  large  and  constantly  increasing  collection  of 
portraits,  also  illustrations  of  a  topographical,  antiquarian, 
uterary,  and  historical  nature,  besides  reproductions  of  pictures, 
objects,  and  documents  in  museums,  galleries,  and  national 
institutions,  all  secured  for  some  special  purpose,  and  available 
for  grangerising  and  use  in  articles  and  books.  Telegrams: 
Chercheur,  London.  Telephone:  4413  Paddington.  ABC  and 
Lieber's  Code  used. 

INTERNATIONAL  COPYRIGHT  BUREAU,  LIMITED.  Founded

aries between  authors  and  publishers,  playwrights  and  managers,

w.c.  Founded  in  1892  for  the  purpose  of  undertaking  the

correspondents  and  as  advertisement  and  general  business 
agents.  It  has  developed  into  a  general  advertising  and  news 
agency,  most  of  its  news  and  correspondence  being  supplied

MONCRIEFF,  Mr.  H.  A.,  St.  Paul's  Chambers,  Ludgate  Hill,  E.c. 
Mr.  Moncrieff  is  not  a  literary  agent.  His  work,  dealing  with 
copyright  valuations,  partnerships,  agreements,  and  so  forth, 
is  of  a  confidential  nature,  and  as  Receiver  or  Trustee  he  has 
had  much  experience  in  the  winding  up  of  numerous  publishing 
firms  which  have  become  extinct  during  the  past  ten  years. 

NATIONAL  PRESS  AGENCY,  LIMITED.  EstabUshed  1873. 
This  agency,  situated  at  Whitefriars  House,  CarmeUte  Street, 
London,  e.g.,  supplies  every  requirement  for  daily  and  weekly 
provincial  newspapers.  Leading  articles  and  London  letters ; 
special  columns  by  well-known  writers.  Branches  at  New  York 
and  Sydney.     Manager  :  Mr.  John  Reburn. 

NOPS,  LIMITED,  19  Ludgate  Hill,  London,  e.g.  Established 
1873.  Its  object  is  to  supply  illustrations  and  illustrated 
matter  to  the  press.  It  acts  also  as  a  Literary  Agency,  and 
makes  a  speciality  of  the  sale  of  foreign  translation  rights. 
Telegrams  :  Denops,  London.     Telephone  :  880  Bank. 

NORTHERN  NEWSPAPER  SYNDICATE  (THE).  This  agency 
suppUes  the  Press  with  fiction  and  with  articles  (signed)  on 
the  foremost  subjects  of  the  day.  Proposals  from  authors 
(which  should  not  in  the  first  instance  be  accompanied  by 
MSS.)  are  promptly  considered  and  decided  upon.  Manager  : 
Mr.  James  E.  Lyons.  Telegraphic  address  :  Syndicate,  Kendal. 
Head  office,  Kendal,  England. 

PINKER,  Mr.  J.  B.,  Effingham  House,  Arundel  Street,  London, 
W.G.,  and  156  Fifth  Avenue,  New  York.  Literary  agent. 
Mr.  Pinker  has  always  made  a  special  point  of  helping  young 
authors  in  the  early  stages  of  their  career,  when  they  need 
most  the  aid  of  an  adviser  with  a  thorough  knowledge  of  the 
Uterary  world  and  the  publishing  trade.

PRESS  ASSOCIATION,  LIMITED,  14  New  Bridge  Street,  e.g. 
Manager  :  Mr.  E.  Robbins.  Incorporated  in  1868  for  supply-

PRESS  PICTURE  AGENCY,  LIMITED,  1 1  Tothill  Street,  West- 
minster, s.w.  Supplies  topical  and  other  photographs  of  Royal 
persons  and  of  hving  celebrities ;  also  photographs  of  well- 
known  men  and  women — of  their  houses,  their  animals,  &c.

SCHOOL  OF  JOURNALISM  FOR  WOMEN,  8  and  9  Johnson's 
Court,  Fleet  Street,  e.g.  Provides  training  in  joumaUsm,

Address  :  Crinoline,  London.     Telephone  :  171  Holbom. 

SOCIETY  OF  DRAMATIC  AUTHORS  AND  COMPOSERS  IN 
GERMANY,  THE  (Messrs.  Schuster  &  Loeffler  in  Berlin). 
The  chief  aim  of  the  Society  is  to  contribute,  to  the  best  of  its 
power,  to  a  more  intimate  knowledge  and  appreciation  of  the 
dramatic  culture  of  the  different  nations.  For  that  reason  it 
arranges  productions  of  English  plays  in  Germany  and  elsewhere 
on  the  Continent,  protects  the  rights  of  English  authors  there 
in  every  way,  and  negotiates  for  them  with  the  most  important 
theatre-managers  in  Germany  and  elsewhere  to  the  best 
advantage.  The  translations  are  carefully-prepared  literary 
works,  done  by  literary  men  who  are  themselves  members  of 
the  Society,  and  thoroughly  at  home  in  the  technique  of  the 
stage.  The  London  literary  representative  of  the  Society, 
Mr.  Frank  E.  Washburn  Freund,  The  Cottage,  Lyon  Road, 
Harrow-on-the-Hill,  will  always  be  pleased  to  consider  English 
plays  which  might  be  suitable  for  production  abroad,  whether 
the  author  be  known  or  unknown,  and  whether  the  play  has 
been  given  in  English  or  not. 

SPRIGG,  PEDRICK,  &  CO.,  LIMITED.  London  and  New  York, 
Uterary  agents,  readers,  brokers,  and  newspaper  serialists. 
This  Company  consists  of  an  association  of  literary  and  business 
men  who  are  well  versed  in  modem  literature,  and  widely 
experienced  in  all  the  methods  and  machinery  of  modem 
publishing  and  editing.  Some  of  the  foremost  writers  are 
represented  solely  by  it,  and  occasionally  it  acts  for  many 
more.  It  also  takes  up  a  limited  number  of  new  authors, 
devotes  special  attention  to  their  work,  and  assists  as  far  as 
possible  in  their  advancement  and  recognition.  To  authors 
it  also  renders  expert  assistance  in  the  reading,  reporting  upon, 
and  revision  of  MSS.,  and  under  the  supervision  of  a  well- 
known  critic  is  making  a  speciality  of  dramatic  work.  The 
company  is  ably  represented  in  America  by  the  leading  literary 


3i8  THE  LITERARY  YEAR-BOOK       [part  i. 

agent  of  the  United  States.  A  special  study  of  the  serial 
market  is  made  with  a  view  of  finding  and  filling  suitable 
vacancies  for  clients'  work.  The  Company  also  undertakes  to 
negotiate  the  sale  and  purchase  of  newspaper  and  magazine 
properties,  to  arrange  partnerships  and  shares  in  publishing 
concerns,  and  generally  to  effect  investments  in  Uterary, 
pubUshing,  and  journalistic  enterprises.  Courses  of  literary 
and  journalistic  tuition  have  been  arranged  with  a  well- 
known  author,  editor,  and  reviewer.  Altogether  the  business 
of  the  Company  embraces  over  twenty  departments.  Detailed 
prospectus  with  testimonials  may  be  had  on  application. 
Amongst  those  of  others,  S.  P.  &  Co.  have  been  instrumental 
in  successfully  placing  important  MSS.  by  the  following : 
WilUam  Le  Queux,  Hume  Nisbet,  Louis  Tracy,  George  GriflSth, 
Douglas  Sladen,  Fergus  Hume,  H.  D.  Lowry,  Fred  Whishaw, 
WiUiam  Westall,  Bertram  Mitford,  G.  Manville  Fenn,  John 
Strange  Winter,  Father  Ignatius,  J.  Bloundell  Burton,  Coulson

Louis  Zangwill,  George  Paston,  Lady  Florence  Dixie,  Baroness 
de  Bertouch,  Archibald  Dunn,  jnr.,  Madame  Albanesi,  Alice 
and  Claude  Askew,  A.  St.  John  Adcock,  Guy  Boothby,  Shan

THE  LITERARY  AGENCY,  2-4  Tudor  Street,  e.g.  Conducted 
by  W.  Scott,  B.A.,  LL.D.,  and  J.  D.  Rickey,  B.A.  This 
agency  arranges  for  the  sale  of  good  articles,  sketches,  short 
stories,  and  novels. 

THE  LONDON  ELECTROTYPE  AGENCY,  LIMITED,  31  St. 
Bride  Street,  e.g.  Correspondents  in  Paris,  Berlin,  and  New 
York.  This  Agency,  established  in  1901,  is  under  the  direction 
of  Mr.  H.  C.  Buckworth,  whose  connection  since  1877  with  the 
business  of  illustrations  dates  back  to  the  earliest  electrotype 
agency,  founded  in  1873.  The  principal  representatives  of  the 
firm  have  also  been  engaged  in  the  business  for  many  years.  It 
is  of  an  international  character,  and  is  carried  on  for  the  purpose  of 
suppljdng  electrotypes  for  the  EngUsh  and  foreign  newspapers, 
magazines,  &c.  The  sole  agencies  for  illustrations  include  the 
Illustrated  London  News,  Sketch,  Lady's  Pictorial,  L* Illustration 
(Paris),  London  Magazine,  and  The  World  and  His  Wife.  An  im- 
portant feature  is  the  supply  of  photographs  from  various  sources 
for  the  purpose  of  pubUcation — Royalty  (portraits,  incidents,  and 
residences),  current  events,  celebrities  of  the  day,  views,  studies 


PART  I.]  AGENTS  319 

of  natural  history,  military,  naval,  theatrical,  and  other  subjects 
being  included  in  the  series  available  for  reproduction.  The 
firm  are  special  Press  Agents  for  such  well-known  photographers 
as  Madame  Lallie  Chsirles,  F.  Ralph  of  Dersingham,  Ernest 
H.  Mills,  Messrs.  Bassano,  J.  C.  Dinham,  Bourne  &  Shepherd 
of  Calcutta,  and  others.  Every  faciUty  is  afiorded  to  authors, 
editors,  and  publishers  who  desire  to  secure  illustrations  for 
any  purpose,  the  best  and  latest  material  available  being  placed 
at  their  disposal.  The  supply  of  half-tone  and  Une  blocks,  and 
the  execution  of  original  drawings,  forms  another  branch  of 
the  business.  Telegrams  :  "  Ambrotype,  London."  Telephone  : 
1 94 1,  Holbom.

♦WATT,  Messrs.  A.  P.,  &  SON,  Hastings  House,  Norfolk  Street. 
Strand,  w.c.  Messrs.  A.  P.  Watt  &  Son  are  at  all  times  glad 
*  to  hear  from  authors,  whether  at  the  beginning  of  their  career 
or  of  established  reputation,  with  a  view  to  the  disposal  of  their 
work.  A  good  idea  of  the  value  to  authors  of  Messrs.  A.  P. 
Watt  &  Son's  Literary  Agency  is  given  in  the  following  extract 
from  the  Autobiography  of  the  late  Sir  Walter  Besant  :  "  Let 
me  here  express  my  great  and  lasting  gratitude  to  my  agents, 
Mr.  A.  P.  Watt  and  his  son,  by  whose  watch  and  ward  my 
interests  have  been  so  carefully  guarded  for  eighteen  years. 
During  that  time  I  have  always  been  engaged  for  three  years  in 
advance  ;  I  have  been  relieved  from  every  kind  of  pecuniary 
anxiety ;  my  income  has  been  multipUed  by  three  at  least ; 
and  I  have  had,  through  them,  the  offer  of  a  great  deal  more 
work  than  I  could  undertake.  I  cannot  speak  too  strongly 
of  the  services  rendered  to  me  by  my  literary  agents.  Of 
course,  there  are  different  kinds  of  agents.     There  is  the  agent, 

*  To  Mr.  A  P.  Watt  is  due  the  rise  of  the  literary  agent. .  He  was  the  first  to  con* 
ceive  and  put  into  practice  the  idea  of  the  literary  agent,  acting  as  an  intermediary 
between  authors  and  publishers  and  editors.

for  example,  who  knows  nothing  about  his  business.  But 
the  agent  who  does  know  his  business,  who  knows  also  editors, 
publishers,  and  their  arrangements,  may  be  of  immense  use  to 
the  noveUst,  the  essayist,  the  traveller — ^in  short,  to  the  author 
of  any  book  that  can  command  a  circulation  and  a  public 
demand.  And  such  an  agent  is  Mr.  A.  P.  Watt."  Amongst 
the  many  cUents  of  the  j&rm  are  :  The  Rt.  Hon.  A.  J.  Balfour,

Crockett,  Sir  Arthur  Conan  Doyle,  Miss  Fowler,  Mr.  H.  Rider

III.  PRESS-CUTTING. 

Press  -  Catting  Agents  supply  Extracts  from  the  Papers  upon  all 
coneeivable  subjects.  The  nsual  charge  for  such  extracts  is 
about  £1  Is.  for  125  cuttings,  a  reduction  being  made  where  the 
subscription  covers  a  large  quantity.  American  Agents  gener- 
ally charge  $5  for  100  cuttings^  or  $3*50  for  50.  These 
Agencies  undertake  all  kinds  of  work  connected  with  the  supply 
of  general  information.

LAW   AND    LETTERS 


We  begin  with  a  sketch  of  the  law  of  copyright,  including 
the  right  of  public  representation  of  dramatic  and  musical 
pieces.  After  dealing  with  the  law  as  it  is,  with  due  regard 
to  the  cases  decided  since  the  last  publication  of  the  Year- 
Book,  we  shall  refer  to  the  subject  of  law  reform.  We 
shall  then  discuss  the  relations  of  authors  and  publishers, 
and  shall  conclude  with  a  brief  consideration  of  the  usual 
clauses  in  agreements  ordinarily  entered  into  between 
author  and  publisher,  explaining  where  necessary  their  use 
and  application. 

I.  LAW  OF  COPYRIGHT 

It  is  convenient  to  deal  separately  with  the  law  ex- 
clusively regarding  the  United  Kingdom  or  Home  copy- 
right, and  with  Colonial  and  International  copyrights. 

HOME   COPYRIGHT 

The  heads  under  which  the  law  treats  the  subject-matter 
are  as  follows : 

1.  Books. 

2.  Dramatic  pieces. 

3.  Musical  compositions. 

4.  Lectures. 

5.  Engravings  and  other  works  of  the  same  kind. 

6.  Fine  arts,  including  paintings,  drawings,  and  photo- 
graphs. 

7.  Sculpture. 

341  .  :: '      : 


342  THE  LITERARY  YEAR-BOOK      [part  ii. 

The  law  consists  partly  of  common  law  principles,  and 
partly  of  statute  law,  which  is  contained  in  the  provisions 
of  the  several  Acts  of  ParUament,  which  are  next  stated  in 
order  of  date,  with  their  titles,  namely  : 

8  Geo.  II.,  c.  13 — Engraving  Copyright  Act,  1734. 

7  Geo.  III.,  c.  38 — Engraving  Copyright  Act,  1766. 

15  Geo.  III.,  c.  53 — Copyright  Act,  1775. 

17  Geo.  III.,  c.  57 — Prints  Copyright  Act,  1777. 

54  Geo.  III.,  c.  56 — Sculpture  Copyright  Act,  1814. 

3  &  4  Wm.  IV.,  c.  15 — Dramatic  Copyright  Act,  1833. 

5  &  6  Wm.  IV.,  c.  65 — Lectures  Copyright  Act,  1835. 

6  &  7  Wm.  IV.,  c.  59 — Prints  and  Engravings  Copy- 
right (Ireland)  Act,  1836. 

5  &  6  Vict.,  c.  45 — Copyright  Act,  1842  (Books,  &c.). 

15  &  16  Vict.,  c.  12 — International  Copyright  Act,  1852 
(explanatory  of  the  Prints  and  Engravings  Copyright 
Acts  above). 

25  &  26  Vict.,  c.  68 — Fine  Arts  Copyright  Act,  1862. 

45  &  46  Vict.,  c.  40 — Cop3n:ight  (Musical  Compositions) 
Act,  1882. 

51  &  52  Vict.,  c.  17 — Copyright  (Musical  Compositions) 
Act,  1888. 

2  Edward  VII.,  c.  15 — Musical  (Summary  Proceed- 
ings) Copyright  Act,  1902. 

6  Edward  VII.,  c.  36 — Musical  Copyright  Act,  1906. 

Unpublished  Matter 

As  regards  any  of  the  above  subject-matter  which  has 
not  been  published,  the  author  or  owner  has  by  the  common 
law  an  absolute  right,  so  long  as  it  remains  unpublished, 
to  prevent  the  publication  of  any  copy  of  it. 

The  communication  to  others  in  a  limited  way  or  on 
the  faith  that  there  will  not  be  publication,  what  may  be 
termed  **  private  publication,"  does  not  destroy  such  right. 
For  example,  commimication  by  sending  a  letter,  or  by 
deUvering  an  address  to  a  limited  audience,  or  communica- 
tion made  to  an  employe  in  the  course  of  his  employment, 
is  not  publication  in  the  legal  sense. 


PART  II.]  LAW  AND  LETTERS  343 

L  Copyright  in  Books 

Generally 

This  copyright  endures  for  the  hfe  of  the  author  and 
sevien  years  after  his  death,  or  for  forty-two  years  from 
the  date  of  publication,  whichever  is  the  longer,  and  is 
by  the  Copyright  Act,  1842,  declared  to  be  the  property 
of  the  author  and  his  assigns,  but  in  the  case  of  a  book 
published  after  the  author's  death,  the  copyright  endures 
for  forty-two  years  from  the  date  of  first  publication,  and 
is  by  tne  same  Act  declared  to  be  the  property  of  the 
proprietor  of  the  author's  manuscript  from  which  such 
book  shall  be  first  published,  and  his  assigns.  The  term 
"  book "  includes  every  volume,  part  or  division  of  a 
volume,  pamphlet,  sheet  of  letterpress,  sheet  of  music, 
map,  chart,  or  plan,  separately  published  ;  and  under  one 
or  other  of  these  heads  protection  has  been  extended  to — 

Maps, 

Newspapers, 

Compilations  of  various  kinds — e.g.,  directories  and 
catalogues,  annotations  of  standard  works,  new  editions 
with  substantial  alterations  and  additions. 

And  even  verbatim  reports  of  speeches  (see  Walter  v. 
Lane,  the  case  of  the  Times  reports  of  Lord  Rosebery's 
speeches,  decided  by  the  House  of  Lords  in  1900). 

The  recent  case  of  Macmillan  v.  Dent  raised  interesting 
questions  as  to  the  right  to  publish  posthumous  letters. 
The  case  was  decided  by  Mr.  Justice  Kekewich  in  December, 
1905,  and  his  decision  was  upheld  in  the  Court  of  Appeal 
by  Lord  Justices  Vaughan  Williams,  Moulton,  and  Buckley 
on  November  7, 1906.  The  letters  in  question  were  written 
by  Charles  Lamb,  and  had  not  been  published  in,  or  prior 
to,  1895,  when  the  copyright  therein  was  sold  and  the 
actual  letters  lent  by  A.  B.,  in  whose  possession  they  then 
were,  to  Messrs  Smith  Elder,  and  Co.,  by  whom  a  licence 
to  print  them  was  granted  to  the  plaintiffs.  The  letters 
were  subsequently  published  by  the  plaintiffs  from  copies 
taken  by  thertr.  Some  years  later  the  defendent  bought 
the  actual  letters  from  A.   B.,  having  been  previously 


344  THE  LITERARY  YEAR-BOOK     [part  ii. 

informed  by  A.  B.  of  the  said  assignment  by  him  to  the 
plaintiffs  of  the  copyright  therein,  and  published  the  letters 
from  the  originals  in  an  edition  of  Lamb's  letters.  The 
plaintiffs,  having  registered  themselves  as  proprietors  of 
the  cop5night,  sued  the  defendant  to  restrain  publication 
of  any  book  infringing  their  rights  in  the  letters,  and  it 
was  decided  and  upheld  that  they  were  the  assigns  of  the 
author's  manuscript — that  is,  of  the  actual  paper  on  which 
the  writing  was  and  the  writing  upon  it — and  therefore 
that  the  copyright  in  the  letters  was  the  property  of  the 
plaintiffs  as  such  assigns,  and  that  the  right  of  publication 
was  vested  in  them.  A.  B.,  having  the  letters  rightfully 
in  his  possession,  was  entitled  to  publish  them  himself, 
or  to  hand  them  over  to  the  plaintiffs  for  publication,  and 
to  give  them  the  right  of  publication,  and  having  done 
the  last,  A.  B.  had  nothing  remaining  in  him  to  pass  to 
anyone,  except  the  right  to  the  letters  themselves,  which 
he  had  retained,  and  could  part  with — e.g.,  to  a  collector 
of  autographs,  but  without  any  right  of  publication. 

Encyclopedias,  Periodicals,  and  other  Proprietary  Works 

As  regards  literary  work  composed  for  publication  in 
or  as  part  of  any  encyclopaedia,  review,  magazine,  periodical 
work,  or  serial  publication,  by  any  persons  employed  to 
compose  the  same  on  the  terms  that  the  copyright  should 
belong  to  the  proprietor  or  publisher,  after  the  work  has 
been  paid  for  by  such  proprietor  or  publisher,  the  copy- 
right in  all  such  work  becomes  the  property  of  the  pro- 
prietor or  publisher,  subject,  however,  to  the  following 
qualification  in  the  case  of  contributions  to  periodicals  : 
After  twenty-eight  years  from  first  publication  the  right 
of  separate  publication  reverts  to  the  author,  and  endures 
for  the  remainder  of  the  full  term  of  copyright,  and  during 
the  twenty-eight  years  the  publisher  or  proprietor  may  not 
publish  them  separately  without  the  consent,  previously 
obtained,  of  the  author  or  his  assigns  (see  Section  i8  of 
the  Copyright  Act,  1842). 

The  contract  between  the  contributing  author  and  the 
pubhsher  or  proprietor  of  the  periodical  *vork  need  not 
be  in  writing,  nor  is  an  express  agreement  that  the^copy- 
right  shall  belong  to  the  latter  necessary. 


PART  II.]  LAW  AND  LETTERS  345 

It  was  laid  down  many  years  ago  that,  where  the  pro- 
prietors of  a  periodical  employ  a  person  to  write  an  article, 
or  a  series  of  articles  or  reports,  expressly  for  publication 
therein,  it  is  necessarily  implied  that  the  copjnright  of  the 
articles  so  written  for  such  periodical,  and  paid  for  by  the 
proprietors  and  publishers  thereof,  is  the  property  of  such 
proprietors  and  publishers.  (By  the  Court  of  Common 
Pleas  in  Sweet  v.  Benning,  where  the  periodical  was  a 
weekly  paper  called  the  Jurist,  which  principally  con- 
sisted of  law  reports.)  The  principle  of  that  case  was 
recently  approved  by  the  House  of  Lords  and  applied  to 
articles  contributed  to  an  encyclopaedia  of  sport  in  Laurence 
and  Btdlen  v.  Aflalo  and  Cook,  and  it  is  conceived  that  if 
an  author  who  has  been  paid  for  his  work  claims  separate 
copyright  in  contributions  to  any  of  the  different  kinds 
of  publications  mentioned  above,  he  will,  in  the  absence  of 
an  express  agreement  reserving  his  right,  have  great  diffi- 
culty in  negativing  an  implied  agreement  that  the  copy- 
right should  belong  to  the  proprietor  or  publisher.  But 
the  question  is  one  of  fact  as  well  as  law,  and  will  always 
depend  upon  the  particular  circumstances  of  each  case. 

If  an  author  specially  reserve  the  right  of  separate 
publication  of  any  contributed  work,  he  will  then  be 
entitled  to  the  copyright  in  it  when  published  separately 
at  any  time,  but  without  prejudice  to  the  right  of  the  pro- 
prietor or  publisher  to  copyright  in  the  production  of 
which  it  formed  part. 

Whether  a  proprietary  work  is  published  in  serial  form 
or  otherwise,  employment  of  the.  author  by  the  proprietor 
to  compose  the  work,  the  composition  of  the  work  by  the 
author  under  such  employment,  on  the  terms  that  the 
copyright  shall  belong  to  the  proprietor,  and  payment  for 
the  work  by  the  proprietor,  are  conditions  precedent  to 
the  vesting  of  the  copyright  therein  in  the  proprietor.  In 
the  recent  case  of  Ward,  Lock  and  Co.,  Limited,  v.  John 
Long,  in  the  Chancery  Division,  these  requisites  were 
much  discussed  in  rather  peculiar  circumstances. 

It  appeared  that  the  author  submitted  through  his 
agent  a  synopsis  of  a  story  to  the  plaintiffs,  and  an  agree- 
ment by  letters  in  December,  1904,  was  come  to  by  which 
the  plaintiffs  agreed  to  purchase  the  cop3n:ight  in  volume 


34^  THE  LITERARY  YEAR-BOOK     [part  ii. 

form  of  the  story,  which  was  to  cx>nsist  of  80,000  words, 
for  the  sum  of  £200,  of  which  £160  was  subsequently  paid. 
But  when  the  words  of  the  story  came  to  be  counted  it 
was  found  to  consist  of  not  more  than  about  70,000.  That 
being  so,  the  plaintiffs  refused  to  pay  the  remaining  ;f40, 
the  balance  of  the  purchase-money.  Whereupon  the 
author  treated  the  contract  as  void,  and  sold  the  volume 
rights  of  the  story  to  the  defendant.  The  plaintiffs,  who 
were  duly  registered  as  proprietors  of  copyright,  claimed 
to  be  the  owners  of  the  complete  copyright  in  the  book. 
The  main  defence  was  that  the  plaintiffs,  as  publishers, 
employed  the  author  to  compose  the  story  and  that  the 
purchase  price  of  £200  had  not  been  paid. 

The  Judge  Was  so  impressed  with  the  importance  and 
difficulty  of  the  case  that  he  adjourned  it  for  further 
argument.  He  thought  that  an  agreement  to  assign 
copyright,  if  in  writing,  might  be  an  assignment  of  copy- 
right. The  difficulty  was  to  say  whether  it  was  in  itself 
necessarily  an  assignment.  Section  18  of  the  Act  of  1842 
contemplated  the  employment  of  some  author.  What 
was  the  meaning  of  ''employment*'?  There  was  no 
authority  on  the  point.  Some  meaning  must  be  given 
to  it,  and  it  must  be  distinguished  from  assignment.  It 
seemed  quite  possible  to  regard  the  contract  in  the  case  as 
an  emplojmient.  Could  an  agreement  to  assign  something 
not  in  existence  be  an  "  employment  '7  That  was  the  great 
difficulty  of  the  section  as  regarded  this  case.  But  the 
judge  subsequently  decided  that  the  agreement  was  a  good 
assignment  of  copyright,  and  the  fact  that  the  plaintiffs 
had  employed  the  author  to  compose  the  book  did  not 
bring  the  case  within  Section  18.  He  pointed  out,  however, 
that  Section  18,  though  it  seemed  at  first  sight  only  to 
apply  to  periodicals,  said  "  any  book,'*  and  might  apply 
to  a  single  volume  where  the  parties  so  intended. 

Conditions  for  Acquiring  Copyright 

The  book  must  be  published — 

1.  In  the  United  Kingdom. 

2.  The  book  must  not  have  been  previously  published. 

It  may  be  published  contemporaneously — d.g.,  in 
the  United  States. 


PART  II.]  LAW  AND  LETTERS  347 

Territorial  Extent 

The  right  extends  to  the  United  Kingdom  and  to  every 
part  of  the  British  dominions. 

Presentation  to  Libraries 

One  complete  copy  of  every  edition  of  every  book 
pubhshed  (unless  a  new  edition  is  a  mere  reissue  without 
change)  must  be  delivered  gratuitously  by  the  publisher 
(i)  to  the  British  Museum  without  demand ;  and  on  de- 
mand in  writing  to  (2)  the  Bodleian  Library,  Oxford ; 
(3)  the  Cambridge  University  Library  ;  (4)  the  Advocates' 
Library,  Edinburgh  ;  and  (5)  the  Library  of  Trinity  College, 
Dublin.     Default  exposes  the  publisher  to  a  £5  penalty. 

Registration  of  Books 

The  law  provides  for  the  keeping  at  Stationers*  Hall  of 
a  register  wherein  may  be  registered  the  proprietorship  in 
the  copyright  of  books  and  assignments  thereof  and 
licences  affecting  such  copyright,  and  for  inspection  by 
the  pubhc,  and  the  issue  of  stamped  and  certified  copies 
of  entries  which  can  be  given  in  evidence,  and  will  be 
frimd  facie  proof  of  the  proprietorship  or  assignment  of 
copyright  or  licence;  and,  in  order  to  protect  his  right, 
the  owner  of  the  copyright  must  register  in  the  prescribed 
form  (fee  five  shilhngs). 

No  proceedings  can  be  taken  before  registration,  but 
after  registration  all  infringements  may  be  sued  for,  whether 
they  took  place  before  or  after  registration,  unless  the 
action  is  barred  by  time. 

As  regards  encyclopaedias  and  periodicals  or  serial  pro- 
ductions (including  newspapers),  the  date  of  publication 
of  the  first  volume,  number,  or  part  must  be  registered. 

Assignment  of  Right 

« 

A  registered  owner  may  assign  his  interest,  or  any  portion 
of  his  interest,  by  entry  on  the  register.  An  assignment 
made  otherwise  must  be  in  writing. 


348  THE  LITERARY  YEAR-BOOK      [part  ii. 

A  recent  case  of  Jttde  v.  Reid  may  be  mentioned  as  a 
warning  to  parties  to  have  it  clear  on  the  face  of  the  docu- 
ment whether  an  assignment  is  intended  or  not.  Under 
the  agreement  there  the  author,  Mr.  Jude,  gave  Mr. 
Newsam,  managing  director  of  the  defendants,  who  were 
publishers,  **  the  sole  and  exclusive  right  "  to  print  and 
publish  the  work  on  three  conditions.  The  first  was  not 
inconsistent  with  an  assignment  of  the  copyright ;  the 
second  was  also  consistent  with  an  assignment,  but  was 
equally  consistent  with  there  being  no  assignment ;  and 
the  third  was  to  supply  Jude  with  such  copies  as  he  might 
require.  Now,  was  that  a  mere  publisher  and  author 
agreement,  or  was  it  an  assignment  ?  The  conclusion 
was  that  the  words  pointed  to  an  author  and  publisher 
agreement. 

Infringements  of  Copyright 
These  are  committed — 

1.  By  publication  in  the  United  Kingdom  of  an  un- 
authorized edition,  or  importation  for  sale  of  foreign 
reprints,  of  a  copyright  work — ix,^  literary  piracy, 

2.  By  the  illegitimate  appropriation  of  another's  literary 
labour — *.^.,  literary  larceny. 


Remedies  for  Infringement 
The  statute  law  gives  an  action  for  damages  for — 

1.  Unlawful  printing. 

2.  Importing  unlawfully  printed  books  for  sale  or  hire. 

3.  Knowingly  selling,  or  pubUshing,  or  exposing  for  sale 
or  hire,  or  possessing  for  sale  or  hire,  unlawfully  printed  or 
imported  books. 

It  also  provides  for  the  seizure  of  imlawfully  imported 
books  by  the  Customs,  and  for  offenders  being  fined  upon 
conviction. 

It  gives  the  property  in  pirated  copies  to  the  registered 
proprietor  of  the  copyright. 

He  may  also  sue  for  an  injunction  to  restrain  infringe- 
ment of  his  copyright. 


PART  II.]  LAW  AND  LETTERS  349 


Limitations  of  Actions 

Any  such  actions  as  above  must  be  brought  within 
twelve  months  after  the  offence. 


Other  Remedies 

The  common  law  treats  sale  of  a  work  under  the  name 
and  title  of  another  man  or  another  man's  work,  so  as  to 
deceive  the  pubUc,  as  a  fraud,  and  the  offender  is  liable 
to  an  action,  and  may  be  restrained  by  injunction.  There 
is  no  special  period  of  hmitation  of  such  action. 


Infringement  and  Fair  Use 

Fair  use  may  be  made  even  of  copyright  works  of  other 
authors  in  the  composition  or  compilation  of  new  books, 
but  the  new  work  must  be  substantially  the  result  of 
independent  labour  and  research.  Thus  the  text  for  a 
new  edition  of  old  MSS.  in  the  British  Museum  must  not 
be  copied  from  an  existing  copyright  edition,  but  from  the 
MSS.  {Parry  v.  Moring,  the  case  of  the  Letters  of  Dorothy, 
Osborne).  Dramatization  of  novels  is  not  an  infringe- 
ment of  copyright,  but  copies  of  the  words  of  the  play  may 
infringe  the  copyright  in  the  novel  (Warne  v.  Seebohm, 
the  case  of  **Little  Lord  Fauntleroy*'),  and  the  lawrequires 
a  copy  of  every  play  produced  to  be  sent  to  the  Lord  Cham- 
berlain. An  abridgement  may  or  may  not  be  an  infringe- 
ment of  copyright,  accoarding  to  the  use  made  of  the  original 
work. 

Immoral  Books 

There  is  no  copyright  in  matter  which  is— 

1.  Immoral. 

2.  Irreligious. 

3.  Seditious  or  libellous. 

4.  Fraudulent — i,e.y  professing  to  be  what  it  is  not  in 
such  a  manner  as  to  be  a  fraud  upon  purchasers. 


350  THE  LITERARY  YEAR-BOOK      [part  ii. 

II.  Copyright  in  Dramatic  Pieces 

This  cop\Tight  comprises  two  separate  rights,  the  first 
deahng  with  cop\Tight  in  the  author's  work  as  written 
matter,  and  the  second  with  the  copyright  in  such  work 
as  represented  on  the  stage  or  otherwise  to  the  public. 

{a)  Right  of  Publication  in  Print, 

The  law  is  the  same  as  that  of  copyright  in  books  (ante^ 

q.v.). 

(b)  Right  of  Public  Performance 

The  exclusive  right  of  public  performance  of  dramatic 
pieces,  sometimes  called  playright,  endures  for  forty-two 
years  from  the  first  public  representation,  or  for  the  life 
of  the  author  and  seven  years  after  his  death,  whichever 
is  the  longer.  Such  representation  must  be  given  in  the 
United  Kingdom. 

Registration 

Proprietorship  may  be  registered  at  Stationers'  Hall,  but 
registration  is  not  necessary  in  order  to  take  proceedings 
for  infringement,  this  registration  being  permissive  only, 
and  not  compulsory. 

It  seems  that  the  effect  of  registering  a  statement  in- 
correct in  material  particulars  is  merely  to  vitiate  the 
registration,  and  prevent  its  being  accepted  as  primA 
facie  evidence  of  title  {Hardacre  v.  Armstrong). 

Assignment  or  License 

Writing  of  the  author  or  his  agent  is  necessary.  An 
ji;s^if<nment  of  copyright  in  the  printed  book  does  not 
iniii^it'S  the  right  of  performance,  unless  such  assignment 
jb  j^tf^ihtercd  with  a  statement  of  the  intention  to  do  so, 
or  unl^'.ss  the  right  is  clearly  conveyed  by  an  unregistered 
afi«iignrnent. 

Remedies  for  Infringement 

rary  unauthorized  representation  of  a  dramatic  piece 
Jie  offender  to  a  statutory  penalty  of  fcMty  shiUings

or  full  damages,  and  to  an  action  for  an  injunction  to 
restrain  the  infringement  of  right. 

Limitation  of  Actions 

I    All  actions  or  proceedings  must  be  brought  within  twelve 
months  after  the  offence. 

III.  Copyright  in  Musical  Compositions 

This  copyright  is  analogous  to  the  copyright  in  dramatic 
pieces,  and  in  like  manner  comprises — 

.   5-  Right  of  pubUcation  in  print. 
2.  Right  of  public  performance. 

I.  Right  of  Publication  in  Print 

The  law  is  the  same  as  that  of  cop3n:ight  in  books 
{antCy  q.v.),  except  in  the  following  particulars :  It  has 
been  decided  that  perforated  music  sheets  for  use  in  a 
mechanical  piano  or  organ  are  not  copies  of  music,  and 
copyright  gives  no  protection  against  the  reproduction 
of  music  in  this  manner  (Boosey  v.  Whight,  music  for  an 
Moliaii  organ). 

In  addition  to  the  same  remedies  for  infringement  of 
copyright  as  in  the  case  of  books,  in  the  case  of  music 
under  the  statute  2  Edward  Vlt.,  c.  15,  a  constable  was 
empowered,  by  order  of  a  magistrate  or  on  the  written 
request  of  the  owner  of  the  copyright,  to  seize  pirated 
copies,  and  the  magistrate  may  order  their  destruction  or 
delivery  up.  Technical  difficulties  as  to  applying  this 
statute  arose — ^mainly  owing  to  the  decision  of  the  Divi- 
sional Court  that  a  summons  must  be  first  served  on  the 
hawker  or  other  offender  (ex  parte  Francis).  An  Act  with 
itiore  stringent  provisions  received  the  Royal  Assent  on 
August  4  last,  by  which  "  every  person  who  prints,  repro- 
duces, or  sells,  or  exposes,  offers,  or  has  in  his  possession 
for  sale  any  priated  copies  of  music,  or  has  in  his  possession 
plates  for  reproducing  pirated  copies  of  music,  shall  (unless 
he  proves  that  he  acted  innocently)  be  guilty  of  an  offence  *' 
punishable  summarily,  and  he  mil  be  liable  to  be  fined 


352  THE  LITERARY  YEAR-BOOK      [part  ii. 

£5,  and  on  a  second  or  subsequent  conviction  to  be  im- 
prisoned or  fined  £10.  It  is  also  enacted  that  any  con- 
stable may  take  into  custody  without  warrant  any  person 
who  sells,  exposes,  offers,  or  has  in  his  possession  for  sale 
any  pirated  copies  of  any  such  music  as  may  be  specified 
in  a  writing  addressed  to  the  chief  of  police,  and  signed 
by  the  apparent  owner  of  the  copyright  or  his  agent, 
requesting  the  arrest,  at  the  risk  of  such  owner,  of  all 
offenders  against  the  Act.  And  a  search  warrant  may  be 
granted  in  the  case  of  suspected  premises. 

2.  Right  of  Public  Performance 

The  law  is  the  same  as  that  of  playright  (see  ante), 
except  in  the  following  particulars  :  A  note  of  the  reser- 
vation of  the  right  of  public  performance  must  be  printed 
on  the  title-page  of  every  copy  of  a  musical  compo- 
sition, in  order  to  preserve  the  exclusive  right  of  public 
performance. 

Instead  of  the  penalty  of  forty  shillings  for  every  un- 
authorized performance,  the  penalty  and  the  costs  are  in 
the  discretion  of  the  court. 

IV.  Copyright  in  Lectures 

(a)  By  the  Lectures  Copyright  Act,  1835,  ^he  author  or 
his  assign  has  the  sole  right  of  printing  and  publishing  a 
lecture,  notwithstanding  delivery.  This  copyright  endures 
for  the  longer  of  the  two  periods  of  twenty-eight  years 
and  the  life  of  the  author. 

J..'; 

Conditions  for  Acquiring  Copyright 

Notice  in  writing  of  the  intended  lecture  must  be  given 
to  two  justices  two  days  before  delivering  it.  The  statute 
does  not  apply  to  a  lecture  delivered  in  a  University,  or 
pubhc  school  or  college,  or  on  any  public  foundation,  or 
under  any  gift  or  endowment. 

Remedies  for  Infringement 

Action  under  the  statute  for  penalties  and  forfeiture  for 
unlawful  printing,  and  for  knowingly  seUing  or  puljlishing, 
or  exposing  for  sale,  unlawfully  printed  lectures.      ^ 


PART  II.]  LAW  AND  LETTERS  353 

(b)  By  the  Copyright  Act,  1842,  a  lecture  will  be  pro- 
tected as  a  **  book  **  (see  ante)  if  it  is  first  published  in  print. 

Publication 

Delivery  of  a  lecture  for  the  instruction  or  amusement 
of  a  particular  audience  is  not  true  publication,  so  as  to 
make  it  pubHc  property ;  it  is  only  "  private  pubUcation/' 
and  the  author  retains  his  right  in  it  as  unpublished  matter. 

V.  Copyright  in  Engravings  and  the  Like 

This  copyright  endures  for  twenty-eight  years  from  the 
first  pubUshing.  The  subject-matter  includes  any  print 
invented  or  designed,  graved,  etched,  or  worked  in  mezzo- 
tints or  chiaroscuro,  or  hthographed  by  the  author,  or 
reproduced  at  his  instance  by  any  of  such  methods  from 
the  author's  work,  design,  or  invention,  or  from  any 
picture,  drawing,  model,  or  sculpture. 

Conditions  for  Acquiring  Copyright 

The  print  must  be  engraved  in  Great  Britain,  but  the 
engraver  need  not  be  British. 

The  day  of  first  publication,  with  the  name  of  the  pro- 
prietor, must  be  engraved  on  each  plate,  and  printed  on 
every  print. 

No  registration  is  required. 

Licensed  Reproductions 

The  written  consent  of  the  proprietor,  attested  by  two 
witnesses,  is  necessary. 

Remedies  for  Infringement 

Action  for  damages  in  respect  of  copying  and  seUing 
without  the  proper  consent,  or  printing,  reprinting,  or 
importing  for  sale,  any  copyright  print,  or  knowingly 
publishing  or  exposing  for  sale  imlawfully  printed  copies, 
or  causing  others  to  do  any  of  these  things. 

Action  for  Penalties. — Five  shilUngs  for  every  print  in 
the  offender's  possession,  and  forfeiture  of  the  plate  and 
all  prints. 

Action  for  injunction  to  restrain  infringement. 

12 


354  THE  LITERARY  YEAR-BOOK      [part  ii. 

Limitation  of  Actions 

The  penalty  must  be  sued  for  within  six  months  after 
the  offence,  and  three  months  after  discovering  it,  and  is 
recoverable  by  summary  proceedings. 

VI.  Copyright  in  Paintings,  Drawings,  and  Photo- 
graphs— Fine  Arts  Copyright 

This  copyright  endures  for  the  life  of  the  artist,  and 
seven  years  after  his  death. 

The  subject-matter  includes  original  paintings  and  draw- 
ings and  the  designs  of  such,  and  original  photographs  and 
the  negatives. 

Conditions  for  Acquiring  Copyright 

The  author  must  be  a  British  subject  or  resident  within 
the  British  dominions. 

If  any  such  work  is  (i)  sold,  or  (2)  executed  "  for  or  on 
behalf  of  any  other  person  for  a  good  or  a  valuable  con- 
sideration,'* the  author  will  not  retain  the  copjright, 
unless  it  be  expressly  reserved  to  him  by  agreement  in 
writing,  but  the  cop3n"ight  will  belong  in  the  first  case  to 
the  purchaser,  and  in  the  second  case  to  the  person  for 
whom  the  work  was  executed,  with  this  qualification  in 
the  case  of  a  purchaser — that  an  agreement  in  writing 
that  he  shall  have  the  copyright  is  necessary. 

Accordingly,  where  a  photograph  is  taken  at  the  request 
of  the  sitter  upon  the  terms  that  the  sitter  will  pay  for 
taking  it,  or  in  circumstances  which  raise  an  implied 
promise  on  his  part  to  pay,  the  cop5n:ight  in  the  photo- 
graph belongs  to  the  sitter,  though  the  negative  remains 
the  property  of  the  photographer  {Boucas  v.  Cooke,  the 
boy  preacher's  case). 

It  has  been  recently  held  that  photographers  who  were 
at  their  own  request  allowed  by  the  proprietor  of  a  school 
to  take  photographs  of  the  school  at  their  own  risk,  on  the 
chance  of  selling  copies  to  the  proprietor,  must  be  deemed 
to  have  taken  the  photographs  for  or  on  behalf  of  the  pro- 
prietor **  for  a  good  consideration,"  and  that  the  copy* 
right  therefore  belonged  to  him  (Stackemann  v.  Paton). 


PART  II.]  LAW  AND  LETTERS  355 

Territorial  Extent 

This  copyright  extends  to  the  whole  of  the  United 
Kingdom,  but  not  to  any  part  of  the  British  dominions 
outside  {Graves  v.  Gorrie,  unlicensed  reproduction  in 
Canada  of  copies  of  an  English  cop3n:ight  picture). 

Registration 

The  Fine  Arts  Copyright  Act,  1862,  provides  for  the 
keeping  of  a  register  at  Stationers'  Hall,  and  prescribes 
registration  of  tihie  proprietor  of  the  copyright  and  other 
particulars.  The  provisions  as  to  inspection,  etc.,  are  the 
same  as  for  the  register  of  books  {ante,  q.v,).  No  action 
will  lie  for  any  infringement  or  offence  committed  before 
registration. 

Assignment  of  Right 

Assignments  and  licenses  must  be  in  writing.  Assign- 
ments have  to  be  registered. 

Remedies  for  Infringement 

Action  for  penalties — up  to  £10  for  every  offence,  with 
forfeiture  of  piratical  copies  and  negatives — in  respect  of 
repeating  or  copying  for  sale,  hire,  or  exhibition  and 
distribution,  or  knowingly  importing,  selling,  publishing 
unlawful  copies,  or  causing  any  such  thing  to  be  done. 

Action  for  damages,  with  forfeiture  of  piratical  copies, 
m  respect  of  the  same  matters. 

Action  for  injunction  to  restrain  future  infringements. 
Penalties  and  forfeitures  may  be  enforced  by  action  or 
summarily. 

Limitation  of  Actions 
There  is  no  special  limitation. 

vn.  Copyright  in  Sculptures 

This  copyright  endures  for  fourteen  years  from  the  first 
putting  forth  or  pubhshing  the  sculpture,  with  a  further 
fourteen  years  if  the  sculptor  shall  be  living  at  the  end
