Aathors*  Assoeiatioiiy  The.  1902.  Objects — ^To  assist  and  advise 
and  protect  the  beginner  in  literature.  To  criticise  and,  if 
necessary  and  desirable,  revise  MSS.  ;  and  to  give  competent 
expert  advice  upon  the  advisability  or  otherwise  of  adopting 
a  Uterary  life.  To  give  legal  advice  on  questions  within  the 
scope  of  the  Association,  and  to  afford  members  the  protection 
and  advantages  of  a  powerful  and  responsible  organization  in 
touch  with  the  higher,  as  well  as  the  lower,  branches  of  the 
profession  of  letters.  To  act  as  general  Uterary  agents,  and 
to  arrange  with  pubUshers  and  editors  for  the  pubUcation  of 
the  work  of  members  when  suitable  and  desirable.  To  advise 
upon  publication  of  MSS..  and  suitable  magazines,  periodicals, 
or  publishing  houses.  To  direct  the  efforts  of  the  inexperienced 
writer  into  remunerative  channels — ^Uterary  or  otherwise.  To 
make  contracts  with  editors  and  publishers  on  behalf  of 
members,  and  to  watch  and  protect  the  interests  of  members. 
To  promote  whatever  may  tend  to  the  furtherance  of  the 
interests  of  the  members,  the  improvements  of  their  claims  to 
recognition,  and  the  unification  of  the  literary  profession.  To 
bring  unknown  authors  before  the  notice  of  editors  and  pub- 
lishers. To  advise  upon  and  (in  particular  cases)  arrange  for 
publication  of  musical  compositions,  and  book  and  magazine 
work.  And  to  use  the  funds  of  the  Society  for  the  furtherance 
of  all  or  any  of  the  above  objects,  as  maybe  deemed  desirable. 
The  Association  does  not  enter  into  competition  with  any 
existing  literary  society.  Subscription — One  guinea  for  the 
first  year ;  subsequent  years  half  a  guinea.  General  Secretary — 
Galloway  Kyle,  F.R.S.L.,  Darlington. 

Aathors,  Ineorporatod  Soeiety  of»  39  Old  Queen  Street,  Storey^s 
Gate,  s.w.  Founded  in  1883  by  Sir  Walter  Besant,  Sir 
Frederick  Pollock,  and  other  well-known  writers.  Incorporated 
in  1884  under  the  Presidency  of  Lord  Tennjrson.  The  present 
President  is  Mr.  George  Meredith.  Its  main  objects  are  the 
maintenance,  definition,  and  defence  of  literary,  dramatic,  and 
musical  property,  the  consolidation  of  the  laws  of  Domestic 
Copyright,  and  the  promotion  of  International  Copyright.  The 
memb^hip  is  about  1,600,  and  continues  to  increase.  The 
general  work  of  the  Society,  with  much  information  on  technical 
subjects,  will  be  found  in  the  pages  of  The  Author,  published 
monthly,  and  now  in  its  fifteenth  volume.  (No  issue  in 
August  and  September.)  Full  details  of  its  works  are  con- 
tained in  the  Prospectus  issued  from  the  Society's  offices. 
The  Society  issues  also  from  time  to  time  pamphlets  bearing  on 
literary  production.  Among  these  may  be  mentioned  "  The 
Methods  of  Publishing."  "  The  Cost  of  Production  "  (now  out 
of    print),  "  Forms  of  Agreement  issued    by  the  Publishers'

Association,  with  Comments  by  G.  Herbert  Thring,"  "  The 
Addenda  to  the  Methods  of  Publishing,"  and  a  few  others. 
All  information  with  regard  to  the  standing  of  the  different 
publishing  houses,  contracts  for  the  publication  of  books. 
serial  issue  in  magazines,  dramatic  productions,  and  the 
marketing  of  musical  compositions,  can  be  obtained  from 
the  Secretary.  Members  are  also  entitled  to  the  opinion  of 
the  Society's  SoUcitor  in  addition  to  that  of  the  Secretary, 
who  is  himself  a  SoUcitor.  The  rights  of  authors  are  supported 
in  the  Courts  of  Law  at  the  expense  of  the  Society,  subject  to 
the  sanction  of  the  Committee,  and  counsel's  opinion  is  obtained 
(with  the  Committee's  consent)  without  any  extra  charge  to 
the  member.  These  are  a  few  of  the  many  matters  that  it 
takes  into  consideration.  One  of  the  most  interesting  develop- 
ments is  that  of  a  Pension  Scheme  for  the  benefit  of  authors 
who  are  members  of  the  Society.  The  Pension  Fund  Com- 
mittee liave  already  assigned  two  pensions,  and  hope  to  grant 
another  shortly.  The  names  on  the  Council  include  many  of 
those  best  known  in  the  various  branches  of  literature.  The 
Society  is  managed  by  a  special  Committee,  with  Sub-Com- 
mittees on  Art,  Copyright,  and  the  Drama.  Subscription — 
One  guinea  per  annum,  or  ten  guineas  for  life-membersMp. 
Secretary — Mr.  G.  Herbert  Thring,  SoUcitor  in  England  for  La 
Soci6t6  des  Gens  de  Lettres.

The  by-laws  of  the  Academy  were  allowed  by  an  order  of  the 
Privy  Council,  dated  5  th  February,  1903.  By  these  by-laws  the 
number  of  ordinary  Fellows  is  hmited  to  one  hundred.  The 
Academy  has  also  been  admitted  as  a  constituent  member  of  the 
International  Association  of  Academies  in  the  philosophico- 
historic  section.  The  temporary  postal  address  of  the  Academy  is 
The  Society  of  Antiquaries  of  London,  BurUngton  House,  w. 

British  Association  for  the  Advancement  of  Selenee,  Burlington 
House.  1 83 1 .  Objects — To  give  a  stronger  impulse  and  a  more 
systematic  direction  to  scientific  inquiry  ;  to  promote  the  inter- 
course of  those  who  cultivate  science  in  different  parts  of  the 
British  Empire  with  one  another  and  with  foreign  philosophers  ; 
and  to  obtain  a  more  general  attention  to  the  objects  of  science, 
and  a  removal  of  any  disadvantages  of  a  pubUc  kind  which 
impede  its  progress.  Meetings — One  week  in  each  year ;  in 
1906  at  York.  In  1905  the  Association  met  in  South  Africa. 
Publication  —  Annual  Report.  President  (1906) — Prof.  Ray 
Lankester,  F.R.S.  General  Secretaries — ^Major  P.  A.  Mac- 
Mahon,  R.A.,  F.R.S.,  and  Prof.  W.  A.  Herdman,  F.R.S. 
Assistant  Secretary — A.  Silva  White,  Burlington  House,  w. 

British  Child-Study  Association.  1894.  Objects— To  interest 
parents,  teachers,  and  others  in  the  systematic  observation  of 
children  and  young  people  with  a  view  to  gaining  greater 
insight  into  child-nature,  and  securing  more  sympathetic  and 
scientific  methods  in  training  the  young.  Publication  —  The 
Paidologist,  three  times  annually.  Annual  Subscription — Three 
shilUngs.  Hon.  General  Secretary — Miss  Mary  Louch,  Colbury 
House,  Totton,  Hants.  Hon.  Secretary  (London  Branch) — 
Miss  Kate  Stevens,  Carhsle  House,  Dartmouth  Park  Hill,  N,w. 


PART  II.]  SOCIETIES  537 

British  Record  Society,  Limited.  1886.  Object— The  publication 
of  Records  from  the  Public  Record  Ofl6ce  and  Calendars  of 
Wills  from  the  various  Probate  Registries  relating  to  Great 
Britain.  Yearly  subscription — £1  is.  Hon.  Secretary,  E.  A. 
Fry,  124  Chancery  Lane,  w.c. 
Britisli  School  at  Athens. — This  School  (founded  in  1886)  gives 
to  British  students  of  Greek  archaeology  and  art  the  oppor- 
tunity of  pursuing  their  researches  in  Greece  itself,  with  com- 
mand of  the  means  which  the  recent  great  advances  of  the 
science  have  rendered  indispensable. 

Athens  is  every  year  becoming  more  and  more  the  centre  of 
the  archaeological  world.  The  architecture  of  Greece  can 
nowhere  else  be  studied  to  such  advantage  ;  and  the  concentra- 
tion in  the  Athenian  museums  of  the  numerous  and  most 
important  discoveries  which  have  taken  place  on  Greek  soil  in 
the  last  few  years  has  made  a  personal  knowledge  of  them 
indispensable  to  a  proper  training  ;  in  particular,  they  may 
almost  be  said  to  hold  a  monopoly  of  the  materials  for  the 
investigation  of  prehistoric  and  early  archaic  art. 

The  student  requires  two  auxiUaries  when  working  in 
Athens — firstly,  the  command  of  an  adequate  library ;  and 
secondly,  the  advice  of  a  trained  archaeologist,  residing  on  the 
spot,  and  following  the  rapid  advances  of  the  science,  due 
partly  to  new  discovery  and  partly  to  the  rearrangement  of 
old  materials. 

These  advantages  are  now  provided  for  French,  German, 
Austrian,  American,  and  English  archaeologists,  through  the 
schools  which  they  have  estabUshed.  By  the  recent  addition 
of  the  Penrose  Memorial  Library,  the  British  School  is  now  in 
point  of  equipment  one  of  the  best  in  Athens.  It  is  also  by 
means  of  these  schools  that  most  excavations  on  Greek  soil 
have  been  carried  out ;  and  those  conducted  in  Cyprus,  in 
the  Peloponnese,  in  Melos,  and  in  Crete  by  the  British  School 
are  an  encouraging  proof  of  the  work  that  may  be  done  in  the 
future  if  the  School  is  adequately  supported. 

Any  persons  who  bring  satisfactory  testimonials  of  their 
quaUfications  are  admitted  as  students  free  of  charge.  The 
principal  conditions  imposed  are  that  they  shall  pursue  some 
definite  course  of  Hellenic  study  or  research,  residing  for  the 
purpose  not  less  than  three  months  in  Greek  lands,  and  that 
they  shall  at  the  end  of  the  session  write  a  report  of  the  work 
wliich  they  have  done.  AppUcations  from  intending  students 
should  be  made  to  the  Secretary,  John  ff.  Bakbr-Penoyre, 
Esq.,  M.A.,  22  Albemarle  Street,  W.,  who  will  also  be  happy  to 
supply  any  further  information. 

Donations  or  annual  subscriptions  to  the  School  are  greatly 
needed,  and  will  be  gladly  received  and  acknowledged  by  the 
Hon.  Treasurer,  Walter  Leaf,  Esq.,  D.Litt.,  6  Sussex  Place. 
Regent's  Park,  n.w. 
Bronte  Society,  The.  Founded  1893.  Incorporated  1902.  The 
membership  of  the  Society  now  (1906)  exceeds  350.  scattered 


538  THE  LITERARY  YEAR-BOOK      [part  ii. 

over  all  parts  of  Great  Britain  and  America.  Fifteen  Parts 
of  the  Society's  Transactions,  and  three  supplementary  ones,

Chemistry,  Institute  of,  of  Great  Britain  and  Ireland,  30  Bloomsbury 
Square,  w.c.  Founded  1877.  Incorporated  by  Royal  Charter, 
June  13,  1885.  Objects — ^To  promote  the  better  education  of 
persons  desirous  of  qualifying  themselves  to  be  professional 
consulting  analytical  and  technological  chemists  and  public 
analysts ;  to  examine  candidates,  and  to  grant  certificates 
of  competency  ;  and  to  elevate  the  profession  of  chemistry  by 
setting  up  a  high  standard  of  scientific  and  practical  pro- 
ficiency, and  by  requiring  on  the  part  of  the  members  the 
observance  of  strict  rules  in  regard  to  professional  conduct. 
Associateship — No  person  is  qusdified  to  become  an  Associate 
of  the  Institute  until  he  has  attained  the  age  of  twenty-one 
years,  and  unless  he  has  passed  a  Preliminary  Examination  in 
subjects  of  general  education,  has  completed  a  course  of  at 
least  three  years'  study  in  Theoretical  and  Practical  Chem- 
istry, Physics,  Mathematics,  and  one  other  subject  to  be 
chosen  from  the  list  given  in  the  Book  of  Regulations,  and 
has  passed  the  Intermediate  and  Final  Examinations  of 
the  bistitute.  Fellowship — In  order  to  become  eligible  for 
the  Fellowship  an  Associate  is  required  to  have  been  con- 
tinuously engaged  for  a  further  term  of  three  years  in  the 
study  and  practice  of  Chemistry  in  a  manner  satisfactory  to 
the  CounciL  Students — Candidates  applying  for  i-egistration 
as  Students  must  pass  an  approved  preliminary  examinatioD, 
and  must  be  not  less  than  seventeen  years  of  age.  Publica- 
tions—  Proceedings  (three  issues  annusilly),  gratis  to  Fellows, 
Associates,  and  Students  ;  The  Register  of  Fellows,  Associates, 
and  Students  (gratis) ;  The  Regulations  for  Admission  of 
Students,  Associates,  ani  Fellows,  with  Past  Examination 
Papers,  one  shilling  (post  free,  is.  id.) ;  A  List  of  Official 
Chemical  Appointments  held  in  Great  Britain  and  Ireland,  in 
India  and  the  Colonies,  two  shillings  net.  (post  free,  2s.  3d.).

Diekens  Fellowship,  The.  Founded  1902,  to  encourage  good  feeling 
on  Dickensian  principles  among  the  members,  and  to  take  such 
measures  as  may  be  expedient  to  remedy  or  ameliorate  those 
social  evils  which  would  have  appealed  to  the  heart  of  Charles 
Dickens.  There  are  over  five  thousand  members,  who  pay  a 
minimum  subscription  of  one  shilling  a  year.  Branches  are 
established  in  chief  towns  of  the  United  Kingdom,  America, 
and  the  Colonies.  The  Fellowship  has  its  own  organ — a 
monthly  magazine,  entitled  The  Dickensian,  edited  by  B.  W. 
Matz.  President — Mr.  Henry  F.  Dickens,  K.C.  Hon.  Gen.

542  THE  LITERARY  YEAR-BOOK      [part  ii. 

physical  sciences  generally  and  archaeology,  especially  the 
natural  history  of  the  county  of  Dorset  and  its  antiquities, 
prehistoric  records,  and  ethnology.  Its  influence  is  used  to 
prevent,  as  far  as  possible,  the  extirpation  of  rare  plants  and 
animals,  and  to  promote  the  preservation  of  the  antiquities 
of  the  county,  it  aims  at  effecting  its  objects  by  a  series  of 
summer  meetings  held  in  different  locaUties  each  year,  and  by 
a  series  of  winter  meetings  held  at  the  Dorset  County  Museum. 
An  illustrated  volume  of  the  dab's  Proceedings  is  issued  annually.

Early  English  Text  Society.  1864.  Object — To  bring  the  mass  of 
Old  EngUsh  Literature  within  the  reach  of  the  ordinary  student,

rested,  of  having  felt  httle  interest  in  the  monuments  of  her

Original  Sories  the  whole  of  our  unpnnted  MS.  literature,  and 
in  its  Extra  Series  to  reprint  in  careful  editions  all  that  is  most 
valuable  of  printed  MSS.  and  early  printed  books.  It  greatly 
needs  more  support.  The  Society  has  issued  to  its  subscribers

English  Goethe  Sodety.  Objects — ^The  English  Goethe  Society  was 
founded  on  February  26,  1886,  for  the  purpose  of  promoting 
the  study  of  Goethe's  work  and  thought ;  and  in  189 1  its  scope 
was  extended,  so  that,  while  always  keeping  Goethe  as  the 
central  figure,  the  attention  of  the  members  might  also  be 
directed  to  other  fields  of  German  literature,  art,  and  science. 
The  Society  pursues  its  aims  by  means  of  meetings,  discussions, 
the  publication  of  transactions,  and  in  any  other  mode  which 
may  from  time  to  time  seem  advisable  to  the  governing  body. 
Meetings — Five  each  year.  Publications — Transactions,  and 
other  original  papers.  Subscription — Half  a  guinea  per  annum. 
One  guinea  per  annum  includes  membership  of  the  German 
Goethe  Society  at  Weimar.  The  Society  is  flourishing,  but 
would  welcome  new  members.  Secretary — Dr.  Eugene  Oswald, 
49  Blomfield  Road,  Maida  Hill.  w. 

Entente  Cordiale,  L'  (Anglo-French  Association).  1897.  Objects — 
To  develop  and  maintain  friendly  social  and  commercial

Ethologleal  Society  for  the  Systematic  Study  of  Haman  GharaAtor. 
Founded  1 904.  The  ob j  ect  of  the  Society  is  the  study  of  Human 
Nature,  not  tlirough  any  one  department  of  science,  but  taking 
from  all  its  different  branches  the  most  practical  and  useful  to 
arrive  at  a  knowledge  of  the  intellect  and  character  of  man,  and 
the  laws  which  govern  their  manifestation  ;  and  not  limited  to 
any  one  method  of  research,  but  collecting  all  material  that  is 
available,  and  applying  it  to  the  preservation  of  the  mental 
health  of  the  individual,  to  education,  moral  reform,  and  the 
solution  of  various  sociological  problems.  Extract  from  Rules 
— **  The  Society  shall  hold  a  series  of  *  Conversazioni  *  from 
November  till  July,  to  which  eminent  authorities  shall  be 
invited  to  deliver  lectures  on  the  relation  of  their  branch  of 
science  or  philosophy  to  Human  Nature.  The  Annual  Sub- 
scription shall  be  for  Members  one  guinea,  and  for  Associates 
half  a  guinea.  A  Journal  shall  be  published  containing  the 
proceedings  of  the  Society  and  original  contributions."  Presi- 
dent— Bernard  Hollander,  Esq.,  M.D.  Treasurer — 
W.  Cranstoun  Todd,  Esq.  Hon,  Secretary — A.  Flindell 
Brady,  Esq.,  35 a  Welbeck  Street,  Cavendish  Square,  W. 

Fabian  Society.  Founded  1883  for  the  study  of  Socialism  and  its 
propaganda  mainly  by  educational  methods.  Membership — 
885,  chiefly  in  London  and  the  provinces,  is  confined  to 
Socialists,  but  associates  paying  los.  and  subscribers  of  5  s. 
receive  publications,  and  can  attend  the  meetings.  These, 
usually  lectures,  are  held  fortnightly  at  Essex  Hall,  Essex 
Street,  Strand,  except  in  the  holiday  season.  The  lectures  and 
publications  are  chiefly  devoted  to  the  consideration  of  political 
and  social  problems,  with  a  view  to  the  reconstruction  of 
society  on  Socialistic  lines.  The  Society "S  has  a  circulating 
library  of  over  4,000  books  on  Political  and][Economic  subjects, 
which  are  supplied  in  boxes  to  Trade  Unions,  Co-operative 
Societies,  and  other  organizations  at  a  charge  of '6s.  perTyear.

It  publishes  Fabian  Tracts  usually  at  id.,  which  explains  its

Read  on  SociiU  and  Economic  Subjects  (fourth  eaition),  1901, 
and  a  supplement.  More  Books  to  Read  (1906),  id.  Its  organ

Folk-Song  Soeiety. — This  Society  was  founded  in  1 898  for  the  pur-

and  discussion.  Nine  numbers  of  the  journal,  containing 
several  hundreds  of  songs  and  tunes,  copiously  annotated  by 
exj>erts,  never  before  printed,  have  appeared  (up  to  December,

Friends  of  Russian  Freedom,  Soeiety  of.  Founded  1889.  Objects — 
To  further  the  cause  of  political  and  religious  liberty  in  Russia 
through  the  awakening  of  English  public  opinion  on  the  subject, 
and  to  give  material  help  to  the  victims  of  political  and  religious 
intolerance  in  Russia,  as  well  as  assistance  to  Russian  workmen 
in  their  just  struggles  for  the  right  of  combination  and  the 
improvement  of  the  conditions  of  their  life.  The  Society 
pubUshes  Free  Russia,  a  monthly  journal ;  and  arranges 
lectures  to  treat  and  discuss  questions  affecting  Russian  liberty. 
Minimum  Annual  Subscription — 5  s.  Funds  are  urgently 
needed  ;  and  full  particulars  of  the  aims  of  the  Society  and 
the  important  work  it  has  already  done  may  be  obtained  on 
appUcation  to  Mr.  J.  Frederick  Green,  40  Outer  Temple, 
Strand,  w.c.

International  Musical  Soeiety.  1899.  Object — To  unite  in  one  body 
those  who  prosecute  musical  research,  in  whatever  country,  by 
means  of  (a)  an  organized  system  of  local  meetings  and  general 
conferences,  and  (6)  a  pubUshed  monthly  journal  (about  600  pp. 
yearly)  and  quarterly  magazine  (about  750  pp.  yearly).  The 
language  of  publications  is  alternately  German,  English, 
French,  or  Italian  ;  but  on  an  average  one-fourth  of  space  is 
given  to  EngUsh,  and  the  same  to  French.  The  Society  has 
thirty-five  organized  branches  in  America,  Austria,  Belgium, 
Denmark,  Finland,  France,  Germany,  Great  Britain  and 
Ireland,  Holland,  Italy,  Russia,  Spain,  Sweden,  and  Switzer- 
land. Membership — By  paying  a  subscription  (in  return  for 
which  the  journal  and  magazine  are  received  post  free)  of  £1  ; 
or,  if  in  combination  with,  and  addition  to,  the  (British) 
Musical  Association,  £1  us.  Publications — As  above;  also

Kyrle  Society.  This  Society  owes  its  origin  to  a  letter  written  in 
1876  by  Miss  Miranda  Hill,  calling  attention  to  the  dull,  common- 
place Uves  of  the  poor,  and  suggesting  that  means  taken  to 
enUven  and  beautify  those  Uves  would  be  labour  well  spent. 
Accordingly,  in  1877,  a  society  was  started  with  this  object, 
and  the  name  "  Kyrle  "  was  chosen,  the  idea  being  taken  xrom 
Pope's   lines   eulogizing   John    Kjnrle,    the    "  Man   of   Ross."

Objects — (a)  To  decorate  workmen's  clubs,  hospital  wards,  parish 
rooms,  or  any  room  used  for  social  gatherings,  without  dis- 
tinction of  creed,  (h)  To  secure  and  assist  in  securing  any  open 
spaces  in  or  near  the  metropolis,  and  to  prevent  spaces  being 
illegally  built  upon  ;  to  render  available  as  public  gardens 
disused  burial-grounds  and  other  waste  spaces,  and  to  provide 
seats,  plants,  &c.,  for  them,  (c)  To  distribute  cut  flowers, 
plants,  ferns,  and  bulbs  to  hospitals,  workhouses,  and  other 
institutions,  and  amongst  the  homes  of  the  poor,  {d)  To 
organize  a  voluntary  choir  of  singers  to  perform  oratorios  for 
the  poor,  {e)  To  distribute  books,  magazines,  and  periodicals 
to  hospitals,  infirmaries,  workhouses,  clubs,  and  libraries  for

Linnean  Society  of  London,  Burlington  House,  w.  This  Society 
was  founded  in  February  1788  by  Sir  James  Edward  Smith, 
who,  on  the  recommendation  of  Sir  Joseph  Banks,  purchased 
the  library,  manuscripts,  and  famous  natural  history  collections 
of  Linnaeus  from  his  son's  executrix,  by  whom  they  were  offered 
for  sale.  In  March,  1 802 ,  the  Society  was  incorporated  by  Royal 
Charter,  and  a  supplemental  charter  was  granted  in  1904 
for  the  admission  of  women.  The  meetings  were  held  first  at 
the  house  of  the  founder,  afterwards  in  Soho  Square,  and  even- 
tually at  Burlington  House,  whither  the  library  and  collections 
(with  the  exception  of  the  minerals,  which  were  sold  by  Sir 
J.  E.  Smith)  were  removed  in  1857,  and  where  they  are  still 
carefully  preserved.  The  centenary  anniversary  of  the  Society 
was  celebrated  on  the  24th  May,  1888,  when  a  Linnean  gold 
medal  was  struck  in  commemoration  of  the  event ;  this  is 
annually  awarded  at  the  anniversary  meeting,  alternately  to  a 
botanist  and  to  a  zoologist.  The  Society,  under  the  patronage 
of  His  ]\lajesty  the  King,  is  governed  by  a  President  and  Council, 
with  Treasurer  and  three  Secretaries,  annually  elected  at  the 
anniversary  meeting.  Fortnightly  evening  meetings  are  held 
throughout  the  session  from  November  to  June,  and  the 
botanical  and  zoological  papers  read  at  these  meetings  are 
subsequently  published  in  the  shape  of  quarto  Transactions  and 
octavo  Journals  extensively  illustrated.  General  Secretary — 
B.  Daydon  Jackson. 

Literary  Fund,  Royal,  40  Denison  House,  Westminster,  s.w.  1790. 
Object — The  assistance  of  necessitous  authors,  their  widows  and 
orphans,  or  mothers  or  sisters.  The  income  of  this  fund  is  about 
;^3,ooo.  The  number  of  grants  to  authors  and  relatives  of 
authors  since  the  foundation  of  the  fund  in  1790  totals  4,805, 
to  the  amount  of  ;^  146,972.  The  average  number  of  grants  in 
recent  years  has  been  about  forty,  the  amount  disbursed  in  each 
year  being  on  an  average  about  ;£2,6oo.  The  fund  holds  an 
anniversary  festival  each  year.  Subscription — One  guinea  per 
annum  or  Life  Donation  of  ten  guineas  paid  in  one  sum.  Secre- 
tary — A.  Llewelyn  Roberts,  B.A.

London  School  of  Eeonomies  and  Political  Seienee  (University  of 
London).  President — The  Right  Hon.  Lord  Rothschild. 
Chairman — Sidney  Webb.  Director — H.  J.  IkLs.CKiNDER.  The 
London  School  of  Economics  and  PoUtical  Science  was  founded 
in  1895  with  the  intention  of  providing  a-system  of  higher  educa- 
tion which  should  stand  in  the  same  relation  to  the  life  and 
calling  of  the  pubUc  administrator  and  the  merchant  as  do 
the  medical  schools  of  the  Universities  to  those  of  the  doctor. 
Under  the  new  constitution  of  the  University  of  London,  a 
"  Faculty  of  Economics  and  Political  Science  (including  Com- 
merce and  Industry)  "  was  estabUshed  in  1900,  and  the  School 
of  Economics  was  constituted  by  Act  of  Parliament  a  "  School 
of  the  University  "  in  that  Faculty.  Students  of  the  School 
who  have  matriculated  at  the  University  of  London  are  regis- 
tered as  internal  students  of  the  University,  and  may  pro- 
ceed to  degrees  in  Science  (B.Sc.  and  D.Sc.)  in  the  Faculty  of 
Economics.  All  the  lectures  and  classes  at  the  School  are,  how- 
ever, open  without  any  distinction  whatever  of  age,  or  sex,  or 
nationality  to  those  who  have  not  matriculated  and  do  not 
wish  to  pursue  a  full  University  course.  Special  instruction 
is  arranged  for  those  engaged  in  commerce,  banking,  insurance, 
accounting  and  railway  administration,  for  civil  and  municipal 
officials,  and  for  librarians.  Much  is  done  by  the  School  for 
the  encouragement  of  research  by  the  provision  of  courses  of 
lectures  on  methods  of  investigation,  by  the  award  of  student- 
ships varying  in  value  from  ;f25  to  ;£200,  and  by  the  co-ordina- 
tion of  different  agencies  for  providing  the  student  engaged  in 
research  with  the  help  he  needs.  The  fist  of  the  School  publica- 
tions bears  witness  to  results  achieved  in  this  direction.  The 
success  of  the  School  has  from  the  first  proved  steady  and  con- 
tinuous. Twice  it  has  had  to  remove  to  more  commodious 
premises  from  its  original  home  at  9  John  Street,  Adelphi,  and 
already  considerable  pressure  is  being  felt  upon  the  space  pro- 
vided in  the  new  building  erected  for  it  in  1902.  Since  the 
School  was  opened  in  1895  ii^ore  than  3,500  students  have  been 
entered  on  its  books,  of  whom  about  10  per  cent,  have  been 
foreigners.

Modern  Language  Association,  Tlie.  This  Association  has  been 
established  for  the  following  objects  : — {a)  To  raise  the  standard 
of  eflSciency  in  the  teaching  of  Modem  Languages,  and  pro- 
mote their  study  in  our  schools  and  the  country  generally ; 
(6)  to  obtain  for  Modem  Languages  the  status  to  which  their 
intrinsic  value,  as  instruments  of  mental  discipline,  entitles 
them — apart  from  their  acknowledged  commercial  and  utili- 
tarian importance  ;  (c)  to  provide  means  of  communication 
for  students  and  teachers  of  foreign  languages.  The  annual 
subscription  is  los.  6d.  The  organs  of  the  Association  are

Psychical  Research,  Society  for,  th  Hanover  Square,  w.  1882. 
Objects — To  examine  the  nature  and  extent  of  any  influence 
which  may  be  exerted  by  one  mind  upon  another,  otherwise 
than  through  the  recognised  sensory  channels  ;  to  study  hypno- 
tism and  mesmerism,  and  to  inquire  into  the  alleged  phenomena 
of  clairvoyance  ;  to  investigate  any  reports,  resting  on  testi- 
mony sulKiciently  strong  and  not  too  remote,  of  apparitions 
coinciding  with  some  external  event  (as,  for  instance,  a  death), 
or  giving  information  previously  unknown  to  the  percipient, 
or  being  seen  by  two  or  more  persons  independently  01  each 
other  ;  to  inc^uirc  into  various  alleged  phenomena  apparently 
inexplicable  by  known  laws  of  nature,  and  commonly  reierred  by 
s])iritualists  to  the  agency  of  extra-human  intelligences ;  to 
collect  and  collate  existing  materials  bearing  on  the  histoxy  of 
these  su!)jects.  The  aim  of  the  Society  is  to  approach  these 
various  ])roblems  without  prejudice  or  prepossession  of  any 
kind,  and  in  the  same  spirit  of  exact  and  unimpassioned  inquiry 
which  lias  enabled  science  to  solve  so  many  problems  once  not 
less  obscure.  Publications — Proceedings  and  Journal,  Sub^ 
scription — Members,  £2  2s.  per  annuni ;  Associates,  £1  is. 
Secretary — Miss  Alice  Johnson,  20  Hanover  Square,  w.

Pure  Literature  Society,  11  Buckingham  Street,  w.c.  1854.  Object

Quill  Club,  The.  Founded  1898.  Objects — To  afford  assistance  to 
those  desirous  of  entering  the  ranks  of  literature.  To  supply 
aspirants  with  criticism  of  their  work,  free  advice  concerning 
the  placing  of  MSS.  with  magazines,  etc.,  and  suggestions  with 
regard  to  transactions  with  publishers,  agents,  and  the  like. 
To  protect  its  members  against  tricksters  trading  on  literary 
beginners,  and  to  maintain  a  record  of  concerns  which,  after 
investigation,  are  considered  to  be  dishonestly  conducted. 
To  provide  members  with  a  postal  lending  library  of  selected 
volumes  containing  instruction  in  literary  and  journalistic 
matters.  To  afford  aspirants  facilities  for  social  intercourse  by 
meetings  in  London  and  other  centres.  The  Qub  supplies 
wants  of  the  unfledged  author  and  literary  student  that  are  not 
met  by  any  other  society.  It  is  managed  by  honorary  officers, 
who  are  elected  by  the  members  yearly.  The  income  is  entirely 
devoted  to  the  work  of  the  Qub,  and  audited  balance-sheets 
are  published  annually.  Subscription — ^Ten  shillings  yearly. 
No  entrance  fee.  Meetings — In  London,  on  the  first  Saturday 
of  each  month.  Publication  —  The  Quill.  Club  Circular 
(monthly).  President — ^Max  Pemberton.  Chairman — Juan 
W.  P.  Chamberlin.  Honorary  Secretary — ^W.  J.  Corley, 
38  Niton  Street,  London,  S.W. 

Rationalist  Press  Assoeiation,  Limited.  Founded  1899.  Objects — 
"  To  stimulate  freedom  of  thought  and  inquiry  in  reference  to 
ethics,  theology,  philosophy,  and  kindred  subjects.  To  pro- 
mote a  secul^  system  of  education.  To  publish  and  to  dis- 
tribute books,  pamphlets,  and  periodicals  designed  to  .  .  . 
assist  in  the  spread  of  Rationalist  principles.  Rationalism 
may  be  defined  as  the  mental  attitude  which  unreservedly  accepts 
the  supremacy  of  reason,  and  aims  at  establishing  a  system  of 
philosophy  and  ethics  verifiable  by  experience  and  independent 
of  all  arbitrary  assumptions  or  authority."  Among  the  cheap 
reprints  already  published  are  Darwin's  Origin  of  Species, 
Mill's  Essay  on  Liberty,  Sir  Leslie  Stephen's  An  Agnostic's 
Apology,  Renan's  Life  of  Jesus,  and  works  by  Spencer,  Haeckel,

Rome,  British  School  at.  This  school,  projected  in  1899,  was  opened 
in  the  spring  of  190 1.  It  has  been  modelled,  in  many  respects, 
on  the  existing  British  School  at  Athens  ;  and  the  two  schools 
will,  it  is  hoped,  be  ultimately  placed  under  the  control  of  a 
single  committee.  Objects — ^The  School  is  intended,  first,  as  a 
training-ground  for  students  fresh  from  the  universities  and 
other  educational  institutions  ;  secondly,  as  a  centre  round 
which  more  mature  students  may  group  themselves  for  pur- 
poses of  systematic  research  ;  and,  thirdly,  as  a  source  of 
information  and  advice  for  visitors  desiring  to  pursue  serious 
studies  in  Rome  and  Italy.  The  province  of  the  School  is  not 
purely  archaeological,  but  includes  all  periods  of  Roman  and 
Italian  history,  art,  antiquities,  and  literature.  The  excavation 
of  ancient  sites,  which  has  formed  an  important  part  of  the 
work  of  the  School  at  Athens,  is  excluded  in  Italy  by  the  rules 
of  the  Itahan  Government.  But  in  other  respects  the  work  of 
the  Roman  School  is  more  varied  than  that  of  the  Athenian. 
since  the  range  of  studies  which  centre  in  Rome  is  wider, 
including,  for  example,  palaeography,  Christian  as  well  as  clas- 
sical antiquities,  and  the  art  and  architecture  of  the  Renaissance. 
Vol.  I.  of  the  Papers  of  the  school,  pubUshed  in  August,  1902, 
contains  papers  by  the  Director  (Mr.  Rushforth)  on  the  "  Church 
of  S.  Maria  Antigua,"  and  by  Mr.  T.  Ashby,  jun.,  on  the 
"  Classical  Topography  of  the  Roman  Campagna."  Vol.  II., 
published  October,  1904,  consists  of  reproductions  of  the 
drawings  of  Andreas  Koner,  with  text  by  Mr.  T.  Ashby,  iun. 
With  the  sanction  and  co-operation  of  the  Italian  authorities 
the  school  has  undertaken  a  work  of  lasting  importance — ^the 
cataloguing  of  the  works  of  ancient  art  in  the  municipal 
museums  in  Rome. 

At  present  the  annual  income  of  the  School  is  less  than 
£SOO,  much  of  which  is  precarious  ;  and  it  is  estimated  that 
an  income  of  £1 ,000  will  be  required  to  secure  permanence  and 
efficiency.  The  Director  of  the  School  is  Mr.  H.  Stuart  Jones. 
Further  information  will  be  supplied  by  the  Secretary,  John  pf.

International   Exeliango  Bnreao  of  tlie   Smitksonijui  InstttattoiL 

Since  its  foundation  in  1846  the  Smithsonian  Institntion  has 


566  THE  LITERARY  YEAR-BOOK     [part  ii. 

conducted  a  system  of  the  exchange  of  literary  and  scientific 
publications  voluntarily  presented  by  institutions  or  indi- 
viduals in  the  United  States  to  correspondents  throughout  the 
world,  and  reciprocally. 

These  exchanges  are  in  no  sense  of  a  commercial  nature,  for 
no  publications  for  sale  are  allowed  transmission.  More  than 
one-half  of  the  exchanges  consist  of  Government  documents, 
while  the  other  half  are  publications  exchanged  between  learned 
societies  and  savants  throughout  the  world.  The  International 
Exchange  Service  has  salaried  resident  agents  in  London, 
Leipzig,  and  Budapest,  and  a  number  of  agents  in  various  parts 
of  the  world  lend  their  services  gratuitously.  The  correspon- 
dents who  more  or  less  regularly  exchange  publications  through 
the  Smithsonian  Institution  now  aggregate  44,012,  of  which 
9,559  are  in  the  United  States  and  the  rest  in  foreign  lands,  ex- 
tending even  to  the  remotest  comers  of  the  world. 

Further  particulars  with  reference  to  the  above  can  be  ob- 
tained on  appUcation  to  Messrs.  W.  Wesley  &  Son,  28  Essex 
Street,  Strand,  w.c,  who  are  the  London  agents  for  the 
Smithsonian  Institution.     {See  p.  569.)

Victoria 

Royal  Geographieal  Soeiety  of  Australasia,  Victoria.  Instituted 
1883.  The  objects  of  the  Society  are  :  (i)  Scientific — The  ad- 
vancement of  geographical  science,  the  study  of  physical  geo- 
graphy, and  the  exploration  of  Australasia,  with  the  islands 
and  seas  adjacent  thereto,  to  obtain  information  upon  their 
physical  features,  fauna,  flora,  and  geological  formation,  &c. 
(2)  Commercial — The  study  of  commercial  geography,  the 
natural  and  artificial  products,  and  the  manufactures  of  various 
countries  to  promote  commerce.  (3)  Educational — The  dis- 
semination of  knowledge  of  physical,  commercial,  and  political 
geography  among  all  classes,  by  means  of  illustrated  public 
lectures  and  publications.  (4)  Historical — ^The  collection  and 
pubUcation  of  historical  records  of  gec^;raphical  interest,  and 
of  memoirs  of  notable  men  of  Australasia.  (5)  The  compila- 
tion, from  reliable  data,  of  the  geography  of  Australasia.  Any 
lady  or  gentleman  may  become  an  ordinary  member,  subject 
to  election.  Subscription — Gentlemen,  £1  is.  ;  ladies,  los.  6d. 
per  annum.  Life  Membership — gentlemen,  £10  los. ;  ladies, 
^5  5s.     Hon.  Secretary — A.  C.  Macdonald.

Canadian  Soeiety  of  Authors.  Founded  February,  1899.  Objects — 
To  promote  the  producticm  of  literature  in  Canada,  and  the 


5^8  THE  LITERARY  YEAR-BOOK      [part  n. 

interests  of  Canadian  authors  ;  to  discuss  and  circulate  informa- 
tion witli  regard  to  public  questions  affecting  literary  workers  ; 
and  to  obtain  and  distribute  information  as  to  channels  of 
jmbhcation  open  to  Canadian  authors.  The  Society  exerted 
great  influence  in  connection  with  the  preparation  and  passing

Royal  Society  of  Canada,  Ottawa.  Founded,  1882,  by  the  Duke  of 
Argyll  for  the  advancement  of  science.  Most  of  the  historical

SOCIETIES:   FOREIGN 

AMERICA 

Smithsonian  Institution,  Washington,  U.S. A.  Founded  by  the  muni- 
licence  of  James  Smithson.  "  for  the  increase  and  diffusion 
of  knowledge  among  men,"  who  left  an  ^idowment  of  $540,000, 
an  amoun  t  since  increased  to  nearly  $1 ,000,000.  The  Act  ol  Con- 
gress estabUshing  the  Institution  was  passed  on  August  10, 1846. 
Its  statutory  members  are  the  President,  the  Vice-President, 
the  Chief  Justice  of  the  United  States,  and  the  heads  of  the 


570  THE  LITERARY  YEAR-BOOK      [part  ii. 

executive  departments.  The  Institatiun  is  controlled  and 
directed  by  a  Board  of  Regents,  through  an  Executive  Officer, 
who  is  also  the  Secretary  of  the  Board.  There  are  five  chief 
divisions  of  the  work  of  the  Institution,  which  is  mainly  original 
scientific  advancement  and  research.  These  are :  (i)  The 
National  Museum ;  (2)  The  International  Exchanges  (see 
?•  536);  (3)  The  Bureau  of  American  Ethnology;  (4)  The 
National  Zoological  Park  ;  and  (5)  The  Astrophysical  Observa- 
tory. It  has  a  table  at  the  Naples  Zoological  station,  under- 
takes expeditions  and  explorations,  and  issues  annual  reports 
and  numerous  monographs  on  the  subjects  in  which  its  savants 
and  officers  make  research.  Secretary — S.  P.  Langlsy,  Esq., 
Washington  (D.C.),  U.S.A.  {Seeip,  536). 
Society  of  Ameriean  Authors,  Ineorporated.  Founded  May  12. 1892. 
Objects — To  further  and  unify  the  interests  of  American  authors, 
and  to  better  the  conditions  of  the  profession  of  letters  in 
America.  Subscription — Five  dollars  per  annum.  EfUrance 
Fee — Two  dollars.  The  organ  is  The  American  Author,  pub- 
lished monthly.  President — Hon.  Rastus  S.  Ransom.  Secre- 
tary— Mr.  G.  Grosvenor  Dawe,  128  Broadway,  New  York.

FRANCE 

Aead^mie  Fran^aise.  The  Academie  Fran^aise  was  founded  in  1635 
by  Cardinal  Richelieu,  and  is  the  highest  of  the  five  Academies 
constituting  the  InstUut  de  France.  It  is  composed  of  forty 
members,  elected  for  life.  The  Academy  awards  prizes  annually 
in  ail  branches  of  learning  and  Uterature,  and  others  at  longer 
periods  ;  it  is  the  official  guardian  of  the  French  language,  and

Goncoort  Aeademy.  Founded  January.  1903.  Und^  his  will, 
Edmond  de  Goncourt  left  his  entire  property  to  Alphonse 
Daudet  and  Leon  Hennique,  directing  them  to  solicit  official

each  Academician  an  annuity  of  6,000  francs  was  bequeathed  ; 
and  a  prize  of  5,000  francs  was  to  be  awarded  every  year  to  the 
author  of  some  work  of  fiction.  The  members  were  to  meet 
at  certain  intervals,  and  other  rules  were  laid  down  by  the 
will  as  to  the  organization  of  the  Academy.  Edmoiid  de 
Goncourt  died  July  16,  1896.  Legal  disputes  arose  relative 
to  the  vaUdity  of  the  will,  and  oSier  delajrs  occurred ;  and 
it  was  not  until  January  19,  1903,  that  official  sanction  was 
granted  to  the  foundation.  By  the  will  only  eight  members 
were  nominated,  as  follows:  Alphonse  Dandet,  J.  K.  Hnys-

Soei6t^  des  Aatanrs  et  Compositaors  dramatlqnes.  Founded  in  1829 
to  protect  the  interests  of  its  members,  and  to  collect  the

Soei^t^  des  Gens  de  Lettres.  Founded  December  10. 1837.  Objects — ^To 
defend  the  interests,  social,  |>ersonal,  and  pecuniary,  of  all  its 
members,  and  to  see  that  they  obtain  and  pxx>fit  by  their  rights. 
To  assure  them  an  adequate  return  for  their  literary  labours. 
To  improve  the  terms  under  which  these  labours  are  undertaken. 
To  make  as  profitable  a  use  as  possible  for  the  general  good  of 
the  Society  of  the  contributions  of  its  members,  notably  in  the 
matters  of  certain  **  rights  of  reproduction  "  over  th^  work. 
The  latter  object  refers  to  the  practice  prevailing  in  France  of 
selling  or  leasing  the  serial  rights  of  stories  after  they  have  ap- 
peared in  volume  form.     Members  must  yield  these  rights  to  the 
Society,  which  pays  the  member  a  fixed  price,  and  gets  what  it 
can  from  publishers  and  editors  in  excess  of  this  for  the  benefit 
of  the  Society.     The  Society  has  three  funds  :  (1)  A  fund  from 
which  advances  can  be  made  to  assist  members  in  their  literary 
work  ;  (2)  a  fund  upon  which  sick  members  can  draw ;  and 
(3)  a  pension  fund.     The  Society  also  has  the  direction  of 
otiier  funds  for  annual  Uterary  prizes,  such  as  those  of  the 
Fondation  Chauchard,  Le  Prix  Balzac,  Le  Prix  Petit  Bourg, 
which  together  amount  to    10,000  francs ;  there  are  several 
smaller  prizes  of  from   200   to  600  francs  each.     M.   Sully- 
Prudhomme  has  also  placed  at  the  disposition  of  the  Society  a 
certain  part  of  the  Nobel  award  which  he  gained  in  1901.     lliis 
prize,  amounting  to  1500  francs  per  annum,  is  to  be  applied 
each  year,  during  the  hfe  of  M.  SuUy-Prudhomme,  for  the 
benefit  of  a  young  poet  who  shall  be  choisen  by  a  jury  of  six 
members  of  the  Society,  for  the  purpose  of  publishing  his  first

HOLLAND 

In  almost  all  provinces  of  the  Netherlands  there  is  a  provincial 
Society  which  chiefly  has  for  object  the  history,  especially  of 
customs  and  habits,  of  former  times.  Also  in  several  towns 
an  archaeological  Society  for  local  history,  etc.  Many  of  these 
societies  have  a  library  and  a  museum. 

There  are  also  an  Historical  Society  {Secretary — ^Dr.  S.  Muller, 
Utrecht)  and  a  Nederl.  Ondheidkundlge  Bond  {Secretary — 
Dr.  OvBRVooRDE,  Leiden). 

In  Friesland  there  is  a  special  Frisian  Society  entirely  devoted  to  the 
study,  maintenance,  and  promotion  of  the  Frisian  language. 

In  Leiden  exists  the  most  famous  Maats-Chappy  van  Nederlandsehe 
Letterkunde.  This  society  possesses  a  splendid  library  (c/.I^den), 
and  annually  elects  twenty  members  from  Holland  and  eight 
from  abroad,  and  publishes  yearly  an  Annual  and  biographies 
of  deceased  members  ;  also  a  Tydschrift  (periodical  for  philology 
and  literary  history),  philological  and  historical  tracts  (to-day

PUBLIC   LIBRARIES 

THE  YEAR'S  WORK 

By  a  public  LIBRARIAN. 

During  the  last  few  years  great  advances  have  been  made 
in  all  branches  of  the  administration  of  public  libraries  and 
in  the  sphere  of  their  influence  ;  but  there  is  perhaps  no 
year  in  the  whole  history  of  the  fifty-six  years  of  their 
existence,  since  the  passing  of  what  is  sometimes  known 
as  the  "  Ewart  Act  "  in  1850,  in  which  the  advance  has 
been  so  important  and  so  steady,  and  in  which  the  striving 
after  the  ideal  has  been  so  sane.  Side  by  side  with  this 
advance  a  greater  appreciation  of  their  efforts  and  a  clearer 
understanding  of  their  aims  has  been  apparent  in  the 
public.  The  usual  two  or  three  antagonistic  articles  have 
appeared  in  the  reviews,  but  they  have  fallen  rather  flat, 
and  their  reception  by  the  great  body  of  the  press  has 
been,  at  least,  cold.  The  chief  feature  of  these  review 
articles  is  the  parade  of  what  is  now  known  as  the  *'  fiction 
bogey."  As  Mr.  Carnegie  said  on  a  recent  occasion,  the 
amount  of  fiction  issued  from  public  libraries  is  really  about 
15  per  cent,  if  either  a  time  test  or  a  cost  test  is  imposed, 
and  a  numerical  test  is  of  no  value.  Possibly  Mr.  Carnegie's 
statement  may  have  some  weight  with  the  writers  of  the 
review  articles,  while  the  repeated  explanations  of  librarians 
themselves  have  been  disregarded. 

The  number  of  new  libraries  either  completed  or  com- 
menced is  also  very  large.  This  is  due  no  doubt  to  the 
continued  munificence  of  Mr.  Carnegie.  It  is  a  striking 
fact  that  the  whole  of  the  large  London  boroughs,  with 
the  exception  of  Marylebone  and  the  greater  part  of  Pad-r 
dington,    have    now    adopted    '*  the    Acts.''    The    three 

581 


582  THE  LITERARY  YEAR-BOOK      [part  ii. 

boroughs,  Islington,  St.  Pancras,  and  Hackney,  are  all 
busy  removing  the  **  blot  from  their  'scutcheon.'*  At 
present  the  result  of  an  interesting  experiment  at  the  first 
of  these  is  being  awaited  with  much  interest  by  librarians 
all  over  the  country.  The  experiment  was  partly  the 
cause  and  partly  the  result  of  the  agitation  against  the 
news-rooms,  which  received  a  severe  repulse  at  the  Annual 
Conference  in  1905.  There  are  extensive  reading-rooms 
provided  at  the  Islington  Libraries,  with  a  liberal  selection 
of  the  best  periodicals — the  equivalent  of  the  magazine- 
rooms  at  other  libraries.  But  the  news-room  is  conspicuous 
by  its  absence.  The  Times  will  be  taken,  but  more  for  the 
purpose  of  filing  for  reference  than  for  any  other  reason  ; 
and  the  advertisement-sheets  of  the  chief  London  dailies 
will  be  exliibited  outside  the  libraries  in  the  early  morning 
hours.  From  one  point  of  view  the  plan  has  a  great  deal 
in  its  favour — its  saving.  The  result  of  this  is  apparent  in 
the  amount  of  the  income  set  aside  for  the  purchase  of 
books.  For  the  next  seven  years  this  will  reach  a  total  of 
£14,000,  exclusive  of  the  books  already  purchased  for 
foundation  purposes.  This  amount  is  probably  unprece- 
dented in  London  boroughs.  But  the  crux  of  the  question 
is  this :  Will  the  public  appreciate  this  large  expenditure 
on  books,  or  will  they  prefer  the  scrappy  but  interesting 
daily  paper  with  all  its  faults  ?  There  is  no  doubt  that 
this  is  one  of  the  ultimate  ideals  of  public  library  work, 
and  if  the  Islington  public  can  be  induced  to  appreciate  it 
without  any  previous  educating  process,  they  will  at  once 
take  a  position  far  in  advance  of  most  library  districts  in 
this  country.  Perhaps  it  is  because  of  this  apparent  pros- 
perity of  the  public  libraries  that  the  Associated  Book- 
sellers and  the  Publishers'  Association  have  again  en- 
deavoured to  **  put  on  the  screw  "  in  the  form  of  a  close 
time  for  net  books.  Although  the  measure  is  really  aimed 
at  the  public  library,  both  booksellers  and  publishers 
apparently  forget  that  the  increase  in  its  influence  in- 
evitably makes  a  greater  intensity  of  poverty  in  the  public 
library.  A  greater  influence  means  more  work  ;  and  more 
work,  until  the  removal  of  the  **  penny  limit,"  means  an 
economy  so  strict  that  it  would  be  better  described  as 
starvation. 


PART  II.]  PUBLIC  LIBRARIES  583 

The  resolutions  passed  by  the  associations  interested  are 
a  glaring  instance  of  "  terminological  inexactitude." 
Librarians  do  not  object  to  the  net  book  system.  It  is 
difficult  to  imderstand  the  reason  for  a  discount  to  the 
retail  buyer  of  single  books,  and  it  is  impossible  to  say 
anything  in  its  favour.  What  public  libraries  do  object 
to  is  the  refusal  of  the  associations  to  allow  a  bookseller 
to  give  the  discount  due  to  libraries  as  large  buyers. 
Proprietaiy  subscription  libraries  buy  direct  as  "trade." 
But  public  libraries,  which  are  simply  co-operative  book- 
clubs, do  not  even  receive  the  treatment  accorded  similar 
institutions  in  the  United  States,  where  the  net  book 
system  was  nursed  and  fostered.  Curiously  enough,  this 
country'  stands  practically  alone  in  its  refusal  to  allow  an 
additional  discount  to  public  libraries. 

The  opportunity,  eagerly  grasped  at  in  some  localities, 
of  dealing  a  blow  to  the  individual  public  library  by  the 
infliction  of  a  heavy  assessment  for  the  purpose  of  local 
rates,  has  not  been  so  far-reaching  as  was  at  first  feared. 
Something  of  this  was  no  doubt  due  to  the  action  of  the 
London  County  Council.  Of  all  the  public  libraries  in  the 
country,  there  are  only  some  forty  rated  at  more  than  a 
nominal  figure  in  England.  Many  of  these  assessments 
are  the  result  of  petty  local  jealousy.  It  is,  however,  a 
curious  fact  that  the  smaller  the  library  the  heavier  the 
imposition.  The  Library  Association  has  arranged  for  the 
inclusion  of  a  clause  in  the  Omnibus  Bill,  which  it  is  hoped 
will  be  introduced  during  the  next  session  by  Mr.  H.  J. 
Tennant,  M.P.,  providing  for  the  exemption  of  all  public 
libraries  from  the  payment  of  rates.  The  association 
might  have  been  better  advised  to  make  this  the  subject 
of  a  separate  Bill.  The  other  clauses  of  the  proposed 
Omnibus  Bill,  which  was  the  subject  of  a  special  conference 
at  Birmingham,  deal  with  the  extension  of  the  Acts  to 
county  areas  and  the  removal  of  the  rate  limitation.  The 
principal  clauses  dealing  with  these  two  matters  are  as 
follows  : 

I.  Where  the  Public  Libraries  Acts  are  already  adopted 
(whether  with  or  without  any  limitation  of  the  rate),  or 
may  after  the  passing  of  this  Act  be  adopted,  in  any  dis- 
trict the  library  authority  may  levy  a  rate  in  the  manner 


584  THE  LITERARY  YE'AR-BOOK      [part  ii. 

prescribed  by  the  Public  Libraries  Acts  for  the  amount  of 
their  expenses  in  each  financial  year  or  half-year,  and  any 
limitation  in  any  Act  of  Parliament  of  the  expenditure  or 
of  the  rate  to  be  levied  by  a  library  authority  is  hereby 
repealed. 

2.  The  principal  Act  may  be  adopted  for  the  administra- 
tive county  or  any  part  thereof  by  a  resolution  of  the 
County  Council  passed  at  a  meeting  of  the  County  Council, 
and  three  months  before  the  meeting  special  notice  of  the 
meeting  and  of  the  intention  to  propose  the  resolution  shall 
be  given  to  every  member  of  the  Council,  and  the  notice 
shall  be  deemed  to  have  been  duly  given  to  a  member, 
etc. 

In  spite  of  the  great  improvement  which  has  taken  place 
in  this  respect,  it  is  doubtful  if  the  public  yet  appreciates 
the  fact  that  not  only  does  education  not  finish  with  the 
termination  of  the  school  course,  but  that  the  groundwork 
laid  at  the  schools  is  wasted  unless  the  education  is  con- 
tinued. It  is  a  matter  for  great  regret  that  something  has 
not  yet  been  done  in  the  way  of  rural  libraries,  other  than 
the  spasmodic  efforts  of  private  generosity  and  enterprise. 
It  is  true  that  this  individual  work  has  met  with  signal 
success  in  Herefordshire,  due  to  the  enthusiasm  of  the 
Bishop  of  Hereford  ;  and  in  the  Isle  of  Wight,  as  the  result 
of  Sir  C.  Seeley's  ;  in  Scotland,  where  there  were  early 
itinerating  libraries  ;  and  in  Yorkshire  and  Lancashire, 
where  village  libraries  were  formed  fifty  years  ago,  and 
where  there  are  now  thirty-three  village  centres ;  in  Carlisle, 
where  ten  centres  are  provided  for  ;  and  in  Cumberland 
and  elsewhere.  But  these  successes  only  accentuate  the 
crying  need  of  other  localities.  It  is  not  proposed  to 
interfere  with  those  towns  in  which  libraries  are  already 
in  existence,  but  to  supply  the  needs  of  the  villages, 
hamlets,  and  rural  districts  throughout  the  various  counties. 

The  clause  relating  to  the  removal  of  the  rate  limitation 
is  not  so  formidable  as  it  appears,  because  it  would  still 
lie  with  the  local  authority  to  determine  the  amount  of 
the  rate  to  be  levied.  As  a  matter  of  fact,  the  measure 
would  probably  promote  economy,  and  a  reduced  rate  in 
many  places,  if  it  were  known  that  in  the  event  of  a  pressing 
need  the  present  limit  might  be  exceeded.     It  is  strange 


PART  II.]  PUBLIC  LIBRARIES  5^5 

that,  the  need  for  pubhc  libraries  being  granted  by  the 
fact  of  their  existence,  the  limit  of  their  usefulness  should 
be  a  penny  in  the  pound.  This  is  still  more  remarkable 
when  it  is  remembered  that  even  this  imiform  rating  pro- 
duces most  unequal  incomes.  The  produce  of  a  penny  rate 
ranges  from  £400  in  a  town  of  30,000  inhabitants  to  £1,200 
in  a  town  of  the  same  size.  In  other  words,  in  the  one 
town  the  rate  is  3id.  per  head,  while  in  the  other  the 
average  payment  of  each  inhabitant  is  three  times  that 
amoimt.  Possibly  in  the  early  days,  when  education  was 
practically  an  unknown  quantity,  the  limit  may  have  had 
a  salutary  effect.     But  education  is  now  a  vital  force. 

This  Omnibus  Bill  has  been  preceded  by  the  withdrawal 
of  the  two  Bills  of  1905. 

One  of  the  most  notable  features  of  the  year  has  been 
the  formation  of  an  association  of  French  Librarians. 
The  first  meeting  was  held  on  April  22,  when  the  following
