M.  Deniker,  Biblioth6caire  du  Museum,  was  elected  first 
president. 

The  publication  of  Mr.  Brown  s  Subject  Classification 
marks  an  epoch  in  this  branch  of  library  science.  Although 
the  work  is  a  great  advance  on  the  author's  previous  book 
on  library  classification,  the  new  system  cannot  easily  be 
adapted  to  the  old  **  adjustable/'  in  spite  of  the  statement 
in  the  introduction  to  the  new  work  that  it  is  an  expansion 
or  elaboration  of  the  latter.  The  new  S3^tem  contains 
many  new  features,  but  one  of  the  principal  points  recom- 
mending it  in  this  country  is  the  fact  that  it  is  an  all- 
British  system.  It  follows,  therefore,  that  English  topo- 
graphy receives  adequate  attention,  as  well  as  English 
manufactures  and  phases  of  social,  commercial,  and  re- 
ligious life.     The  principal  subject-headings  are  : 

A. — Generalia. 

B-D. — Physical  Science. 

E-F. — ^Biological  Science. 

G-H. — Ethnology  and  Medicine. 

I. — Economic  Biology. 

J-K. — Philosophy  and  Religion. 

L. — Social  and  Political  Science. 

M. — Language  and  Literature. 

N. — Literary  Forms. 

0-W — .History,  Geography. 

X. — Biography. 

These  are  subdivided  minutely,  and  are  further  divided 
by  means  of  the  Categorical  Tables — ^series  of  numbers 
which  are  applicable  in  various  degrees  to  all  the  classes — 
and  the  Biographical  Combination  Tables — author  mark- 
ings for  use  in  any  section.  The  chief  claim  on  behalf  of 
the  system  is  that  it  is  a  one-place  classification  :  science 
is  not  divided  into  the  classes  "  pure  "  and  "  applied," 
and  the  classes  are  arranged  in  a  logical  growth  from 
''  matter  and  force  "  to  '*  record  "  through  "  life  and 
mind."     "WTien  a  practical  application  has  remedied  what- 


PART  II.]  PUBLIC  LIBRARIES  5^7 

ever  defects  are  to  be  found  in  the  system,  probably  it  will 
be  the  one  most  generally  adopted  in  this  country.  Another 
publication  of  considerable  importance  is  the  Manual  of 
Annotation,  by  Mr.  E.  A.  Savage.  This  is  a  by-path  of 
cataloguing,  but  as  so  much  depends  on  the  careful  guid- 
ance of  readers,  it  is  one  requiring  the  greatest  care.  The 
subject  is  much  more  advanced  in  America,  where  it  is 
found  in  the  regular  curriculum  in  the  various  schools  of 
library  science.  Library  Work  is  an  American  periodical 
publication  of  great  value,  first  issued  in  the  early  part 
of  the  year.  It  is  devoted  chiefly  to  a  bibliography  of 
library  literature  very  fully  annotated.  Other  publica- 
tions of  importance  are  in  preparation,  amongst  them  a 
new  edition,  revised  and  largely  rewritten,  of  Mr.  Brown's 
Library  Economy,  which  is  expected  about  the  New 
Year.  Mr.  Quinn*s  Manual  of  Cataloguing  is  also  imder- 
going  extensive  revision  ;  and  the  preparation  of  a  dic- 
tionary of  library  terms  is  also  expected  to  be  commenced 
shortly. 

The  question  of  Library  Schools  is  much  nearer  a  prac- 
tical settlement  in  this  coimtry  than  it  has  ever  been 
before,  and  there  is  little  doubt  that  something  will  ulti- 
mately be  accomplished  in  this  direction,  either  when  the 
rate  limit  is  removed,  or  when  the  whole  profession  is 
placed  upon  a  more  equitable  basis.  Already  the  present 
admirable  courses  of  lectures  on  librarianship  at  the  London 
School  of  Economics  in  connection  with  the  London  Uni- 
versity are  doing  much  to  raise  the  tone  of  the  work.  The 
examinations  of  the  Association  show  a  decided  improve- 
ment in  their  rationale,  and,  perhaps  as  a  result  of  this,  in 
the  number  of  students  entering  when  compared  with  those 
of  some  years  back. 

In  the  interests  of  the  profession,  it  is  impossible  to  say 
too  much  in  condenmation  of  the  number  of  ridiculously 
small  libraries  that  have  been  established.  With  the 
notable  exception  of  the  Claydon  Libraries,  it  is  doubtful 
if  these  small  libraries  are  of  adequate  value  to  the  villages 
they  serve.  The  extension  of  the  Acts  to  county  areas 
will  do  much  to  remove  this  evil.  And  the  proposal  of 
the  Association,  when  carried  into  effect,  will  still  further 
improve  matters.    This  proposal  includes,  amongst  other 


588  THE  LITERARY  YEAR-BOOK      [part  il 

things,  the  appointment  when  requested  of  advisory 
librarians  to  very  small  institutions. 

Another  matter  which  received  some  little  public  atten- 
tion during  1906,  and  one  which,  as  it  has  been  presenting 
the  minds  of  librarians  for  some  years  past,  will  receive 
still  more  in  the  future,  is  a  profession  register.  The 
suggestion  at  present  is  for  a  voluntary  registration  of 
librarians.  It  is  difficult  to  see  in  what  way  this  is  to  prove 
of  any  value,  and  any  arbitrary  test  of  size  or  income  as 
a  sine  qua  non  is  absurd.  The  only  form  of  registration 
that  can  be  of  any  real  value  to  the  profession,  and  the 
only  one  which  should  be  tolerated,  is  compulsory  and 
official  registration.  That  such  registration  is  needed  is 
shown  by  the  still  occasional  appointment  of  unqualified 
or  not  properly  qualified  men,  although  such  occurrences 
are  becoming  more  rare  year  by  year. 

The  sound  leather  question  has  been  hardened  consider- 
ably since  the  valuable  report  of  the  Sound  Leather  Com- 
mittee. The  latest  development  is  the  compilation  of  a 
Sound  Leather  Directory,  containing  the  names  of  the  users 
of  sound  leather  and  the  binders  'supplying  it  and  the 
firms  manufacturing  it.  The  increased  price  of  leather 
which  at  one  time  seriously  threatened  bookbinding  has 
scarcely  affected  the  library  work  branch  of  the  trade. 
Possibly  cloth  binding,  which  the  writer  has  recommended 
for  some  years  past  as  a  leather  substitute  for  lending- 
library  work,  has  increased  in  popularity. 

An  interesting  return  which  was  recently  elicited  by  the 
committee  of  a  library  in  the  North  showed  that  there  were 
only  some  thirty  libraries  opening  on  Sunday,  and  one  or 
two  which  have  since  closed  have  still  further  reduced  the 
number.  In  this  connection  it  is  interesting  to  note  that 
there  is  only  one  library  in  Kent  opening  on  Sunday.  This 
is  not  due  generally  to  Sabbatarian  motives,  but  because 
there  is  no  legitimate  demand  for  Sunday  opening. 

The  number  of  libraries  obliterating,  or,  as  it  is  com- 
monly called,  **  blacking-out,"  betting  news  has  increased. 
It  is  difficult  to  oppose  this  crusade,  because  it  Xzys  the 
opposition  open  to  the  suspicion  of  being  partial  to  this 
form  of  "  sport."  The  Sowerby  Committee  have  recently 
circularized  other  libraries  for  the  purpose  of  influencing 


PART  II.]  PUBLIC  LIBRARIES  5-^ 

them  to  send  a  petition  (similar  to  their  own)  to  Parliament 
praying  for  an  Act  forbidding  the  publication  of  betting 
news  in  the  newspiapers.  However  commendable  this 
action  may  be,  it  is  difl&cult  to  miderstand,  unless  it  is 
being  defrayed  privately,  how  this  expenditure  can  be 
defended. 

The  amalgamation  of  the  Library  Assistants'  Associa- 
tion with  the  Library  Association  was  a  matter  of  con- 
siderable interest  during  the  time  of  its  discussion  by  the 
junior  society.  The  matter  was  thoroughly  ventilated 
both  by  means  of  meetings  and  in  the  pages  of  the  Assistant, 
and  there  is  no  doubt  that  it  was  only  after  the  most  careful 
consideration  that  the  members  of  the  Assistants'  Associa- 
tion refused  the  proposed  amalgamation  on  a  plebiscite 
of  its  members.  Although  this  was  not  the  groimd  on 
which  the  refusal  was  based,  it  seems  more  than  probable 
that  some  time  in  the  future  there  will  be  a  broad  but 
indubitable  line  of  demarcation  between  the  assistant  and 
the  librarian.  If  the  University  Chair,  which  is  the  day- 
dream of  many  librarians,  is  ever  realized  (and  there  is 
already  a  whisper  circulating  in  r^ard  to  it),  the  higher 
branches  of  the  profession  must  necessarily  be  closed  to 
those  who  have  not  passed  through  the  course.  Even 
without  the  professional  chair,  it  appears  certain  that, 
when  the  various  branches  of  library  science  have  passed 
their  experimental  stages,  and  have  crjrstallized  into  con- 
crete rules,  without  nece^arily  becoming  too  uniform,  the 
man  who  has  been  able  to  devote  the  whole  of  his  time 
and  energy  to  the  study  of  the  profession  wiU  take  pre- 
cedence of  him  who  has  only  a  certain  amount  of  practical 
work  of  a  not  very  valuaUe  quality  to  rely  upon.  There 
is  no  slur  cast  upon  assistants  in  this,  nor  is  any  incapacity 
presupposed.  If  librarianship  is  to  rank  with  the  other 
professions,  it  must  fall  into  line  with  them  in  its  purely 
material  aspects,  and  articled  pupils,  who  are  now  only 
rarely  to  be  met  with,  will  then  be  the  rule.  Any  amalga* 
mat  ion  between  these  two  Associations  would  in  the  future 
militate  against  the  value  of  the  work  of  both.  The  avowed 
object  of  the  Library  Association  is  not  the  benefit  of  the 
librarian,  but  the  promotion  of  the  library  movem^it ; 
and  barely  half  the  members  find  emplo3mient  in  public 


590  THE  LITERARY  YEAR-BOOK       [part  ii. 

libraries,  although  the  whole  number  is,  of  course,  actively 
interested  in  them.  The  Assistants*  Association,  on  the 
other  hand,  if  it  is  to  receive  the  necessary  support,  must 
watch  vigilantly  over  the  interests  of  its  members. 

This  wide  constitution  of  the  Library  Association  has 
been  the  subject  of  much  controversy.  Although  there  is 
no  doubt  that  there  is  something  fascinating  in  the  present 
constitution,  and  that  the  influence  of  the  Association  is 
immeasurably  increased  by  it,  there  are  many  members 
of  the  opinion  that  the  particular  interest  of  librarians 
might  well  receive  more  attention  than  they  do  at  present. 
The  object  might  be  obtained  by  the  formation  of  a  sub- 
sidiary association  without  any  particular  bias.  At  all 
events,  it  would  be  a  matter  for  great  regret  if  the  Associa- 
tion receded  anything  from  its  present  high  standard. 

A  question  very  far-reaching  in  its  effects  upon  London 
libraries  is  the  proposal  of  Captain  FitzRoy  Hemphill. 
Although  that  proposal  is  only  an  unofficial  suggestion, 
it  appears  to  be  nevertheless  in  accordance  with  the  prob- 
able course  of  events.  Centralization  in  regard  to  public 
libraries  is  no  new  thing  :  it  has  been  mooted  time  and 
again.  Perhaps  the  best  form,  however,  is  that  of  a 
Governmental  Department  for  libraries,  working  on  some- 
what similar  lines  to  the  Board  of  Education.  Captain 
Hemphill's  prognostication  takes  the  form  of  a  Central 
London  Council  absorbing  the  whole  of  the  boroughs  and 
Borough  Councils  of  Greater  London.  Jn  the  absence  of 
any  detailed  particulars  it  is  difficult  to  offer  any  criticism 
or  even  to  prophecy  regarding  its  probable  effect.  There 
is,  however,  no  doubt  that  any  such  centralization  of  con- 
trol would  promote  greater  uniformity  of  the  Metropolitan 
libraries.  One  classification  for  the  whole  of  the  libraries 
in  Greater  London  would,  no  doubt,  follow  the  measure 
in  course  of  time  ;  general  interchangeability  of  tickets 
would  also  follow,  together  with  a  uniform  code  of  rules  ; 
and  not  least,  probably  a  system  of  supply  from  all  London 
to  any  individual  library  would  be  instituted.  It  would 
thus  be  possible  to  have  a  much  larger  selection  of  scarce 
or  expensive  books,  seeing  that  duplicates  of  so  many  other 
books  would  not  be  required  in  every  borough,  as  they 
are  at  present.     It  is  possible  that  this  scheme  might  tie 


PART  II.]  PUBLIC  LIBRARIES  591 

preferable  to  that  suggested  by  Mr.  McKillop  at  the  Annual 
Meeting.  The  proposal  then  made  provided  for  the  forma- 
tion of  a  Central  London  Library,  from  which  books  would 
be  issued  on  loan  to  any  of  the  public  libraries  of  the 
Metropolis.^ 

Juvenile  and  school  libraries  are  still  receiving  as  much 
consideration,  and  it  is  safe  to  say  that  most  of  the  larger 
libraries  erected  during  the  past  year  have  made  adequate 
provision  for  younger  readers  and  borrowers.  The  gener- 
ally accepted  practice,  however,  is  to  separate  the  juvenile 
department  from  the  ordinary  lending  and  reference 
libraries.  Islington  is  something  of  an  example  in  this 
respect.  Not  only  will  each  of  the  five  libraries  be  pro- 
vided with  juvenile  lending  libraries  and  reading-rooms, 
but  special  desks  for  the  preparation  of  home-work  and 
other  serious  objects,  with  all  the  reference-books  a  scholar 
is  likely  to  require  close  at  hand,  are  to  be  provided.  In 
addition  to  providing  for  child  readers,  most  of  the  newer 
libraries  contain  lecture-rooms. 

The  children's  reading-room  and  the  juvenile  lending 
library  are  quite  distinct  from  the  school  library.  During 
the  past  year  school  libraries  have  not  shown  so  much 
development  as  many  of  the  other  branches  of  library 
activity.  This  is  due  in  a  large  measure  to  the  imcertain 
condition  of  the  law  relating  to  education  and  educational 
expenditure  in  the  latter  part  of  1905  and  for  more  than 
six  months  in  1906.  The  usual  system  of  school  libraries 
consists  of  placing  a  collection  of  books,  necessarily  smaQ, 
in  each  school  for  a  period  varying  in  different  places  from 
three  months  to  a  year.  The  success  of  this  depends  en- 
tirely upon  the  efforts  of  the  teachers.  Although  this  plan 
is  eminently  preferable  to  no  libraries,  it  would  appear 
that  the  b^t  results  will  not  be  obtained  imtil  the  con- 
nection between  the  library  and  the  schools  is  much  closer. 
Formerly  it  was  doubtful  if  the  local  education  authority 
could  make  a  grant  in  aid  of  school  libraries  without  having 
actual  property  in  the  books — at  all  events,  the  Board  of 
Education  refused  to  sanction  the  expenditure  on  any 
other  lines.  But  under  Section  22  (2)  of  the  New  Educa- 
tion Act  it  appears  possible  to  do  this.  A  rought  outline 
of  the  suggested  scheme  is  given  here.    All  property  in 


59-2  THE  LITERARY  YEAR-BOOK       [part  ii. 

the  books,  cases,  and  other  material  is  vested  in  the  public 
library  authority.  Books  loaned  to  the  schools  are  actually 
part  of  the  library  stock,  appear  in  the  library  catalogues, 
and  when  not  at  the  schools  are  in  circulation  at  the  library. 
By  this  means  the  small  number  of  books  the  school 
authority  could  purchase  is  augmented  by  all  those  in  the 
library  suitable  for  juvenile  readers,  and  the  books  at  the 
library  are  increased  by  the  additional  books  purchased 
with  a  portion  of  the  grant  from  the  local  education 
authority,  which  is  an  integral  part  of  the  scheme.  This 
grant  is  in  the  nature  of  a  payment  for  something  done, 
and  is  to  be  based  in  respect  to  its  amount  upon  either 
the  number  of  scholars  on  the  rolls  or  upon  the  number  of 
books  issued ;  the  latter  method  is  to  be  preferred.  The 
issue  of  books  must  take  place  weekly  or  fortnightly  in 
direct  loan  from  the  library.  The  particular  method  must 
depend  upon  the  local  conditions,  but  it  may  be  done  either 
through  the  teachers  or  by  an  assistant  from  the  library. 
Until  the  Board  of  Education  see  their  way  to  giving  their 
sanction  to  a  scheme  similar  to  this  in  its  conception,  the 
present  somewhat  unsatisfactory  method  will  continue. 
The  proposed  scheme  permits  of  a  much  larger  selection, 
more  economical  working,  a  selection  of  books  on  current 
topics,  and,  above  all,  it  cements  the  connection  of  the 
children  with  the  library.  And  it  would  make  the  visits 
of  the  school  children  to  the  library  of  living  interest. 
These  half -hour  visits  and  the  accompanying  talks  are 
becoming  more  and  more  popular  year  by  year,  and  point 
unequivocally  to  the  possibilities  of  the  closer  co-operation 
between  the  schools  and  the  library. 

During  the  year  the  National  Home-Reading  Union  has 
made  an  endeavour,  on  the  lines  suggested  at  the  Cam- 
bridge Conference  in  1905,  to  promote  co-operative  work- 
ing between  the  Union  and  the  public  library. 

A  code  of  cataloguing  rules,  founded  on  that  submitted 
to  the  Conference  at  Newcastle  in  1904,  has  now  been 
completed,  and  probably  will  be  published  early  in  1907. 
These  rules  are  the  result  of  some  three  years'  work  of  the 
Committees  apj)ointed  by  the  Library  Association  and  the 
American  Library  Association,  and  will  apply  to  this 
country  as  well  as  to  America. 


PART  II.]  PUBLIC  LIBRARIES  593 

The  Exhibition  of  the  Best  Books  of  1905-6  at  the 
Bradford  Conference  was  the  most  successful  that  has  yet 
been  held.  Many  of  the  books  there  exhibited  were  those 
which  it  would  be  impossible  for  a  librarian  to  see  and 
examine  without  purchasing,  as  they  are  not  stocked  by 
the  booksellers.  The  classified  list  of  these  books  is  also 
an  excellent  production.  Although  at  the  time  of  writing 
it  is  something  of  a  secret,  it  can  scarcely  remain  so  long, 
that  next  year's  (1907)  exhibition  will  surpass  that  of  1906 
as  much  as  that  of  1906  excelled  those  that  preceded  it. 
The  preparations  for  a  permanent  exhibition  of  Best  Books, 
on  the  premises  of  the  Library  Supply  Company,  are  com- 
pleted, and  probably  at  the  time  of  publication  the  shelves 
will  be  well  filled,  as,  with  some  three  or  four  exceptions, 
the  larger  publishing  houses  are  favourably  disposed 
towards  libraries.  Some  time  in  the  future  it  is  hoped 
to  establish  duplicate  exhibitions  in  some  of  the  pro- 
vincial centres.  These  exhibitions  are  of  the  utmost 
importance  to  librarians,  as  there  are  only  about  four 
booksellers  in  London  who  stock  technical  books  to  any 
large  extent. 

A  rendez\'Ous  has  also  been  established  at  the  Library 
Supply  Company  for  those  librarians  who  wish  to  arrange 
meetings  with  others  in  the  Metropolis. 

It  is  difficult,  if  not  impossible,  to  say  what  advances 
have  been  made  in  the  construction  of  public  libraries. 
One  fact,  however,  it  is  impossible  to  dispute — ^viz.,  the 
attention  that  is  being  paid  to  the  subject  by  architects. 
WTiile  mentioning  this,  it  is  necessary  to  call  attention  to 
the  very  slipshod  manner  in  which  some  library  committees 
issue  conditions  of  the  competitions.  It  is  a  reprehensible 
practice  for  a  library  committee  to  struggle  through  the 
initial  stages  without  expert  advice,  trusting  to  the  ability 
of  the  various  architects  to  give  them  something,  while 
they  do  not  teU  the  competitors  what  that  "  something  "  is. 

The  next  Conference,  of  1907,  will  take  place  at  Glasgow. 
It  is  believed  that  the  extensive  library  system  of  the  city, 
made  possible  by  Mr.  Carnegie's  donation,  will  then  be 
sufficiently  completed  to  afEord  a  valuable  object-lesson. 


THE  LIBRARY  ASSOCIATION 

The  Library  Association  was  founded  in  1877  as  the 
Library  Association  of  the  United  Kingdom,  and  incor- 
porated as  **  The  Library  Association  "  by  Royal  charter 
in  1898. 

Amongst  other  work  for  the  good  of  the  Library  Move- 
ment, the  objects  of  the  Association  include  the  promotion 
of  the  better  administration  of  libraries  ;  of  the  adoption 
of  the  Public  Libraries  Acts  ;  of  the  union  of  all  persons 
engaged  or  interested  in  Ubrary  work  by  holding  con- 
ferences and  meetings  for  the  discussion  of  bibliographical 
questions  and  matters  affecting  libraries  or  their  regulation 
or  management,  or  otherwise ;  the  oversight  and  promotion 
of  legislation  ;  and  examinations  in  librarianship,  and  the 
issue  of  certificates  of  membership.  In  connection  with 
the  last  the  Association  has  instituted  classes  and  examina- 
tions, and  the  efforts  of  the  Education  Committee  are 
meeting  with  the  greatest  success. 

Monthly  meetings  are  held  throughout  the  session  from 
November  to  June,  and  a  General  Meeting  takes  place 
annually,  usually  at  the  end  of  August  or  at  the  beginning 
of  September. 

The  Record,  a  monthly  magazine,  is  the  official  orfi[an 
of  the  Association,  and  many  useful  and  important  publi- 
cations have  been  issued  by  the  Association. 

The  Association  is  watching  over  the  whole  question 
of  the  improvement  of  book  production,  and  the  Sound 
Leather  Committee  published  a  valuable  report  on  leather 
for  bookbinding  in  1905.  Already  many  leather  manu- 
facturers have  risen  to  the  occasion  and  produced  leathers 
of  the  necessary  standard.  President,  1906-1907 :  Sir 
William  H.  Bailey.  Hon,  Treasurer :  Henry  R.  Tedder, 
F.S.A.,  The  Athenaeum,  Pall  Mall,  s.w.  Hon,  Solicitor: 
H.  W.  FovARGUE,  Town  Hall,  Eastbourne.  Hon,  Secre* 
tary :  L.  Stanley  Jast,  Pubhc  Library,  Town  HaU, 
Croydon.  Assist.  Secretary :  Mrs.  Reilly,  Whitcomb 
House,  Whitcomb  Street,  s.w.  Offices  :  Whitcomb  House, 
Whitcomb  Street,  Pall  Mall  East,  s.w.  Monthly  meetings 
are  held  at  20,  Hanover  Square,  W. 

594 


PREFATORY  NOTE 

The  Library  Section  of  the  present  issue  of  The  Literary 
Year-Book  has  been  prepared  with  the  sanction  and  with 
the  assistance  of  the  Library  Association.  While  the 
greatly  increased  information  will  be  of  the  utmost  value, 
it  is  to  be  regretted  that  there  are  some  hbraries  from  which 
no  returns  have  been  received.  By  far  the  greater  nimiber 
of  these  is  made  up  of  very  small  districts  in  which  the 
Acts  have  been  adopted  without  having  been  put  into 
effect.  How  far  this  position  is  tenable  either  l^ally  or 
morally  is  a  matter  for  grave  consideration.  Owing  to 
the  late  adoption  of  the  return  by  the  Association,  the 
whole  of  the  work  this  year  has  been  done  in  upwards  of 
a  month,  and  it  was  foimd  impossible  to  obtain  definite 
information  regarding  these  hbraries.  In  the  next  issue, 
however,  this  defect  will  be  remedied.  It  is  scarcely  con- 
ceivable that  the  hbrarians  of  those  places  where  a  library 
is  in  operation  can  have  appreciated  the  importance  of 
supporting  the  work  of  the  Association  in  this  respect. 
It  is  hoped  that  any  other  errors  which  may  have  crept 
in  will  be  ascribed  to  the  shortness  of  the  time  available 
for  the  present  issue,  which  has  been  prepared  within  a 
few  weeks.  There  is  httle  doubt,  nevertheless,  that  the 
following  returns  form  the  most  complete  list  of  libraries 
— at  all  events,  pubhc  libraries — ^yet  published. 

To  make  reference  to  the  returns  easy  a  series  of  uniform 
abbreviations  has  been  kept  throughout,  and  the  particu- 
lars are  given  in  the  same  rotation.  For  the  same  reason, 
and  not  because  of  any  desire  to  increase  their  importance, 
public  hbraries  precede  other  hbraries.  In  accordance 
with  the  expressed  wish  of  many  hbrarians,  a  specially 
prepared  form  will  be  sent  to  the  great  University,  colle- 
giate, and  proprietary  hbraries,  for  future  issues. 

Where  no  statement  is  made  it  must  be   understood 

595 


596  THE  LITERARY  YEAR-BOOK      [part  ii. 

that  no  information  was  given,  or  that,  as  in  the  case  of 
opening  on  Sunday,  the  answer  would  be  in  the  negative. 
The  order  of  the  replies  is  as  follows  :  Name  of  library  ; 
Year  of  adoption  or  foundation  ;  Librarian  ;  Rate  in  the 
pound ;  Is  the  Library  exempt  from  either  rates  or  taxes, 
or  both  ?  Total  income  from  all  sources,  including  product 
of  rate  ;  Number  of  assistants,  male,  female,  caretakers  ; 
Number  of  volumes  in  Lending  Library,  Reference  Library  ; 
Number  of  borrowers'  or  members*  tickets  in  force ;  Number 
of  volumes  issued  annually  in  Lending  Library,  Reference 
Library  ;  Method  of  issue  in  Lending  Library,  Reference 
Library ;  System  of  classification,  Lending  Library,  Refer- 
ence Library  ;  Catalogue  of  Lending  Library,  Reference 
Library  ;  Is  betting  news  obliterated  ?  Is  the  Library  open 
on  Sundays  ?  Is  a  Library  periodical  published  ?  Have  the 
Library  a  juvenile  collection,  a  children's  reading-room, 
lectures,  school  libraries,  delivery  stations,  or  any  other 
special  collections  or  features  ?  Has  the  Library  Committee 
full  delegation  of  powers  under  the  Acts  ?  Number  of 
Branches ;  Total  volumes  in  branches  ;  Total  staff  at 
branches  ;  Total  issues  at  branches. 
The  following  abbreviations  are  uniform  throughout : 


Inc. 

=  Income. 

Class. 

=  Classification. 

Assist. 

Assistants, 

or  Assistant. 

Cat 

=  Catalogue. 

M. 

Male. 

Diet. 

=  Dictionary. 

F. 

=  Female. 

ObUt. 

=  Obliterated. 

C. 

=  Caretaker. 

Op. 

=  Open. 

Vs. 

—  Volumes. 

Sund. 

=  Sunday. 

L. 

—  Lending. 

Juv. 

=  Juvenile. 

R. 

=  Reference. 

Coll. 

=  Collection. 

Iss. 

=  Issues. 

Com. 

=  Committee.

LONDOM. 

The  London  Government  Act,  in  some  cases,  formed  Boroughs 
of  several  existing  Vestries.  The  Libraries  so  amalgamated  are 
branches  in  the  usual  acceptance  of  the  term,  and  are  sometimes 
administered  by  sub-committees ;  but  the  practice  varies  so  much 
in  the  different  Boroughs  that  it  has  been  found  impossible  in  the 
present  issue  of  the  Year-book  to  give  uniform  details  of  all 
the  Libraries  in  these  districts. 

British  Museam  Reading  Room,  Great  Russell  Street.  Blooms- 
bur\-,  w.c.  (1753).  The  number  ol  books  cannot  be  stated 
exactly,  probably  two  millions. 

Admission. — This  is  granted  (to  apphcants  over  twenty- 
one  years  of  age)  on  receipt  of  a  recommendation  by  a  house- 
holder who  is  personally  acquainted  with  the  apphcant. 
The  particular  purpose  for  which  admisaon  is  sought  must 
also  be  indicatc^d  in  the  letter  of  application.  This  must  be 
sent  by  post  two  days  at  least  before  admission  is  required. 
The  ticket  is  usually  for  three  or  six  months.  When  asking 
for  renewal,  it  is  necessary  to  return  the  old  ticket.  Tickets 
must  be  returned  when  no  longer  required.  Apphcants 
under  twenty-one  years  of  age  require  the  Trustees'  special 
permission- 

All  tickets  are  strictly  non-transferable,  and  must  be  shown, 
if  required,  before  entering  the  room.  For  the  Print  Room 
a  separate  ticket  is  required,  subject  to  the  same  conditioiis 
as  to  its  granting,  etc..  as  for  the  Reading  Room. 

No  sticks  or  nmbrdlas  are  permitted  in  the  rooms  ;  over* 
coats  should  also  be  left  with  the  cloak-room  ofi^daL 

Rooms. — Besides  the  Newspaper  Room  (on  the  right  of 
the  main  entrance),  which  also  contains  the  London  Gazette, 
Journals  of  the  House  of  Commons,  and  other  PuHamentary 
papers,  and  the  Print  Room  (open  from  10  a.m.  till  5  p.m.), 
there  is  the  Rotunda.  The  lattei  is  the  BoauUiis  Room 
(For  Hours,  see  p.  637). 

Application  for  Books. — Round  the  central  desk, 
occupied  by  the  Superintendent  and  his  attendants,  will  be 
seen  boxes  of  printed  shps.  These  are  filled  in,  as  indicated, 
from  the  (Catalogues,  the  chief  items  to  note  being  the  correct 
press-mark  of  the  book,  and  the  nnmb^  of  the  reader's  seat. 


636  THE  LITERARY  YEAR-BOOK      [part  ii. 

LONDON  {continued)— 

The  other  particulars — i.e.,  date  of  publication,  title  and 
author  of  the  book — are  also,  however,  of  importance,  and 
not  to  be  omitted  by  those  desiring  to  be  served  without 
delay.  The  slips  are  placed  in  baskets  at  the  centre  counter, 
the  reader  returning  to  his  desk  to  await  the  books,  a  matter 
usually  of  between  ten  and  thirty  minutes,  according  to  the 
pressure  of  work  and  the  portion  of  the  library  in  which  the 
books  are  stored.  No  applications  are  received  after 
3.30  p.m.  in  January,  February,  November,  and  December; 
4.30  p.m.  in  March  and  October;  5.30  p.m.  in  April  and 
September ;  and  6.30  p.m.  in  the  summer  months.  When 
reading  is  over  for  the  day  the  reader  restores  the  books  to 
the  centre  desk,  receiving  in  exchange  the  slips,  which,  unless 
he  intends  using  the  same  book  or  books  again  within  the  next 
few  days,  are  best  destroyed.  Readers  are  held  responsible 
for  books  until  the  sUps  have  been  redelivered  to  them. 

Kept  Books. — Books  are  kept,  by  request,  subject  to  no 
other  reader  putting  in  a  ticket  for  them  during  the  absence 
of  the  reader  for  whom  they  are  put  aside.  In  each  book 
a  slip  of  paper  must  be  placed,  across  the  top  of  which  (so 
as  to  be  legible  without  opening  the  book)  are  written  the 
date  and  reader's  name.  There  are  special  baskets  at  Bar  A 
and  Bar  T  for  sUps  of  "  kept "  books,  and  the  latter  are 
usually  brought  round  within  a  few  minutes. 

The  Bar. — It  is  sometimes  impossible  for  a  reader  to 
spend  any  time  at  the  Reading  Room  until  after  the  latest 
hour  for  putting  in  application  slips  in  the  ordinary  way. 
In  such  cases  it  is  permitted  to  send  the  slips,  filled  in  before- 
hand, by  post,  to  be  deUvered  at  the  Museum,  some  hours 
before  the  books  are  actually  wanted.  Instead  of  filling  in  any 
desk-number  the  applicant  marks  Bar  A  or  Bar  T,  and  the 
books  are  kept  for  him  in  the  cages  on  either  side  of  the  central 
desk  until  called  for  later  in  the  day.  "  Kept "  books,  if  not 
called  for  within  a  day  or  two,  are  returned  to  the  shelves. 

Reference  Shelves. — In  addition  to  the  bulk  of  the 
collection,  which  is  only  available  as  indicated  in  the  fore- 
going remarks,  there  are  on  the  shelves  on  the  ground  floor 
of  the  Rotunda  itself  some  20,000  reference  volumes,  ranging 
through  history,  geography  and  travel,  and  every  science, 
down  to  such  works  of  reference  as  encyclopaedias,  lexicons, 
directories,  and  the  like.  These  the  reader  is  permitted  to 
take  down  for  himself,  but  must  replace  on  the  proper  shelf 
when  he  has  finished  using  them.  With  regard  to  the 
encyclopaedias,  dictionaries,  etc.,  these  must  be  returned  to 
the  shelves  as  soon  as  done  with,  and  in  some  cases  must  be 
consulted  at  the  shelf.  In  Continental  Ubraries  readers  are 
not  permitted  to  carry  off  to  their  own  desks  reference  works 
of  this  description. 

Catalogues. — The  Author  Catalogue,  stretching  in 
double  row  around  the  central  desk,  is  now,  in  its  printed 


PART  n.]  LffiRARIES  637 

LOMDOM  {continued) — 

form,  ccmdensed  to  about  960  vK^ames.  In  the  way  of 
Class  Catalogues,  which  for  the  most  part  exist  in  the 
unrealized  dreams  of  successive  generations  of  fibrarians,  we 
have  Fortescne's  exceedin^y  nsefnl  Subject  Index,  and 
Pbole's  Ukewise  valuable  Iin>EX  to  Periodicai^.  A  new 
e^tion  of  Fortescne's  Subject  Index,  i  881-1900,  has  now 
been  printed.  These  aids  to  reference  are,  with  their 
respective  supplements,  to  be  found  next  the  central  desk. 
There  are,  in  suldition  to  these,  special  catalogues  oi  manu- 
scripts, music,  and  maps,  and  useful  biUiographies  on  every 
subject  are  to  be  found  in  the  cases  at  the  end  of  the 
readers'  desks. 

Tracing. — Application  must  be  made  to  the  Superinten- 
dent for  pemussKHL. 

The  Beading  Room  is  open  on  week-days  (except  the  first 
four  days  in  March  and  S^tember,  Good  Friday,  Christmas 
Day,  and  days  appointed  by  authority),  from  9  a.m.  to  7  p.m., 
throughout  the  year. 

The  Newspaper  Room,  which  is  under  the  same  regula- 
tions as  the  Reading  Room,  is  open  January,  February, 
10  a.111.  to  3.50  p.in. ;  March,  10  a.111.  to  4.50  p.m. ;  April  to 
August,  10  a.m.  to  5.50  pum. ;  September,  October,  10  a.m. 
to  4.50  p.111. ;  Novendier,  December,  10  a.m.  to  3.50  pum. 
The  Room  is  closed  on  Sundajrs,  Good  Friday,  Christmas 
Day,  and  the  first  four  week-days  of  March  and  September. 

The  Students'  Room,  Department  of  MSS»,  is  open 
from  10  a.ni.  to  5  p.in.,  it  being  required  that  all  MSS.  be 
given  up  at  4.45  p.m.  The  Room  is  dosed  at  certain  times, 
as  in  the  case  of  the  Reading  and  Newmiper  "BoonuL. 

The  Print  Room  and  the  Oriental  Students'  Room  are 
open  from  10  a.in.  to  5  p.m.

FOREIGN 

PARIS 

Bibliothdque  Nationale 

(58  Rue  Richelieu.) 

This  Library,  founded  in  1367,  contains  nearly  3,000,000  volumes, 
250,000  maps,  and  102,000  manuscripts. 

Admission  is  sought  in  a  letter  to  the  Administrateur  GSniral, 
setting  forth  the  nature  of  the  applicant's  work,  his  name,  pro- 
fession, and  abode.  Applications  from  foreigners  should  be 
accompanied  by  a  recommendation  from  their  Embassy,  or  from 
some  responsible  person  known  to  the  Administrator.  Such 
ticket  of  admission,  when  granted,  is  signed  by  the  recipient,  and 
is  strictly  non-transferable. 

Application  for  Books. — On  entering  the  Salle  de  Travail,  each 
reader  is  handed  a  bulletin  personnel,  which,  having  filled  in 
accurately  his  name  and  address,  and  the  number  of  his  seat,  he 
hands  to  the  Superintendent.  On  this  one  of  the  officials  enters 
the  names  of  all  books  taken  out  by  him,  and  he  is  not  allowed  to 
leave  the  room  until  he  has  handed  the  doorkeeper  this  bulletin 
with  the  official  rendu  stamped  against  every  book. 

Books  Kept. — ^Works  are  kept  by  request  for  twenty-four  hours, 
after  which  they  are  returned  to  the  shelves.  Should  any  other 
reader,  however,  apply  for  any  of  these  "  kept  "  books,  they  are  at 
his  disposal. 

There  is  a  Lending-out  branch  of  the  Bibliothique,  applying  only 
to  books  of  which  there  are  more  than  one  copy. 

All  the  rooms  are  closed  January  i  and  2,  Shrove  Tuesday,  Palm 
Sunday  to  Easter  Monday  (inclusive).  Ascension  Day,  Whit- 
Sunday  and  Monday,  July  14,  August  15,  November  i,  and  Christ- 
mas Day. 

BERUN 

Kdnigliehe  BibUothek 

(Platz  am  Opernhaus.) 

Founded  in  1661,  this  Library  has  about  1,228,000  volumes  and 
33,600  manuscripts.     Maps  not  counted. 

Admission. — ^This  is  freely  granted  on  application,  though  the 
Director  has  to  be  satisfied  as  to  the  applicant's  credentials.  Cards 
for  the  Reading-Room  only  do  not  include  the  use  of  the  Lending 
Library  ;  but  a  card  for  the  latter  gives  access  to  every  department. 

678 


PART  II.]  LIBRARIES  679 

Appiication  for  Books. — Order-forms  are  sold  at  the  Library 
at  the  rate  ol  twenty-five  ior  10  pfennig.  They  are  in  the  nature  of 
a  receipt  for  the  book  required,  of  which  the  applicant  has  to  fill  in 
all  the  usual  particulars  as  to  title,  date,  size,  &c.,  which  are  to  be 
got  from  the  Alphabetic  Catalogue. 

A  book  ( Vanmerkbuch)  is  kept  in  the  Lending  Library  for  the  use 
of  those  desiring  to  secure  any  book  as  soon  as  returned  by  another 
borrower. 

The  Readixg-Room  {Lesesaal). — ^The  reader  can  take  down  all 
books  available  on  the  shelves,  but  must  return  them,  when  done 
wth  to  the  sbdves.  The  same  appHcation-forms  (see  above)  arc 
available,  but  are  marked  "  Lesesaal J*^ 

Books  Kept. — Books  can  be  kept  from  day  to  day  for  not  longer 
than  three  weeks.  Should  the  reader  stay  away  for  three  consecutive 
days,  the  book  is  returned  to  the  shdf.  Those  desiring  to  keep 
books  in  this  way  place  in  them  a  slip  on  which  is  written  their 
name  and  the  date. 

The  Library  is  open  on  week-days  (with  the  exception  of  the 
customary  public  holidays,  the  Saturday  before  Easter,  the 
Saturday  before  Whitsun,  and  December  24)  from  (i)  Lending 
Branch,  9  a.m.  to  6  p.m.  ;  (2)  Reading-Room,  9  a,m.  to  9  p.m. 

Staff. — 71  officials,  42  a.ssistants  and  39  subordinate  officials. 

ExpEXDiTCRE. — 649,474  marks  per  annum,  of  which  409,700 
marks  are  for  establishment  and  146,400  marks  ior  increasing  and 
maintaining  the  collection  of  books. 


HOLLAHD 

Royal  Ubrary  (KoniiiUijke  Bibliotiieek) 

(The  Hague.) 

This  Library,  founded  in  1798,  has  a  large  number  of  incunabula, 
codices  and  illuminated  medieval  manuscripts.  Religion,  History, 
Literature,  Art,  Law,  and  Politics  are  well  represented.  The  Library 
also  contains  30,000  pamphlets  illustratmg  the  history  of  the 
Netherlands,  700  v(^umes  of  publications  by  Elsevier  aiui  works 
by  Aldine,  Estienne,  and  Flantyn  in  great  number.  Of  the  6,000 
maTiUscripts,  600  relate  to  theology  and  the  history  of  the  Qinrch. 
There  is  a  "  Book  of  Hours  "  which  bdonged  to  Philip  the  Good 
(fifteenth  century),  containing  178  miniatures^  The  oldest  illumi- 
nated manuscript,  a  copy  of  the  Gospels,  dates  from  the  ninth 
centurv*.  The  Library  also  possesses  a  copy  ol  the  third  Shake- 
speare foho.  Nearly  all  recent  works  published  in  Hcdland  are 
received  by  the  Library,  as  wdl  as  important  foreign  books.  The 
annual  budget  for  the  acquisition  of  books,  etc.,  is  nearly  ^^3,000. 
The  Reading-Room  is  open  from  10  to  4.  Books  are  lent  <hi  per- 
sonal appUcation,  or  sent  by  post  to  applicants  who  are  well  known 
or  recommended. 

Librarian,  Dr.  W.  G.  C.  Byvanck.

AMSTERDAM

THE    NOBEL   FOUNDATION   AND 
THE   NOBEL   PRIZES 

(Repninted  from  The  Author  with  the  kind  permission 
of  the  Incorporated  Society  of  Authors.) 

Alfred  Berxhard  Nobel,  the  inventor  of  dynamite,  died  in  1896, 
leaving  the  residue  of  his  great  fortune,  estimated  at  about 
;£2, 000.000  sterUng,  for  the  foundation  of  five  annual  prizes,  to  be 
awarded  for  the  most  important  discoveries  in  physics,  chemistry, 
and  physiology  or  medicme,  for  the  most  remarkable  work  of  an 
idealist  tendency,  and  for  the  greatest  service  rendered  to  the  cause 
of  |>eace  during  the  year.     The  value  of  each  prize  is  about  j^8,ooo. 

An  official  French  translation  of  the  statutes  and  regulations 
relating  to  the  Nobel  Foundation  has  been  issued.  The  original 
documents  received  the  sanction  of  the  King  of  Sweden  on  June  29, 
1900. 

It  v^ill  be  seen  that  the  text  of  the  will  has  received  a  liberal 
interpretation.  In  addition  to  the  prizes,  provision  is  made  for  the 
estabUshment  of  Nobel  institutes  where  researches  may  be  carried 
on.  and  of  special  funds  from  which  grants  may  be  given  to  promote 
the  objects  which  the  founder  had  at  heart ;  and  both  institutes 
and  special  funds  are  likely,  to  say  the  least,  to  be  quite  as  productive 
of  good  as  the  great  prizes. 

It  will  be  noticed  that  the  benefits  of  the  foundation  are  to  be 
open  to  all  nationalities  without  distinction.  No  restriction  of  sex 
is  mentioned  in  the  statutes. 

The  Nobel  Foundation  is  based  on  the  will  of  Dr.  Alfred  Bemhard 
Nobel,  dated  November  27,  1895,  of  which  the  following  passage 
contains  the  stipulations  with  regard  to  the  foundation  :  "  The 
residue  of  my  realizable  property  left  after  my  death  shall  be  disposed 
of  as  follows:  The  capit^d,  invested  in  safe  investments  by  the 
executors  of  my  will,  shall  constitute  a  fund  of  which  the  interest 
shall  be  distributed  annually  as  a  reward  to  such  persons  as  have 
rendered  to  humanity  the  greatest  services  during  the  preceding 
year.  The  total  amount  shall  be  divided  into  five  equal  parts, 
and  allotted,  one  [part]  to  the  person  who  has  made  the  most 
important  discovery  or  invention  in  the  ph3rsical  sciences ;  another 
[p^art]  to  the  person  who  has  made  the  most  important  discovery 
or  the  greatest  improvement  in  chemistry ;  the  third  to  the  author 
of  the  most  important  discovery  in  the  domain  of  phjrsiology  or 
medicine  ;  the  fourth  to  the  person  who  has  produced  the  most 
remarkable  Uterary  work  of  an  idealist  tendency ;  and,  finally,  the 
fifth  to  the  person  who  has  done  most,  or  laboured  best,  for  the 
cause  of  fraternity  among  different  peoples,  for  the  suppression  or 
reduction  of  standmg  armies,  or  for  the  formation  and  promotiofi  of 
peace  congresses. 

''  The  prizes  shall  be  awarded  as  follows :  For  phjrsics  and 
chemistry  by  the  Swedish  Academy  of  Sciences  ;  for  work  in  phyat- 

681 


682  THE  LITERARY  YEAR-BOOK      [part  ii. 

ology  and  medicine  by  the  Caroline  Institute  of  Stockholm  ;  for 
literature  by  the  Stockholm  Academy  ;  and,  finally,  for  the  cause  of 
peace,  by  a  commission  of  five  members  elected  by  the  Norwegian 
Storthing.  It  is  my  express  desire  that  in  the  award  of  the  prizes 
no  account  shall  be  taken  of  nationality,  so  that  each  prize  may  go 
to  the  most  worthy,  whether  he  be  Scandinavian  or  not." 

An  agreement  was  made  with  certain  heirs  to  a  9maller  portion 
of  Dr.  Nobel's  property,  by  which  they  renounced  the  inheritance 
for  themselves  and  their  heirs,  on  the  express  condition  that — 

(a)  The  general  statutes  regulating  the  award  of  the  prizes  by  the 
competent  authorities  should  be  drawn  up  in  concert  with  a 
delegate  representing  the  Robert  Nobel  family,  and  sub- 
mitted for  approval  to  the  King  of  Sweden. 

(6)  That  certain  permanent  regulations,  stated  below,  should  be 
passed  with  regard  to  the  award  of  the  prizes. 

I. — ^The  Statutes. 
(i)  The  General  Fund, 

1 .  The  General  Fund  is  to  be  controlled  by  a  council  of  administra- 
tion, composed  of  five  members,  of  Swedish  nationality,  sitting  at 
Stockholm.  The  president  is  to  be  nominated  by  the  King  of 
Sweden.  The  four  other  members  are  to  be  elected  by  a  body  of 
fifteen  representatives,  chosen  by  the  corporate  bodies  named  in  the 
will.     (See  2,  below.) 

A  deputy  is  also  to  be  elected -to  act  in  the  absence  of  the  president, 
and  two  deputies  are  to  be  elected  for  each  of  the  other  members  of 
the  council. 

The  members  and  deputies  will  be  elected  in  future  for  a  period  of 
two  years,  the  term  of  office  beginning  on  May  i.  But  two  of  the 
members  of  the  first  council,  selected  by  lot,  will  retire,  after  a  single 
year  of  office,  so  that  in  future  two  may  retire  annually. 

The  council  will  elect  one  of  its  members  as  "  director  and  adminis- 
trator "  {directeur-g^rant). 

2.  Of  the  fifteen  representatives,  six  are  to  be  chosen  by  the 
Academy  of  Sciences  (for  the  two  sections  of  physics  and  chemistry), 
and  three  are  to  be  chosen  by  each  of  the  other  corporate  bodies. 
In  addition,  the  Academy  of  Sciences  will  nominate  four  deputy- 
representatives,  and  each  of  the  other  corporate  bodies  two,  to  act 
for  representatives  prevented  from  so  doing. 

The  representatives  will  hold  office  for  two  years.  They  will  elect 
a  president  from  among  their  number.  Nine  representatives  will 
form  a  quorum. 

In  certain  cases  the  representatives  may  be  called  on  to  control 
the  management  of  the  foundation  by  the  council. 

3.  The  functions  of  the  council  include  the  payment  of  the  money 
value  of,  prizes  to  the  prize-winners  and  the  payment  on  requisition 
of  all  expenses  incurred  in  the  award  of  prizes,  the  payment  of  sums 
due  to  the  Nobel  Institutes,  and  of  sums  required  for  all  other 
purposes. 


PART  II.]  THE  NOBEL  PRIZES  683 

4.  One-tenth  of  the  net  annual  revenue  from  the  General  Fund 
is  to  be  added  to  the  capital ;  and  the  interest  on  sums  for  prizes 
not  awarded  is  also  to  be  added  to  the  capital  till  such  sums  have 
been  returned  to  the  General  Fund  or  transferred  to  a  special  fund. 

(2)  The  Nobel  Prizes. 

1 .  The  prizes  are  to  be  awarded  as  a  rule  annually. 

2.  For  a  work  to  be  admitted  to  compete  for  a  prize  it  must  have 
been  printed  and  pubUshed. 

3.  In  order  to  be  admitted  to  the  competition,  each  work  must  be 
proposed  in  writing  by  some  person  or  body  of  persons  qualified  to 
do  so.  Regulations  defining  the  categories  of  persons  qualified  to 
propose  works  for  the  competitions  are  given  below  under  the 
special  regulations  for  the  various  sections.  No  person  may  propose 
his  own  work. 

4.  Each  proposal  must  be  accompanied  by  the  written  works  and 
documents  on  which  the  proposal  is  based,  and  by  a  written  state- 
ment in  support  of  the  proposal.  The  corporate  body  with  whom 
the  award  of  a  prize  lies  shall  be  dispensed  from  proceeding  to  a 
detailed  examination  of  any  proposal  in  the  following  cases  :  (i)  If 
the  proposal  or  works  presented  are  not  written  either  in  one  of  the 
Scandinavian  languages,  or  in  English,  French,  German,  or  Latin ; 
(2)  if  the  majority  of  the  corporate  body  in  question  would  be 
required,  in  order  to  appreciate  the  proposed  work,  to  acquaint 
themselves  with  a  memoir  written  in  a  language  of  which  the 
interpretation  would  entail  special  difi&culties  or  considerable 
expense. 

5.  The  proposals  received  from  the  previous  ist  of  February  to 
the  I  St  of  February  in  each  year  shall  be  considered  together.  The 
will  prescribes  that  prizes  shall  be  awarded  for  work  done  "  in  the 
preceding  year,"  but  this  expression  is  to  be  taken  to  mean  "  that 
the  object  of  the  awards  shaU  be  [as  a  rule]  the  most  recent  fruit  of 
efforts  in  the  fields  designated  in  the  will,  but  [that  it  may  be]  older 
work  in  [exceptional]  cases  where  its  importance  has  only  recently 
been  demonstrated." 

6.  If  no  work  be  regarded  as  deserving  of  a  prize  in  any  section 
the  amount  of  the  prize  is  to  be  held  over  until  the  next  year ;  if 
the  prize  be  not  awarded  then,  the  money  may  be  either  returned  to 
the  General  Fund  by  the  corporate  body  concerned  or  invested  to 
form  a  special  fund  for  the  section,  the  income  from  which  is  to  be 
used  to  promote  the  chief  objects  of  the  founder,  otherwise  than  by 
the  award  of  a  prize.  (See  also  under  special  funds,  below.)  In 
accordance  with  an  agreement  made  with  certain  heirs  of  Nobel, 
referred  to  above,  it  is  stipulated,  for  all  future  time,  that  in  each 
section  a  prize  shall  be  awarded  at  least  once  in  each  consecutive 
period  of  five  years  ;  and  that  the  value  of  the  prize  given  shall  not 
be  less  than  sixty  per  cent.  (60  %)  of  the  total  amount  available  of 
the  prize,  and  that  it  shall  not  be  divided  into  more  than  three 
prizes.  The  present  statutes  provide  that  the  amount  of  the  prize 
may  be  equally  divided  between  two  works,  both  being  judged 
worthy  of  the  prize. 


684  THE  LITERARY  YEAR-BOOK      [part  ii. 

A  prize  may  be  awarded  to  the  joint  work  of  two  or  more  collabo- 
rators. ) 

The  corporate  bodies  are  allowed  to  decide  if  prizes  within  their 
respective  jurisdictions  may  or  may  not  be  award^  to  an  institution 
or  a  society. 

7.  The  four  prizes  for  physics,  chemistry,  literature,  and  medicine 
will  be  awarded  by  the  corporate  bodies  named  in  the  will,  after 
receiving  a  report  in  each  case  from  a  special  committee,  to  be  called 
a  Nobel  committee,  and  consisting  of  three  or  five  members  elected 
by  the  corporate  body  concerned.  The  **  Peace "  prize  will  be 
awarded  by  a  commissoin  of  the  Norwegian  Storthing  m  accordance 
with  the  terms  of  the  will.  Members  of  the  Nobel  committees  and 
of  the  commission  of  the  Storthing  may  be  of  foreign  nationaUty. 
In  special  cases  the  corporate  bodies  concerned  may  add  to  the 
Nobel  committees  additional  members  possessing  special  competence. 

Members  of  the  Nobel  committees  may  receive  a  suitable  re- 
muneration for  their  work, '  to  be  fixed  by  the  corporate  body 
concerned. 

8.  Against  the  decisions  with  regard  to  the  award  of  prizes  no 
appeal  can  be  made. 

If  in  the  deUberations  with  regard  to  the  award  any  difference  of 
opinion  should  arise,  it  shall  neither  be  mentioned  in  the  minutes 
of  the  proceedings  nor  otherwise  revealed  in  any  way. 

9.  A  solemn  assembly  will  be  held  on  the  loth  of  December  in  each 
year,  the  anniversary  of  the  death  of  the  founder,  at  which  the 
corporate  bodies  charged  with  the  award  of  the  prizes  will  publicly 
announce  their  decisions,  and  will  present  to  each  successful  candi- 
date an  order  for  the  amount  of  his  prize,  a  diploma,  and  a  gold 
medal  bearing  the  efi&gy  of  the  donor,  and  an  appropriate  inscription. 

10.  Each  prizeman  is  bound,  unless  he  is  prevented  from  domg  so 
{d  moins  d'empSchement),  to  deliver  a  public  lecture  on  the  subject  of 
the  work  for  which  the  prize  was  awarded.  The  lecture  in  the  case 
of  the  first  four  prizes  is  to  be  delivered  at  Stockholm,  in  the  case  of 
the  "  Peace  "  pnze  at  Chris tiania. 

II. — Special  Regulations  for  the  Section  of  Literature. 

1 .  The  corporate  body  for  the  section  is  the  Swedish  Academy. 
According  to  the  statutes,  the  term  "  literature  "  in  the  will  is 

intended  to  apply  not  only  to  purely  literary  works,  but  to  all  other 
works  possessing  from  their  form  and  style  Uterary  value. 

The  Nobel  Prize  in  Literature. 

2.  The  following  institutions  and  persons  are  to  be  entitled  to 
nominate  competitors  for  the  prizes,  in  accordance  with  the  statutes  : 

(i.)  Members  of  the  Swedish  Academy  (referred  to  hereafter  as 
"  the  Academy  *')  and  members  of  the  French  Academy 
and  the  Spanish  Academy,  which  resemble  this  Academy 
in  their  organization  and  aims. 

(ii.)  Members  of  the  Uterary  sections  of  other  Academies  and 
members  of  Uterary  societies  similar  to  Academies. 

(iu.)  University  professors  of  aesthetics,  Uterature,  and  history. 


PART  II.]  THE  NOBEL  PRIZES  685 

This  regulation  is  to  be  published  at  least  once  every  five  years  in 
an  official  journal  or  in  one  of  the  newspapers  most  widely  read  in 
the  three  Scandinavian  countries  and  the  principal  countries  of  the 
civilized  world. 

The  Nobel  Literary  Institute. 

3.  The  institute  will  include  an  important  library  devoted  chiefly 
to  modem  literature.  The  Academy  will  nominate  a  librarian,  with 
one  or  more  assistant  librarians,  and  also  a  number  of  qualified 
Uterary  assistants,  titular  and  supernumerary,  whose  duty  it  will  be 
to  draw  up  reports  on  questions  relating  to  the  prize  and  on  recent 
foreign  Uterature,  and  to  execute  such  translations  of  foreign  works 
as  may  be  required. 

The  institute  will  be  placed  under  the  supervision  of  an  inspector 
nominated  by  the  King  of  Sweden,  and  under  the  immediate  direc- 
tion of  one  of  the  members  of  the  Academy  nominated  specially  by 
this  body. 

The  Special  Fund  for  Literature, 

4.  The  Academy  has  power  to  use  the  special  fund  to  encourage, 
in  accordance  with  the  chief  objects  of  the  founder,  all  Uterary 
activity,  in  Sweden  and  abroad,  of  such  kind  as  may  be  regarded 
as  of  importance  to  civilization,  especially  in  the  intellectual  domain 
to  which  it  is  the  duty  of  the  Academy  to  devote  its  attention  and 
care.     (See  below.) 

III. — The  Corporate  Bodies  Mentioned  in  the  Will  of  the 

Founder. 

The  Royal  Academy  of  Sciences  {Kongl.  Vetenskaps  Akademien)  of 
Stockholm  was  founded  in  1739.  Its  present  statutes  bear  the  date 
July  13,  1850.  Its  object  is  to  encourage  the  sciences,  to  aid  in  their 
development,  and  to  spread  scientific  knowledge  by  means  of 
printed  pubUcations. 

The  King  of  Sweden  is  the  patron  of  the  Academy,  which  includes 
a  hundred  Swedish  and  Norwegian  and  seventy-five  foreign 
members.  The  national  members  are  divided  into  nine  sections,  as 
follows  : 

I.  Pure  Mathematics. 
II.  AppUed  Mathematics. 

III.  AppUed  Mechanics. 

IV.  Physical  Sciences. 

V.  Chemistry,  Geology,  and  Mineralogy. 
VI.  Botany  and  Zoology. 
VII.  The  Medical  Sciences. 
VIII.  Technology,  Economics,  and  Statistics. 
IX.  The  Sciences  and  Scientific  Professions  generally. 

The  president  of  the  Academy  is  elected  annuaUy,  and  there  are 
several  other  officers,  including  a  perpetual  secretary,  whose  especial 
business  it  is  to  manage  the  afiairs  of  the  Academy. 


686  THE  LITERARY  YEAR-BOOK     [part  ii. 

The  Swedish  Academy  {Svenska  Akademien)  of  Stockholm  was 
founded  on  March  20,  1786,  by  Gustavus  III.,  and  at  the  same  time 
received  its  statutes,  which  are  still  in  force.  It  is  devoted  to  litera- 
ture, including  both  prose  and  poetry  ;  and  the  chief  object  of  the 
Academy  is  to  labour  for  the  purity,  the  vigour,  and  the  elevation 
of  the  Swedish  language,  in  scientific  works,  and  especially  in  the 
various  branches  of  poetry  and  prose,  including  those  which  serve 
for  the  interpretation  of  religious  truths.  It  is  the  duty  of  the 
Academy  to  compile  a  dictionary  and  a  grammar  of  the  Swedish 
language,  and  to  publish  treatises  tending  to  strengthen  and  develop 
Uterary  taste  {le  bon  goiit).  The  Academy  awards  annual  prizes  for 
competitions  in  prose  and  poetry.  The  King  is  the  patron  of  the 
Academy.  The  Academy  includes  eighteen  members,  who  must  be 
of  Swedish  nationality.  The  officers  include  a  director,  a  chancellor, 
and  a  perpetual  secretary. 

The  Royal  Caroline  Institute  of  Medicine  and  Surgery  {KongL 
Karolinska  Medico- Kir urgiska  Instituet)  of  Stockholm  dates  from 
the  year  181 5.  The  statutes  at  present  in  force  received  the  sanction 
of  the  King  of  Sweden  on  April  29,  1886.  The  Academy  corresponds 
to  a  Faculty  of  Medicine,  and  lias  the  same  powers  as  the  Faculties 
of  Medicine  in  the  Universities  of  Upsala  and  Lund.  The  institute 
carries  on  the  theoretical  and  practical  teaching  of  the  medical 
sciences,  and  awards  diplomas  to  medical  students  by  examination. 

The  direction  and  administration  of  the  institute  are  in  the  hands 
of  a  Rector,  elected  by  the  College  of  Professors  from  among  the 
members.     The  present  number  of  professors  is  thirty-three. 

The  Norwegian  Storthing  is  the  Legislative  Assembly  of  Norway. 
It  is  elected  indirectly  by  universal  suflErage  triennially.  It  consists 
of  114  members. 


The  first  prize  awarded  for  Literature  under  the  above  conditions 
was  given,  in  1901,  to  M.  Sully  Prudhomme.  It  was  not  until 
January,  1902,  that  steps  were  taken,  on  the  invitation  of  the  Swedish. 
Academy,  for  the  due  representation  of  English  letters.  A  Com- 
mittee was  then  appointed  by  the  Incorporated  Society  of  Authors, 
and  is  composed  of  the  following  gentlemen :  Chairman  :  The  Right 
Hon.  The  Lord  Avebury,  P.C,  D.C.L.,  LL.D.,  F.R.S.  Members 
Arthur  Christopher  Benson,  M.A.,  F.R.H.S. ;  Austin  Dobson,  LL.D. 
Sir  Robert  Giffen,  K.C.B.,  F.R.S.  ;  Edmund  Gosse,  M.A.,  LL.D. 
Mrs.  John  Richard  Green  ;  The  Right  Hon.  R.  B.  Haldane,  M.P., 
LL.D.,  K.C. ;  Thomas  Hardy.  Secretary  :  G.  Herbert  Thring.  This 
Committee,  acting  in  concert  with  all  other  persons  and  bodies  in 
Great  Britain  qualified  to  vote,  awarded  the  prize  in  1902  to  Theodor 
Mommsen,  for  liis  great  work  in  connection  with  Roman  History. 
The  award  in  respect  of  the  year  1903  was  made  to  Bjomsteme 
Bjornson ;  and  in  1904  was  divided  between  Fr6d6rique  Mistral, 
the  Provencal  poet,  and  Jose  Echegaray,  the  Spanish  dramatist. 
Tlie  award  for  1905  was  made  to  Henry  Sienkiewiez.  The  award 
for  1906  went  to  Signor  Carducci,  author  of  Odi  Barbare.

f 


CORRECTED  PROOF 

to  be  altered  throughout  line  by  line,  and  a 
few  words  may  possibly  affect  several  pages. 
The  charges  made  for  corrections  are  based  on 
the  time  consumed  in  making  them,  and  are 
very  difl&cult  to  check,  even  by  an  expert. 

If  it  is  actually  a  necessity  that  a  work  must 
be  corrected  when  in  type,  and  the  amount  of 
these  alterations  is  likely  to  upset  the  arrange- 
ment of  the  lines  and  pages,  it  is  best  to  ask 
for  proofs  in  "  galley "  or  slip  form.  This 
means  a  little  more  trouble  to  the  printer,  but 
it  will  probably  cause  less  expense  in  the  long 
run. 

In  marking  corrections  for  the  printer  certain 
signs  and  s5mibols  are  used  which  express  more 
concisely  the  meaning  than  could  be  indicated 
by  a  more  ordinary  method  of  marking  the 
alterations.  We  give  on  the  opposite  page  the 
principal  characters  used  :  that  page  showing 
the  corrections  as  they  would  be  marked  by  a 
skilled  person  ;  this,  the  facing  page,  the  type 
corrected  according  to  these  directions ;  and 
the  page  overleaf  the  signs  explained  in  detail. 

In  making  a  correction  in  a  proof  always 
mark  the  wrong  letter  or  word  through,  and 
insert  the  alteration  in  the  margin,  not  in 
the  middle  of  the  printed  matter,  as  it  is  apt 
to  be  overlooked  if  there  is  no  marginal  reference 
to  the  correction.  To  keep  the  different  cor- 
rections distinct,  finish  each  one  off  with  a 
stroke,  thus  /. — From  The  Making  and  Issuing 
of  Books y  by  C.  T.  Jacobi.

SIGNS  USED  IN  CORRECTING  PROOFS 


±       To  push  down  a  **  space  '*  or  **  quadrat  '*  standing 

high. 
3      To  bring  letters   or   words    together ;   to  "  full 

out  "  an  indented  line. 
Q     To  reverse  a  letter  set  upside  down. 
□     To  "  indent  '*  a  line  one  "  em  "  of  its  own  body. 
trs.     To  transpose  letters,  words,  or  sentences 
^       To  insert  a  *'  space." 
rom.    To  change  italic  into  roman  letters. 
ital.    To  change. roman  into  italic. 
^       To   insert   **  inverted  commas/'  or  to  insert   a 
'*  superior  '*  letter  or  figure,  make  this    sign 
and  write  the  desired  insertion  above  it. 
L       To  reduce  a  *'  space.'* 
n.p.     To  commence  a  new  paragraph. 
I.e.      To  change  a  capital  or  small  capital  into  a  "  lower- 
case "  letter. 
6        To  delete  a  letter,  word,  line,  etc. 
run  on.    To  cause  matter  to  follow  on  preceding  matter 
(without  commencing  a  new  paragraph). 
^       To  rectify  a  crooked  letter,  word,  line,  etc. 
X       To  substitute  a  good  letter  for  a  battered  or 
broken  one. 
w.f.     To  substitute  for  a  wrong  fount  letter  another 

letter  from  the  correct  fount. 
stet.     To  cause   a  letter,   line,  word,  etc.,  which   has 
been  altered  by  mistake  to  stand  as  it  was  before 
alteration. 
over.     To  move  a  letter  or  word  from  a  crowded  line 
into  the  line  succeeding  it. 
0       To  insert  a  point. 

cap.     To  change  a  lower-case  letter  into  a  capital  (or 
draw  three  lines  ^  under  it). 
sm.  cap.  To  change  a  lower  or  higher  case  letter  into  a 
small  capital  (or  draw  two  lines  =  under  it).

A  FEW  OF  THE 

BIBLIOGRAPHICAL  AND  TYPOGRAPHICAL  TERMS 

IN  MORE  GENERAL  USE 

Books  are  defined  respectively  as  folio,  quarto  (4to),  octavo 
(8vo),  duodecimo  (i2mo),  sextodecimo  (i6mo),  octodecimo 
(i8mo),  vigesimo-quarto  (24mo),  trigesimo-secundo  (32mo), 
etc.  These  definitions  are  arrived  at  by  the  number  of 
times  a  sheet  is  folded  ;  for  instance,  a  folio  would  be  two 
leaves  to  the  sheet,  a  quarto  four  leaves,  and  so  on.  Some- 
times it  is  difi&cult  to  distinguish,  say,  an  octavo  from  a 
quarto  ;  but  if  it  is  printed  on  a  laid  paper,  the  water-lines 
that  run  at  intervals  through  the  sheet  (not  to  be  confused 
with  the  smaller  and  closer  wire-marks  of  the  sheet)  will 
determine  this.  The  water-lines  on  a  folio,  octavo,  and 
octodecimo  would  be  perpendicular ;  on  a  quarto,  duo- 
decimo, and  sextodecimo,  horizontal.  The  signatures  or 
letters  placed  at  the  foot  of  the  first,  and  sometimes  the 
third,  page  also  serve  as  a  guide  to  identification. 

Advance  sheets  or  copies.  Printed  in  advance  for  simul- 
taneous publication  elsewhere,  or  for  preliminary 
notices. 

Ampersand,  The  abbreviation  or  sign  for  the  word 
"  and  '*  :  thus,  &  (roman),  <§•  (italic),  Si  (black-letter). 

Antiqim.     A  German  expression  for  Roman  types. 

Bastard  title.  The  fly-  or  half-title  before  the  fiiU  title  of  the 
work. 

Black-letter.  A  general  expression  used  to  indicate  old 
English  text  or  church  type. 

Block.  A  general  term  used  as  embracing  woodcut,  electro, 
or  zinco. 

697 


bgS  THE  LITERARY  YEAR-BOOK      [part  ii. 

Body  of  the  work.    The  text  or  subject-matter  is   thus 

described    to    distinguish   it   from    the    preliminary, 

appendix,  or  notes. 
Broadside,    A  sheet  printed  on  one  side  only. 
Calendered  paper.     Paper  very  highly  rolled  or  glazed,  much 

used  for  printing  illustrated  books  or  magazines. 
Cast  off.    To  calculate  or  estimate  length  of  copy  to  be 

printed — a   troublesome   task   in   uneven    or   badly- 
written  MS. 
Clarendon.    A  bold  or  fat-faced  type   is  generally  thus 

described.     The  older  founts  were  called  "  Egyptian." 
Clerical  errors.     Mistakes  in  copying  MS. 
Clichd.     French  term  for  a  cast,  usually  applied  to  stereo 

or  electro  duplicates. 
Collate.    To  run  through  the  sheets  of  a  book  to  see  if  the 

signatures  are  in  sequence. 
Colophon,     An  inscription  or  tail-piece,  usually  a  printer*s 

imprint,  at  the  end  of  a  book. 
Cut  edges,     A  book  which  has  been  cut  on  the  three  sides 

is  said  to  have  cut  edges. 
Cut-in  notes.     Side-notes  which  are  inserted  within   the 

text  at  the  side  instead  of  in  the  margin. 
Deckle,    The  raw  rough  edge  of  handmade  paper  is  thus 

termed. 
Dummy  copy,     A  copy  generally  made  up  of  blank  leave- 
to  represent  the  actual  bulk  of  a  work  not  quite  coms 

plete. 
End  leaves  or  papers.     The  blank  fly-leaves  at  either  end 

of  a  book. 
Fly-title,     The  half-title  in  front  of  the  general  title,  or 

which  divides  sections  of  a  work. 
Folio,    A  sheet  of  paper  folded  in  two  leaves  only. 
Fore-edge,     The  outer  side  of  a  book  (distinct  from  the  head 

or  tail)  when  folded. 
Format.     The  bibliographical  term  for  size  and  shape  of  a 

book. 
Fount,    This  term  is  applied  to  the  whole  number  of  letters 

constituting  a  complete  set  of  types  of  any  particular 

cl-Bss  of  face  or  body. 
Fractur,     A  German  expression  for  their  text  or  black-letter 

characters. 


TYPOGRAPHICAL  AND  OTHER  TERMS      699 

Half 'hound.    Books  partly  bound  in  leather,  with  cloth  or 

paper  sides. 
Half 'title.    The  sub-title  in  front  of  the  full  title. 
Heads.    A  term  applied  to  the  margins  at  the  tops  of  the 

pages. 
Indent.    To  set  a  line  some  little  distance  back,  as  in  the 

case  of  a  fresh  paragraph. 
Inferior  figures  aad  letters.     Made  to  range  at  the  bottom 

of  letters,  thus  :   issacio 
Leaded  matter.    Types  with  leads  between  the  lines,  in 

contradistinction  to  '*  solid  *'  matter. 
Letterpress.     Printed  matter  from  type,  as  distinct  from 

lithographic  or  plate  printing. 
Lower-case  letters.    The  small  letters  as  used  here,  a,  b,  c. 

d,  etc. 
Moulds.     Generally  understood  as  the  preliminary  stage  in 

stereotyping  by  paper  process. 
Octavo.     A  sheet  of  paper  folded  into  eight,  shortly  written 

thus  :  8vo. 
Off-set.     The  set-off  of  ink  from  one  sheet  to  another,  the 

result  of  insufi&cient  drying  or  bad  ink. 
Preliminary.     Any  matter  coming  before  the  text  of  a 

work  :  title,  preface,  contents,  etc. 
Proof.     A  trial  print  of  any  forme  of  type,  plates,  or  blocks. 
Pulls.     A  term  generally  applied  to  proofs. 
Quarto.     A  sheet  folded  into  four  leaves  ;  shortly  :  4to. 
Quire,    Twenty-four  sheets.     Books  in  sheets,  that  is,  not 

bound,  are  said  to  be  in  quires. 
Quotes.    A  printer's  term  for  turned  commas  (*')  and  apos- 
trophes (**),  placed  respectively  at  the  beginning  and 

end  of  quoted  matter. 
Ream.    Twenty  quires.     A  printer's  ream  is  516  sheets ; 

handmade  and  drawing  papers  sometimes  472,  480,  or 

500  sheets. 
Recto.     The  right-hand  pages  of  any  work. 
Rubricated.     Printed  in  red  ink. 
Serif.     The  fine  line  on  the  top  and  bottom  of  a  letter,  as 

in  H.     Sanserif  thus  :  H. 
Set-off.     Where  the  ink  sets  off  from  one  sheet  to  another. 
Shoulder  notes.     Marginal  notes  placed  at  the  top  comer  of 

the  page. 


700  THE  LITERARY  YEAR-BOOK      [part  ii. 

Signature.    The  letter  or  figure  at  the  foot  of  the  first  page 

of  a  sheet  to  guide  the  binder  in  folding  ;  also  used  by 

printers  to  identify  any  particular  sheet. 
Size  copy.  A  thickness  or  dummy  copy  [^.v.]. 
Slips.     Applied  to  matter  not  made  up  into  pages,  but 

pulled  as  proofs  in  long  slips. 
Small  capitals     The  smaller  capitals  (as  distinct  from  the 

full  capitals),  thus  :  Standing.     Indicated  in  MS.  by 

two  strokes  (=)  underneath. 
Standing.     Type  not  distributed  after  printing  is  said  to 

be  **  standing." 
Stereotypes.     Casts  of  pages  of  types,  etc.,  in  metal,  either 

by  **  plaster  "  or  **  paper  "  process. 
Superior  letters  and  figures.     Small  letters  cast  at  the  top  of 

the  shoulder  of  type,  used  for  references  or  abbrevia- 
tions, thus  :  1  •-*  8  M*-  N**. 
Swash  letters.     Seventeenth-century  italic  capitals,   with 

tails  and  flourishes,  thus  :  «/  tM  ^,  etc. 
Tails.    The  bottoms  or  tail  ends  of  pages. 
Turned  commas.     Used  at  the  beginning  of  quoted  matter, 

thus,  *' — and  followed  at  the  end  by    .     Sometimes 

called  "  inverted  commas.'* 
Typography.    The  art  or  style  of  printing  from  movable 

letters. 
Uncut  edges.    Books  not  cut  down,  but  not  necessarily 

'*  unopened.*' 
Unopened  edges.     Applied  to  books  the  edges  of  which  have 

not  been  opened. 
Verso.    The  obverse  or  back  of  a  leaf,   the  reverse  of 

"  recto  **  [^.v.]. 
Wrong  fount.     Letters  of  a  different  character  or  series 

mixed  with  another  fount. 

[From  Some  Notes  on  Books  and  Printing,  by  C.  T.  Jacobi.] 


A  LIST  OF 
TECHNICAL  TERMS  IN  THE  VARIOUS 

PROCESSES   OF    REPRODUCTION 

*  Proeesses  or  engraved  plates  whieh  must  be  printed  separately. 

*  Aquatint.     Etching  on  copper  or  steel  through  grounds 

of  finely-powdered  resinous  substances. 
*Autogravure.     A  copper-plate  photo-etched  from  a  bichro- 
mated  gelatine  basis. 

*  Autotype.     Prints  reproduced  by  means  of  certain  bichro- 

mated  gelatine  processes  (carbon  or  collotype). 
*Chromograph.     A  reproduction  of  a  coloured  original  by 

litho,  photo-litho,  or  photo-etched  blocks. 
^Collotype,     A  photo  reproduction  printed  in  ink  from  a 

surface  of  sensitized  and  exposed  gelatine. 
Cuis.     Any  impression  from  a  type  block. 
Electrotype.     A  copper-faced  facsimile  of  a  block  or  plate 
produced  from  a  wax  mould  in  a  depositing  battery. 
^Etching.     An  impression  from  a  copper  or  steel  plate,  the 
lines  of  which  have  been  drawn  by  hand  and  etched 
with  or  without  an  acid. 
*Ferro-print.      Ferro-gallic    and    ferro-prussiate.      Prints 
made  from  salts  of  iron  sensitizers,  principally  used 
for  plans  and  copying  tracings  in  blue  or  black  lines. 
*Glyptotype.     A  print  produced  by  the   Woodbury-type 

process  transferred  to  plate  paper. 
Half -tint  blocks.     See  Tint  blocks. 
Half-tone  blocks.     See  Tint  blocks. 
*Heliotype.     A  process  of  collotype  printing. 
India  proofs.     An  impression  on  China,  Japan,  or  India 
paper,  from  an  engraved  plate. 
*Intaglio,  sunk  or  hollowed.     An  etched  plate  having  sunk 
lines  or  picture. 

701 


702  THE  LITERARY  YEAR-BOOK      [part  ii. 

*Lichtdruck.     Collotype. 
Line  process.    Usually  applied  to  any  photo  process  for 

producing  type  blocks  in  relief. 
^Lithography.      Printing  from   stone  with   ink   specially 

prepared. 
*Lithotint.    An  etching  executed  on  stone. 

*  Mezzotint  J  mixed  (as  in  Turner's  Liber  Studiorum).      The 

subject  first  etched  on  steel  or  copper  to  secure  the 
forms,  then  rocked  over  as  in  pure  mezzotint,  and  the 
subject  *'  scraped  out." 

^Mezzotint,  pure.  The  plate,  steel  or  copper,  first  roughened 
by  a  rocking- tool  till  the  surface  partakes  more  or  less 
of  the  nature  of  a  very  fine  file  ;  then  the  subject 
**  scraped  out  **  to  the  various  degrees  of  light  and 
shade  required,  and  burnished  where  very  hi^  lights 
are  wanted. 
Monotint.  Tint  printing  in  a  single  colour. 
Nickel  face.  The  coating  of  an  engraved  plate  or  block 
with  a  battery-deposited  surface  of  nickel. 

*Photocrome.     A  process  of  reproducing  photographs  in 
colours. 

*PhotO'Chromoiypy.     Photo-etched  type  blocks  for  printing 
in  colours. 

*Photogravure.    A  photo-engraved  plate  in  lines  or  tones 
for  copper-plate  printing,  or  impressions  therefrom. 

*  Photo-lithography.    The  process  of  printing  from  stone 

by  means  of  a  photographic  transfer. 
Photo-zinco.    A  photo-etched  type  block. 
Process   blocks.     Blocks   produced  by  a   photo  -  etching 

process. 
Process    work.      Work   produced   by    a    photo  -  etching 

process. 
Relief  printing,    {a)  Printing  from  type-blocks,  (b)  printing 

letters  or  designs  in  relievo. 
Remarque  proofs.   Original  impressions  from  steel  or  copper 

engraved  plates,  and  which  usually  have  a  device  on 

the  margin  instead  of  a  title. 
*Silverprints.    Usually  defines  a  chloride  of  silver  print — 

an  ordinary  photograph. 
Stereotypes.  Casts  in  stereotype  metal  (lead  and  zinc)  from 

type  or  type  blocks. 


PART  II.]  TECHNICAL  TERMS  703 

"^Three-colour  blocks.  Blocks  chemically  produced  from 
nature  or  pictures  in  colours  by  means  of  a  photo  pro- 
cess, and  which  render  all  the  colours  of  the  original. 

Tint  blocks,    Tj^e  blocks  of  wash  drawings  or  photographs 
produced  by  means  of  ruled  dots  or  tints. 

Vignettes.  Usually  applied  to  illustrations  which  are  shaded 
or  softened  off  at  the  edges. 
*Woodbury  Prints.     Prints  which  closely  resemble  ordinary 
photographs,  produced  by  a  process  invented  by  Mr. 
W.  B.  Woodbury. 

Woodcuts.    Wood  blocks  or  impressions  from  the  same. 

Xylography.    Generally  applied   to   the  printing  of  old 
block-books. 


BIBLIOGRAPHY 

OF 

GEORGE   MEREDITH 


23 


BIBLIOGRAP  H Y 

OF    THK 

WRITINGS  IN  PROSE  AND  VERSE 


GEORGE  MEREDITH.  O.M. 


COMPILED  BY 

A.  J.  K.  ESDAILE,  B.A. 


LONDON 
GEORGE    ROUTLEDGE    AND    SONS,    Limited 


NOTE 


My  plan  in  working  up  this  bibliography  may  be  expressed 
as  follows  :  Before  1879,  *^^  Y^^^  ^^  ^^^  publication  of  The 
Egoist,  which  marked  the  turn  of  the  tide,  I  have  endea- 
voured to  collect  all  published  writing  either  by  or  about 
Mr  Meredith,  except  anonymous  editorial  work.  After 
1879,  while  I  have  still  aimed  at  a  complete  Ust  of  his  own 
writings,  I  have  selected,  from  the  coimtless  criticisms  and 
appreciations  which  a  tardy  recognition  has  produced, 
only  those  which  were  written  by  well-known  men,  or 
which  seemed  to  me  intrinsically  important. 
This  bibUography  is  divided  into  three  sections  : 

I.  Mr  Meredith's  own  writings.  These  are  arranged 
chronologically,  contributions  to  periodicals,  etc.,  being 
enumerated  first,  and  volimie  pubUcations  immediately 
afterwards.  Later  editions  of  volumes  and  reviews  will 
be  found  under  the  year  of  the  first  edition  which  appeared 
in  volume  form,  except  in  the  case  of  the  short  stories  and 
the  Essay  on  Comedy,  which  were  not  produced  as  volumes 
until  nearly  twenty  years  afterwards,  and  are  placed  under 
1877,  ^t^  y^^  ^^  their  first  appearance. 

II.  A  chronological  Ust  of  articles  (not  being  reviews  of 
particular  books)  and  other  writings  of  importance,  renting 
to  Mr  Meredith. 

III.  A  synoptic  table  of  the  poems,  arranged  alphabeti- 
cally and  in  perpendicular  columns,  representing  the  dif- 
ferent editions,  so  that  all  the  places  of  appearance  of  any 
poem  may  be  seen  at  a  glance. 

Personalia  of  every  sort  have  been  rigidly  excluded. 
My  greatest  difl&cidty  has  been  the  existence  of  Mr  Lane's 
admirable  bibliography,  published  with  Mr  Richard  Le 


NOTE 

Gallienne's  George  Meredith  in  1890,  and  partly  brought  up 
to  date  in  1900.  I  soon  saw  that  my  only  hope  was  to 
work  independently,  and  then  to  compare  my  work  with 
Mr  Lane's.  This  I  have  accordingly  done,  with  the  aid 
of  the  British  Museimi  Catalogue,  of  mdexes  to  periodicals, 
etc.  The  result  is  that  I  have  collected  very  Uttle  that  Mr 
Lane  did  not  gather  before  me.  I  must  therefore  acknow- 
ledge to  the  full  my  debt  to  him.  **  What  shall  he  do  that 
cometh  after  the  King  ?"  I  have  also  to  acknowledge 
kind  help  from  Messrs  Chapman  &  Hall,  Ltd.,  the  editors 
of  The  Daily  News,  The  Daily  Chronicle,  The  Westminster 
Gazette,  The  Daily  Mail,  Mr  R.  Curie,  Mr  E.  J.  Byard, 
Baron  Tauchnitz,  and  others. 

I  should  like,  finally,  to  record  that  this  work,  much  of 
it  mechanical  and  tiling,  so  far  from  giving  me  any  weari- 
ness of  the  master,  has  greatly  deepened  and  widened  my 
admiration  of  him.  Though  I  have  played  the  valet  to 
the  hero,  he  is  my  hero  still. 

ARUNDELL  ESDAILE. 

Postscript. — I  am  infomxed  that  Mr  Meredith  contributed  some 
poems  to  Household  Words,  but  have  not  been  able  to  verify  tiiis. 
They  have  not  been  reprinted. 

A.  E.
